test_id_1.query_content: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual guidance for dual eligible pharmacy claims.

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy Claims
For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines –
Pharmacy, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Billing_Guide
lines.pdf. Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and
Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
Pharmacy/ProDUR-D.0-ECCA_Provider_Manual/Pro%20DUR%20ECCA%20Provider%20Manual%20
(D.0).pdf, “when filling prescriptions/orders for a member who is restricted to a primary provider (physician,
clinic, podiatrist, or dentist) and the primary provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the NPI of this
provider must be entered in the ordering/prescribing field.”
Additionally, “if the restricted member was referred by the primary provider to another provider and the
referred-to provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the pharmacy provider must enter the referred-to
provider’s NPI in the ordering/prescribing field. The primary provider’s NPI must be entered as the
referring/primary care provider.”
April 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Please note: The practitioner is expected to limit the NYS Medicaid claim amount to the actual invoice cost
of the drug dosage administered and maintain that documentation.
Practitioners should refer to the associated eMedNY provider manual web pages provided above, for
additional program policy and billing guidance. It is recommended that providers use electronic claims
submission whenever possible. These changes should ease practitioner burden by decreasing paper claim
submission and associated invoice documentation for claims with a DOS on or after October 1, 2024.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
However, all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are still required by
federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible
members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status.
Pharmacies missing one or more Medicare enrollments, must determine if their business situation or business model
requires CMS-855B or CMS-855S Medicare enrollment or both before applying for NY Medicaid Pharmacy enrollment.
The Attestation of Need for/Exemption from Medicare Enrollment form #409602 located here:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/ProviderMaintForms/409602_Attestation_Need_Exemption_from_Me
dicare_Enrollment.pdf enables pharmacy applicants the opportunity to clarify and attest to the need for an exemption
from each type of Medicare enrollment. Note, all community pharmacy applicants would not qualify for exemption, see
table below.
See Table below, which describes pharmacy type with corresponding expected services and Medicare-type enrollment
expected at the time of pharmacy application submission. The more stringent Medicare requirement applies to mixed

pharmacy types.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A complete list of CMS-Approved Accrediting Organizations may be found here:
www.cms.gov/files/document/accrediting-organization-contacts-prospective-clients.pdf
*The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to
ensure that Medicaid dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in
accordance with federal and State laws, and regulations. The three types of Medicare enrollment include CMS-460
Medicare Participating Physician or Supplier Agreement, CMS-855B Medicare enrollment for clinics/group practices
and other suppliers, and CMS-855S Medicare enrollment for durable medical equipment, prosthetics, orthotics, and
supplies (DMEPOS) suppliers. Pharmacies that choose to enroll in the CMS-460 Medicare Participating Physician or
Supplier Agreement, agree to accept assignment for all their Medicare claims, including those not subject to Medicaid
payment. This Medicare enrollment is optional.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
2.4 Pharmacy Services Billing Instructions
This subsection of the Billing Guidelines covers the specific NYS Medicaid billing requirements for Pharmacies. Although
the instructions that follow are based on the eMedNY-000301 paper claim form, they are also intended as a guideline
for electronic billers to find out what information they need to provide in their claims. For further electronic claim
submission information, refer to the NCPDP D.0 Request and Response Companion Guide which is available at
www.emedny.org by clicking: NCPDP D.0 Companion Guide.
It is important that providers adhere to the instructions outlined below. Claims that do not conform to the eMedNY
requirements as described throughout this document may be rejected, pended, or denied.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, provider manual guidance is more up to date overall for dual eligible pharmacy claims and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2024-12-01 is older and is consistent in part, but the provider manual adds more specific current billing/enrollment requirements for dual eligible pharmacy claims. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,21,22 — 2025-10-01] [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — 2024-12-01]
answers.policy_definition: Policy guidance states that for dual eligible members, Medicaid is not the primary payer for certain pharmacy-related services: "Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid." [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01] The newest policy source also directs providers to the eMedNY pharmacy billing materials for pharmacy claims, stating: "For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines – Pharmacy" and "Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — 2023-04-01]. Earlier policy guidance said: "Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will continue to access immunization services through Medicare." [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance says Medicaid-enrolled pharmacy providers must submit claims and accept Medicare assignments for Medicare-covered services for Medicaid dual eligible members: "all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are still required by federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21,22 — 2025-10-01] It also states that NYS Medicaid requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid so dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from properly enrolled providers, and that "The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient" and "The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,21 — 2025-10-01]
similarities: Both sources treat Medicare as the first source for dual eligible pharmacy claims/services before Medicaid pays: "Medicaid is the payer of last resort" and "potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01]; "required by federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21,22 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources indicate dual eligible pharmacy billing is constrained by Medicare/Medicaid coordination rather than ordinary Medicaid-only billing: policy says "Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members" [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01], while the manual says dual eligible members must be served through Medicare-covered billing and enrollment requirements [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,21,22 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The provider manual is more specific and current about pharmacy provider obligations, requiring pharmacies to "submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services" for dual eligible members and to have Medicare enrollment as needed [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21,22 — 2025-10-01], while the policy excerpts mainly say Medicare must be billed first and direct readers to billing manuals [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — 2023-04-01].

The older policy excerpt is narrower and service-specific, stating that "Dual eligible members will continue to access immunization services through Medicare" [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01], whereas the provider manual addresses broader pharmacy claim/payment rules for dual eligible members, including payment limits and Medicare enrollment [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,21,22 — 2025-10-01].
answers.caveats: The policy excerpts provided do not include a 2024 policy statement specifically addressing dual eligible pharmacy claims; the newest policy material here is a 2024 billing-reference notice that points to provider manuals rather than restating the rule [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — 2024-12-01].

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for dual eligible pharmacy claims and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2024-12-01 is older and points providers to the eMedNY pharmacy billing guidance rather than stating the dual-eligible pharmacy claim rule itself. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,21,22,23 — 2025-10-01] [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — 2024-12-01]
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy excerpt does not define the dual-eligible pharmacy claim rule directly; it says, "Practitioners should refer to the associated eMedNY provider manual web pages provided above, for additional program policy and billing guidance." [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — 2024-12-01] Older policy guidance states, "Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will continue to access immunization services through Medicare." [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance states that "all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are still required by federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21,22 — 2025-10-01] It also states that "The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient" and that "If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]
similarities: Both sources tie dual-eligible pharmacy billing to Medicare rather than Medicaid-only billing: the policy says, "Dual eligible members will continue to access immunization services through Medicare" [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01], and the provider manual says pharmacies must "submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21,22 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources indicate Medicaid does not stand alone as the payer for these dual-eligible pharmacy situations: the policy says, "Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members" [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01], while the provider manual says Medicare/Medicaid payment rules apply and "The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The policy excerpt is narrower and older, stating only that pharmacies may bill Medicaid for "non-dual eligible members" and that dual eligible members access immunization services through Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01], while the provider manual is broader and newer, requiring claims submission and Medicare assignment for "Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21,22 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual adds payment-limit and enrollment detail not found in the policy excerpt, including that total Medicare/Medicaid payment "will not exceed" the Medicaid-only amount and that pharmacies may need Medicare enrollment for dual-eligible billing [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,21,22,23 — 2025-10-01]. The policy excerpt does not provide those operational limits or enrollment requirements [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01].
answers.caveats: The newest policy excerpt available does not directly define dual-eligible pharmacy claims; it only directs readers to the provider manual for additional billing guidance. The provider manual is newer and should guide current handling of dual-eligible pharmacy claims unless the user needs historical policy context.

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Through
coordination of benefits, Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid coverable
claims or will pay up to the Medicaid allowed amounts for drugs in classes specifically excluded from being
covered under the third-party liability (TPL) Plan, including Medicare.
As previously communicated in the December 2020 issue of the Medicaid Update, in the article titled
Submission Guidance for Fee-for-Service Claims with Third Party Liability (Medicare or Other Insurance),
available at https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2020/docs/mu_no18_dec20
_pr.pdf, a claim submitted to NYS Medicaid with a zero-fill reimbursement from any TPL Plan must be
submitted. A pharmacy provider who receives a zero-fill response must ensure that the claim was adjudicated
within each TPL Plan claim process requirement. This includes submitting a claim under the appropriate
coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy, such as with the Healthcare Common Procedure Coding System
(HCPCS) or by National Drug Code (NDC).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

www.cms.gov/files/document/accrediting-organization-contacts-prospective-clients.pdf
*The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to
ensure that Medicaid dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in
accordance with federal and State laws, and regulations. The three types of Medicare enrollment include CMS-460
Medicare Participating Physician or Supplier Agreement, CMS-855B Medicare enrollment for clinics/group practices
and other suppliers, and CMS-855S Medicare enrollment for durable medical equipment, prosthetics, orthotics, and
supplies (DMEPOS) suppliers. Pharmacies that choose to enroll in the CMS-460 Medicare Participating Physician or
Supplier Agreement, agree to accept assignment for all their Medicare claims, including those not subject to Medicaid
payment. This Medicare enrollment is optional.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
1. Purpose Statement
The purpose of this document is to assist the provider community in understanding and complying with the New York
State Medicaid (NYS Medicaid) requirements and expectations for Billing and submitting claims.
This document is customized for Pharmacies and should be used by the provider as an instructional, as well as a
reference tool.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, provider manual guidance is more up to date overall for dual eligible pharmacy claims and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2024-12-01 is older and is more general, while the provider manual gives the current pharmacy-specific Medicare/Medicaid coordination rules for dual eligible members. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — Dec 01, 2024]
answers.policy_definition: Policy guidance in the provided Medicaid Update excerpts is limited and does not fully define dual eligible pharmacy claims. The older 2021 update says, "Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will continue to access immunization services through Medicare." [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021] The newer 2024 update does not define dual eligible pharmacy claims directly; it only says practitioners should "refer to the associated eMedNY provider manual web pages provided above, for additional program policy and billing guidance." [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — Dec 01, 2024]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The provider manual states that for dual eligible members, Medicaid-enrolled pharmacy providers must submit Medicare claims and accept assignments for Medicare-covered services: "all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are still required by federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — Oct 01, 2025] It also states that "The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient" and that if Medicare pays more than the Medicaid fee, "no additional payment will be made." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources treat dual eligible pharmacy billing as involving Medicare rather than Medicaid-only billing: the policy says "Dual eligible members will continue to access immunization services through Medicare" [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021], and the provider manual says providers must "submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources indicate Medicaid payment is limited or secondary for dual eligible pharmacy claims: the policy says pharmacies "will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members" [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021], and the provider manual says total Medicare/Medicaid payment "will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The policy excerpt is narrower and older, focusing on immunization services and stating that pharmacies "will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members" [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021], while the provider manual gives broader current pharmacy billing rules for Medicare-covered services for dual eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — Oct 01, 2025].

The provider manual adds a payment rule not stated in the policy excerpts: "If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made" and Medicare/Medicaid payment "must be accepted as payment in full" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025].
answers.caveats: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents beyond the limited 2021 immunization statement and the 2024 referral to provider manuals. The provider manual is the more recent and more specific source for current dual eligible pharmacy claim guidance. [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — Dec 01, 2024] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — Oct 01, 2025]

test_id_4.query_content: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual guidance for dual eligible pharmacy claims.

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy Claims
For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines –
Pharmacy, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Billing_Guide
lines.pdf. Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and
Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
Pharmacy/ProDUR-D.0-ECCA_Provider_Manual/Pro%20DUR%20ECCA%20Provider%20Manual%20
(D.0).pdf, “when filling prescriptions/orders for a member who is restricted to a primary provider (physician,
clinic, podiatrist, or dentist) and the primary provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the NPI of this
provider must be entered in the ordering/prescribing field.”
Additionally, “if the restricted member was referred by the primary provider to another provider and the
referred-to provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the pharmacy provider must enter the referred-to
provider’s NPI in the ordering/prescribing field. The primary provider’s NPI must be entered as the
referring/primary care provider.”
April 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Please note: The practitioner is expected to limit the NYS Medicaid claim amount to the actual invoice cost
of the drug dosage administered and maintain that documentation.
Practitioners should refer to the associated eMedNY provider manual web pages provided above, for
additional program policy and billing guidance. It is recommended that providers use electronic claims
submission whenever possible. These changes should ease practitioner burden by decreasing paper claim
submission and associated invoice documentation for claims with a DOS on or after October 1, 2024.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
However, all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are still required by
federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible
members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status.
Pharmacies missing one or more Medicare enrollments, must determine if their business situation or business model
requires CMS-855B or CMS-855S Medicare enrollment or both before applying for NY Medicaid Pharmacy enrollment.
The Attestation of Need for/Exemption from Medicare Enrollment form #409602 located here:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/ProviderMaintForms/409602_Attestation_Need_Exemption_from_Me
dicare_Enrollment.pdf enables pharmacy applicants the opportunity to clarify and attest to the need for an exemption
from each type of Medicare enrollment. Note, all community pharmacy applicants would not qualify for exemption, see
table below.
See Table below, which describes pharmacy type with corresponding expected services and Medicare-type enrollment
expected at the time of pharmacy application submission. The more stringent Medicare requirement applies to mixed

pharmacy types.

*The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to
ensure that Medicaid dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in

Medicare Participating Physician or Supplier Agreement, CMS-855B Medicare enrollment for clinics/group practices
and other suppliers, and CMS-855S Medicare enrollment for durable medical equipment, prosthetics, orthotics, and

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, provider manual guidance is more up to date overall for dual eligible pharmacy claims and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2024-12-01 is older and is more general, while the provider manual gives the current billing/payment rule for dual eligible pharmacy claims.
answers.policy_definition: Policy guidance is limited in the provided excerpts: the newest policy source says, "Please note: The practitioner is expected to limit the NYS Medicaid claim amount to the actual invoice cost of the drug dosage administered and maintain that documentation. Practitioners should refer to the associated eMedNY provider manual web pages provided above, for additional program policy and billing guidance." [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — 2024-12-01]. Earlier policy guidance states, "Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid." [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance states, "The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made. Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. It also states, "all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are still required by federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — 2025-10-01], and that NYS Medicaid requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies to ensure dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from enrolled providers [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources treat Medicare as relevant before Medicaid for dual eligible pharmacy claims: policy says "potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01], and the provider manual says pharmacies must "submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources indicate Medicaid payment is limited rather than open-ended: policy says the practitioner must "limit the NYS Medicaid claim amount to the actual invoice cost" [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — 2024-12-01], and the provider manual says combined Medicare/Medicaid payment "will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The provider manual is more specific and current on dual eligible pharmacy claim payment methodology, stating that if Medicare payment exceeds the Medicaid fee, "no additional payment will be made" and that Medicare/Medicaid payment "must be accepted as payment in full" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]; the policy excerpts do not provide this same payment-cap rule.

The policy excerpts are more general and direct users to the provider manual for billing guidance: "Practitioners should refer to the associated eMedNY provider manual web pages provided above, for additional program policy and billing guidance" [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — 2024-12-01].
answers.caveats: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents beyond the cited general billing and payer-of-last-resort statements. The provider manual is the more recent source for this issue and should guide current dual eligible pharmacy claim handling unless a historical policy question is being asked.

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Through
coordination of benefits, Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid coverable
claims or will pay up to the Medicaid allowed amounts for drugs in classes specifically excluded from being
covered under the third-party liability (TPL) Plan, including Medicare.
As previously communicated in the December 2020 issue of the Medicaid Update, in the article titled
Submission Guidance for Fee-for-Service Claims with Third Party Liability (Medicare or Other Insurance),
available at https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2020/docs/mu_no18_dec20
_pr.pdf, a claim submitted to NYS Medicaid with a zero-fill reimbursement from any TPL Plan must be
submitted. A pharmacy provider who receives a zero-fill response must ensure that the claim was adjudicated
within each TPL Plan claim process requirement. This includes submitting a claim under the appropriate
coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy, such as with the Healthcare Common Procedure Coding System
(HCPCS) or by National Drug Code (NDC).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, provider manual guidance is more up to date overall for dual eligible pharmacy claims and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2023-04-01 is older and mainly points providers to other billing manuals, while earlier policy guidance says dual eligible members are billed through Medicare rather than Medicaid for the referenced service. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,21,22 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — Apr 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021]
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy excerpt does not itself define a dual eligible pharmacy-claim billing rule; it says, "For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines – Pharmacy" and also to the ProDUR/ECCA manual, so the policy source is cross-referential rather than self-defining here. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — Apr 01, 2023] Earlier policy guidance states, "Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will continue to access immunization services through Medicare." [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021] It also states, "Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid." [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance states that "all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are still required by federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21,22 — Oct 01, 2025] It also states that "The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient" and that "The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] The manual further says NYS Medicaid "requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to ensure that Medicaid dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in accordance with federal and State laws, and regulations." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources treat Medicare as primary or otherwise required before Medicaid for dual eligible pharmacy claims: policy says "potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid" and the manual says providers are "required by federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members". [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21,22 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources limit Medicaid payment so it does not exceed the Medicaid-only amount or otherwise act as secondary payment: policy says Medicaid pays only after other reimbursement is exhausted, and the manual says "The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient." [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]
differences: The policy excerpts are narrower and partly indirect: the 2023 policy update says providers should "refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines – Pharmacy" and the 2021 update says "Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members" for the referenced immunization context. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — Apr 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021]

The provider manual is more explicit and broader for current dual eligible pharmacy claims, stating that Medicaid-enrolled pharmacy providers must "submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status," and that NYS Medicaid "requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid". [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21,22 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21 — Oct 01, 2025]
answers.caveats: The policy excerpts provided do not contain a complete current billing rule for dual eligible pharmacy claims; the newest policy source is cross-referential and the older policy language is service-specific to immunization services. The provider manual is the more recent and more detailed source for current guidance on this issue. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — Apr 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21,22 — Oct 01, 2025]

within each TPL Plan claim process requirement. This includes submitting a claim under the appropriate
coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy, such as with the Healthcare Common Procedure Coding System
(HCPCS) or by National Drug Code (NDC).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy Claims
For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines –
Pharmacy, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Billing_Guide
lines.pdf. Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and
Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
Pharmacy/ProDUR-D.0-ECCA_Provider_Manual/Pro%20DUR%20ECCA%20Provider%20Manual%20
(D.0).pdf, “when filling prescriptions/orders for a member who is restricted to a primary provider (physician,
clinic, podiatrist, or dentist) and the primary provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the NPI of this
provider must be entered in the ordering/prescribing field.”
Additionally, “if the restricted member was referred by the primary provider to another provider and the
referred-to provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the pharmacy provider must enter the referred-to
provider’s NPI in the ordering/prescribing field. The primary provider’s NPI must be entered as the
referring/primary care provider.”
April 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
with Other Payer ID and Other Payer ID Qualifier for Pharmacy
Claims
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payor of last resort and federal regulations
require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A primary physician or primary clinic is responsible for providing all medical care to the restricted recipient, either
directly or through referral of such recipient to another medical provider for appropriate services. If the primary
provider refers the Medicaid restricted recipient to another provider for services, the primary provider’s Medicaid
identification number must be used in the referring field to bill for those services. When dispensing medications
prescribed by the ‘referred’ provider, the pharmacy must enter the primary provider’s Medicaid identification number
in the referring field in the pharmacy claim.
Medicaid members may have durable medical equipment restrictions separate from pharmacy restrictions.
Utilization Threshold
The Utilization Threshold Program (UT) is a post payment review of services and procedures provided to members that
evaluate medical necessity while maintaining fiscal responsibility to the Medicaid Program.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, provider manual guidance is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. Both sources state that Medicaid is payer of last resort and that a responsible third party must pay first for dual-eligible pharmacy claims, but the provider manual adds more specific Medicare/Medicaid payment limits and payment-in-full language. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]; [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025]
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy source says NYS Medicaid providers must bill liable third parties before Medicaid and that "NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort"; it also states that "Federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment." [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025] Earlier policy guidance similarly says that "Through coordination of benefits, Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claims or will pay up to the Medicaid allowed amounts" for certain drugs excluded under the TPL plan, including Medicare. [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:16 — Nov 01, 2021]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance states that "Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment" and that "If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025] It also adds that "The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient" and that "The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources require other coverage to be billed before Medicaid: "providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025]; "a provider [must] pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources frame Medicaid as secondary/payer of last resort: "NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025]; "Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The provider manual is more specific about dual-eligible Medicare pharmacy claims, stating that "The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient" and that "The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy update does not include that payment-cap language in the provided excerpt. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025]

The policy excerpt includes a specific COB payment rule for excluded drug classes, stating Medicaid "will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claims or will pay up to the Medicaid allowed amounts for drugs in classes specifically excluded from being covered under the third-party liability (TPL) Plan, including Medicare" [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:16 — Nov 01, 2021], while the provider manual excerpt provided here does not state that excluded-drug payment rule. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]
answers.caveats: The provided provider manual excerpts are more recent overall, but the exact dual-eligible pharmacy claim rule is only partially visible in the excerpts; the policy excerpt also references older guidance and an external billing guide without the underlying text. Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for any source beyond the quoted scope.

provider_manual.text:
Workers’ Compensation and Coordination of Benefits
If a member has a work-related injury, for which they have an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’
compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.
If the drug claim is NOT related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an
unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field
309-C9. Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on the
claim, if applicable.
The Workers’ Compensation Board can be accessed here: https://www.wcb.ny.gov/content/main/hcpp/hcpp.jsp.

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, provider manual guidance is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. Both sources say Medicaid is secondary to other responsible coverage and that a responsible third party must pay first, but the provider manual gives the clearest current pharmacy-specific rule for dual-eligible claims. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy source says NYS Medicaid providers must bill liable third parties before Medicaid and that “NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort” because “all other available resources [must] be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment.” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] Earlier policy guidance also states that “Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claims” through coordination of benefits, but only after other coverage rules are applied. [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:16 — Nov 01, 2021]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance states that “all available resources [must] be used before Medicaid considers payment” and that “If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025] For pharmacy claims involving Medicare/Medicaid, it further states that “The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient” and that “The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources require other liable coverage to be billed before Medicaid pays: “providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] and “If there is a responsible third party who should be paying ... as primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources place Medicaid in a secondary/payer-of-last-resort role: “NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] and “all available resources [must] be used before Medicaid considers payment” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The provider manual is newer and more specific to pharmacy claims, stating the “responsible third party” must pay first and giving Medicare/Medicaid payment limits for the pharmacy provider [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16,18 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy update states the broader rule that Medicaid is the “payer of last resort” and that providers must bill liable third parties first [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025].

The policy excerpts include an older statement that Medicaid may pay “the patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claims” [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:16 — Nov 01, 2021], but the newer provider manual excerpt does not use that phrasing and instead focuses on primary-payor responsibility and payment limits [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16,18 — Oct 01, 2025].
answers.caveats: The provided policy excerpts do not include a full dual-eligible pharmacy-claim definition, and the April 2025 policy chunk points users to the provider manual for pharmacy claims rather than restating the detailed rule [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]. The provider manual is the more recent and more detailed source for current pharmacy guidance on this issue [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16,18 — Oct 01, 2025].

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, provider manual guidance is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. Both sources say Medicaid is payer of last resort and that a responsible third party must pay first for dual-eligible pharmacy claims, but the provider manual adds more specific payment-limit language.
answers.policy_definition: Policy documents state that NYS Medicaid providers must bill liable third parties before Medicaid because "NYS Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid" and "NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]. Earlier policy guidance also says that through coordination of benefits, "Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claims" or certain excluded drug classes, but only in the TPL context [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:16 — Nov 01, 2021].
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance says that "Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment" and that "If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]. The billing guidelines also place responsibility on the provider to determine other coverage: "It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18-19 — Jul 23, 2025].
similarities: Both sources require other available coverage to be used before Medicaid pays: "NYS Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid" and "NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]; "Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources place first-payment responsibility on the third party when one is responsible: "If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]; "NYS Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025].
differences: The provider manual is more specific about the primary payer rule, stating that "If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy update states the broader rule that providers must bill liable third parties before Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025].

The provider manual adds provider-level operational responsibility: "It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18-19 — Jul 23, 2025], while the policy excerpts provided do not include that same operational instruction [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025].
answers.caveats: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents beyond the general payer-of-last-resort and third-party-first rules; the excerpts do not provide a dual-eligible-specific pharmacy claim definition beyond those coordination-of-benefits principles [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025].

test_id_9.query_content: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance define primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims?
test_id_9.concept: primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims

policy.text:
Through
coordination of benefits, Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid coverable
claims or will pay up to the Medicaid allowed amounts for drugs in classes specifically excluded from being
covered under the third-party liability (TPL) Plan, including Medicare.
As previously communicated in the December 2020 issue of the Medicaid Update, in the article titled
Submission Guidance for Fee-for-Service Claims with Third Party Liability (Medicare or Other Insurance),
available at https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2020/docs/mu_no18_dec20
_pr.pdf, a claim submitted to NYS Medicaid with a zero-fill reimbursement from any TPL Plan must be
submitted. A pharmacy provider who receives a zero-fill response must ensure that the claim was adjudicated
within each TPL Plan claim process requirement. This includes submitting a claim under the appropriate
coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy, such as with the Healthcare Common Procedure Coding System
(HCPCS) or by National Drug Code (NDC).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy Claims
For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines –
Pharmacy, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Billing_Guide
lines.pdf. Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and
Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
Pharmacy/ProDUR-D.0-ECCA_Provider_Manual/Pro%20DUR%20ECCA%20Provider%20Manual%20
(D.0).pdf, “when filling prescriptions/orders for a member who is restricted to a primary provider (physician,
clinic, podiatrist, or dentist) and the primary provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the NPI of this
provider must be entered in the ordering/prescribing field.”
Additionally, “if the restricted member was referred by the primary provider to another provider and the
referred-to provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the pharmacy provider must enter the referred-to
provider’s NPI in the ordering/prescribing field. The primary provider’s NPI must be entered as the
referring/primary care provider.”
April 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
with Other Payer ID and Other Payer ID Qualifier for Pharmacy
Claims
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payor of last resort and federal regulations
require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A primary physician or primary clinic is responsible for providing all medical care to the restricted recipient, either
directly or through referral of such recipient to another medical provider for appropriate services. If the primary
provider refers the Medicaid restricted recipient to another provider for services, the primary provider’s Medicaid
identification number must be used in the referring field to bill for those services. When dispensing medications
prescribed by the ‘referred’ provider, the pharmacy must enter the primary provider’s Medicaid identification number
in the referring field in the pharmacy claim.
Medicaid members may have durable medical equipment restrictions separate from pharmacy restrictions.
Utilization Threshold
The Utilization Threshold Program (UT) is a post payment review of services and procedures provided to members that
evaluate medical necessity while maintaining fiscal responsibility to the Medicaid Program.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.
