answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, provider manual guidance is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. Both sources say Medicaid is secondary to other liable coverage and that a responsible third party must pay first, but the provider manual adds more specific pharmacy billing/payment limits for Medicare/Medicaid claims. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:42 — Oct 1, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 1, 2025]
answers.policy_definition: Policy guidance says NYS Medicaid providers must bill liable third parties before Medicaid, because "NYS Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid" and "NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 1, 2025]. Earlier policy also states that "Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claims or will pay up to the Medicaid allowed amounts for drugs in classes specifically excluded from being covered under the third-party liability (TPL) Plan, including Medicare" [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:16 — Nov 1, 2021].
answers.provider_manual_definition: Provider manual guidance states that "all available resources [must] be used before Medicaid considers payment" and that "If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 1, 2025]. For Medicare/Medicaid claims, it further says "The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient" and "If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 1, 2025].
similarities: Both sources require other liable coverage to pay before Medicaid: "NYS Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid" and "NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 1, 2025].

Both sources state that a responsible primary payer must pay first: "If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 1, 2025].
differences: The provider manual is more specific about pharmacy claim payment limits for Medicare/Medicaid claims: "The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient" and "If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 1, 2025].

Policy guidance includes an explicit patient-responsibility/payment rule for certain claims: "Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claims or will pay up to the Medicaid allowed amounts for drugs in classes specifically excluded from being covered under the third-party liability (TPL) Plan, including Medicare" [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:16 — Nov 1, 2021].
answers.caveats: The provided policy excerpts do not contain a single, fully detailed definition of "primary payer responsibility" for dual-eligible pharmacy claims; they state the payer-of-last-resort rule and some payment consequences, but the most current, detailed guidance in the provided record is the provider manual dated 2025-10-01. The policy excerpts also reference other manuals and prior articles, but only the quoted chunks above are grounded here.

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. Both sources say Medicaid is secondary to other liable coverage for dual-eligible pharmacy claims, but the provider manual gives the clearest current rule that a responsible third party must pay first and Medicaid will not exceed the Medicaid-only amount. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]; [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy guidance says NYS Medicaid providers must bill liable third parties before Medicaid and that Medicaid is the payer of last resort: "NYS Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment." [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] Earlier policy also states that through COB, Medicaid pays patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid-coverable claims or up to Medicaid allowed amounts for drugs excluded from the TPL plan, including Medicare. "Through coordination of benefits, Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claims or will pay up to the Medicaid allowed amounts for drugs in classes specifically excluded from being covered under the third-party liability (TPL) Plan, including Medicare." [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:16 — Nov 01, 2021]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance states that all available resources must be used before Medicaid pays and that a responsible third party must pay first if it is the primary payor: "Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025] It also limits combined Medicare/Medicaid payment to no more than the Medicaid-only amount: "The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources require other liable coverage to be billed before Medicaid: "NYS Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort." [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]; "Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment... a provider [must] pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources place Medicaid payment behind primary third-party responsibility and cap Medicaid-related payment at Medicaid levels: "NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]; "If there is a responsible third party who should be paying... as primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The provider manual is more explicit about primary payer responsibility, stating: "If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025] The policy update uses broader payer-of-last-resort language and does not define primary payor as directly. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]

The policy update includes a pharmacy-claim-specific COB payment rule for certain drugs excluded from TPL/Medicare: "Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claims or will pay up to the Medicaid allowed amounts for drugs in classes specifically excluded from being covered under the third-party liability (TPL) Plan, including Medicare." [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:16 — Nov 01, 2021] The provider manual excerpt provided does not include that same excluded-drug payment language. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16,18 — Oct 01, 2025]
answers.caveats: The provider manual is the newer source overall for this issue, so it should guide current interpretation by default. The policy excerpts provided do not include a newer, equally specific pharmacy-claim definition of primary payer responsibility beyond payer-of-last-resort language, and the 2021 policy excerpt is older background only.

test_id_11.query_content: What are the differences between Medicaid policy and provider manual guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members?

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
October 2019 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 5

Policy and Billing
Reminder to all Providers on Billing Requirements for
Medicare Part C Claims That are Submitted to Medicaid for
Payment of Patient Responsibility
Reminder: All providers must accurately report Medicare Part C cost-sharing amounts on Medicaid
claims. Some providers may be inflating the patient responsibility amount for Part C Medicare Advantage claims,
resulting in overpayment by the Medicaid program and therefore may be leaving the providers subject to
investigation by the Office of the Medicaid Inspector General (OMIG).
A provider of a Medicare Part C benefit cannot seek to recover any copayment, or coinsurance amount
from Medicare/Medicaid dually eligible individuals. The provider is required to accept the Medicare Part C
health plan payment and any Medicaid payment as payment in full for the service. The member may not be billed
for any Medicare Part C copayment/coinsurance amount that was not reimbursed by Medicaid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2019 Volume 35 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Additional information can be found in the Ambulatory
Patient Groups (APGs) Medicaid Fee-for-Service Provider Manual – Policy and Billing Guidelines, located
at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/rates/manual/docs/apg_provider_manual_december
.pdf.
July 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers cannot offer gifts or cash per SSA § 1128A (a)(5), Title 18 NYCRR § 515.2, and Rules of the Board
of Regents §29.1. It is not permissible for providers to:
o Providers cannot make offers where it is likely to influence the enrollee’s selection of a provider of
Medicaid items or services. Note: NYRx allows for the provision of an item or device to a NYRx
member or enrollee which is directly related to the administration of medication for that person
(e.g., medicine cup, dosing spoons, or pill organizer).
o Providers cannot proactively waive copays. Copays may be waived only as provided on a case-by-
case basis per the financial need by request of the individual member or enrollee.
Medicaid Co-payments:
• Some Medicaid members become eligible for Medicaid by spending part of their monthly income on
medical care.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Refusing to
provide services is an unacceptable practice.
o Providers may attempt to collect outstanding copayments through methods such as requesting the
copayment each time the member is provided with services or goods, sending bills or any other
legal means.
• Pharmacy providers must not reduce the amount charged on a Medicaid claim by the copayment that is
collected from a Medicaid member. Each claim that requires a co-payment will have the co-payment
automatically deducted from the final payment when the claim is approved for payment.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2023-07-01 is older and gives only general dual-eligible billing direction, while the provider manual gives more specific current co-payment collection guidance.
answers.policy_definition: Policy documents provide only general dual-eligible billing guidance: Medicaid is “the payer of last resort,” so “potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid.” [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023] The provided policy chunks do not define co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members beyond this general billing rule; “Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents.”
answers.provider_manual_definition: The provider manual states that for dual-eligible/Medicare-related claims, “The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full,” and “a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance amounts from Me...” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] It also says providers “may attempt to collect outstanding copayments through methods such as requesting the copayment each time the member is provided with services or goods, sending bills or any other legal means,” but “Pharmacy providers must not reduce the amount charged on a Medicaid claim by the copayment that is collected from a Medicaid member.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:40 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources treat Medicare as primary to Medicaid for dual-eligible billing: policy says Medicaid is “the payer of last resort” and “Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid.” [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023] The provider manual likewise limits Medicaid payment after Medicare-related payment rules and says the combined payment “must be accepted as payment in full.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources support that Medicaid does not pay first for dual-eligible claims: policy says “Medicaid will make payments only after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted,” and the provider manual says “The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full.” [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]
differences: The policy source does not specifically address who is responsible for co-payments for dual-eligible members; it only gives general payer-of-last-resort billing direction. “Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents.” [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023]

The provider manual is more specific on collection mechanics and limits: it allows providers to “attempt to collect outstanding copayments” by requesting payment, sending bills, or other legal means, but prohibits reducing the Medicaid claim amount by the copayment collected. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:40 — Oct 01, 2025]
answers.caveats: The provider manual excerpt is the newest and should guide current practice, but the relevant sentence on Medicare Part B deductible/coinsurance is truncated in the provided chunk (“from Me...”), so that portion is cited only to the extent shown. The policy excerpts provided do not contain a direct co-payment rule for dual-eligible members, so the policy-side comparison is limited to general billing hierarchy.

test_id_12.query_content: What are the differences between Medicaid policy and provider manual guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members?

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, provider manual guidance is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. The policy source is newer than the older policy chunks but is still dated 2023-07-01, and it states Medicaid is the payer of last resort for dual eligibles, while the provider manual adds specific co-payment collection and claim-adjustment rules. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2023]
answers.policy_definition: Policy guidance says that for dual eligible enrollees, "Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted" and that "potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid." [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023] Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided policy documents to define co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members beyond that payer-of-last-resort billing rule. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2023]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance says that "the Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full" and that "a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance amounts from Me..." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] It also says "Providers may attempt to collect outstanding copayments through methods such as requesting the copayment each time the member is provided with services or goods, sending bills or any other legal means" and that "Pharmacy providers must not reduce the amount charged on a Medicaid claim by the copayment that is collected from a Medicaid member." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:40 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources place Medicare ahead of Medicaid for dual-eligible billing/payment coordination: policy says "Medicaid is the payer of last resort" and "potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023]; the provider manual says "the Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources address dual-eligible cost-sharing as something tied to Medicare/Medicaid payment rules rather than a separate Medicaid-only payment obligation: policy frames dual eligibles under third-party reimbursement before Medicaid [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023], and the provider manual addresses deductible/coinsurance and copayment collection/claim handling [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 40 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The policy documents provided do not specifically define who is responsible for co-payments for dual-eligible members; they only state the general rule that Medicaid pays last after other reimbursement sources are exhausted. [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2023]

The provider manual gives more specific operational guidance on cost-sharing recovery, stating that providers "may attempt to collect outstanding copayments" and that they "must not reduce the amount charged on a Medicaid claim by the copayment that is collected from a Medicaid member." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:40 — Oct 01, 2025]
answers.caveats: The provided policy excerpts do not contain a direct co-payment rule for dual-eligible members, so the policy side is limited to payer-of-last-resort billing guidance. The provider manual excerpt is the more recent source and should guide current practice, but one sentence is truncated in the provided chunk ("from Me...") so the exact full scope of the deductible/coinsurance prohibition cannot be fully confirmed from the excerpt alone. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]

after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:

guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.

policy.text:
• It is the responsibility of the provider to submit the claim using the appropriate claim format with their
usual or customary fee indicated along with reporting the Medicare Advantage Plan deductible
amount, co-insurance amount, co-payment amount and paid amount.
Please note: A provider of a Medicare Part C benefit cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-
insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals. The provider is
required to accept the Medicare Part C health plan payment and any NYS Medicaid payment as payment in
full for the service. The NYS Medicaid member may not be billed for any Medicare Part C co-payment/co-
insurance amount that is not reimbursed by NYS Medicaid.

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.

orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.

provider_manual.text:
A complete list of CMS-Approved Accrediting Organizations may be found here:
www.cms.gov/files/document/accrediting-organization-contacts-prospective-clients.pdf
*The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to
ensure that Medicaid dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in
accordance with federal and State laws, and regulations. The three types of Medicare enrollment include CMS-460
Medicare Participating Physician or Supplier Agreement, CMS-855B Medicare enrollment for clinics/group practices
and other suppliers, and CMS-855S Medicare enrollment for durable medical equipment, prosthetics, orthotics, and
supplies (DMEPOS) suppliers. Pharmacies that choose to enroll in the CMS-460 Medicare Participating Physician or
Supplier Agreement, agree to accept assignment for all their Medicare claims, including those not subject to Medicaid
payment. This Medicare enrollment is optional.

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. The policy source dated 2025-07-01 says providers cannot attempt to recover Medicare Advantage co-payment or co-insurance directly from dual-eligible individuals, while the provider manual adds that Medicare and Medicaid payment, if any, must be accepted as payment in full and that Medicare Part B deductible/coinsurance cannot be sought from the member. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2-3 — Jul 01, 2025]
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy evidence says: “It is the responsibility of the provider to submit the claim using the appropriate claim format with their usual or customary fee indicated along with reporting the Medicare Advantage Plan deductible amount, co-insurance amount, co-payment amount and paid amount.” It also states: “A provider of a Medicare Part C benefit cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals.” [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024] The broader policy framework also says Medicaid is “the payer of last resort” and that “potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid.” [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual evidence says: “The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made. Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full.” It further states: “Per state regulation, a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance amounts from Me...” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] The manual also says Medicaid is not appropriate for most Medicare Part D claims and that Medicaid only pays for certain excluded drugs for dual-eligible members. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources prohibit providers from collecting Medicare-related cost-sharing directly from dual-eligible members: the policy says a provider “cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals,” and the manual says a provider “cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance amounts from” the member. [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources treat Medicare as primary before Medicaid for dual-eligible billing: policy says “Medicaid is the payer of last resort” and “Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid,” while the manual limits Medicaid payment to situations where Medicare payment does not exceed the Medicaid fee. [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]
differences: The policy source is narrower and specifically addresses Medicare Advantage/Part C claim reporting and recovery, stating providers must report the “deductible amount, co-insurance amount, co-payment amount and paid amount” and cannot recover co-payment or co-insurance directly from dual-eligible individuals. [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024] The provider manual is broader and adds that Medicare/Medicaid payment, if any, “must be accepted as payment in full” and that Medicare Part B deductible/coinsurance cannot be sought from the member. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]

The provider manual is the more recent and controlling guidance for this issue, dated 2025-10-01, while the newest policy excerpt is dated 2025-07-01; the policy excerpt provided does not include the same “payment in full” language or the Part B deductible prohibition. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2-3 — Jul 01, 2025]
answers.caveats: The provider manual excerpt is truncated at “from Me...,” so the exact end of the Part B deductible/coinsurance sentence is not fully visible in the provided chunk. Also, the newest policy excerpt provided (July 2025) mentions duals being subject to Medicare rules but does not itself restate co-payment responsibility in the visible text, so the comparison relies on the May 2024 policy excerpt for the specific co-payment language. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2-3 — Jul 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:3 — May 01, 2024]

policy.text:
October 2019 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 5

Policy and Billing
Reminder to all Providers on Billing Requirements for
Medicare Part C Claims That are Submitted to Medicaid for
Payment of Patient Responsibility
Reminder: All providers must accurately report Medicare Part C cost-sharing amounts on Medicaid
claims. Some providers may be inflating the patient responsibility amount for Part C Medicare Advantage claims,
resulting in overpayment by the Medicaid program and therefore may be leaving the providers subject to
investigation by the Office of the Medicaid Inspector General (OMIG).
A provider of a Medicare Part C benefit cannot seek to recover any copayment, or coinsurance amount
from Medicare/Medicaid dually eligible individuals. The provider is required to accept the Medicare Part C
health plan payment and any Medicaid payment as payment in full for the service. The member may not be billed
for any Medicare Part C copayment/coinsurance amount that was not reimbursed by Medicaid.

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, provider manual guidance is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2023-07-01 is older and is more general, while the provider manual gives more specific current instructions on copayment handling for dual-eligible members.
answers.policy_definition: Policy guidance states that for dual eligible enrollees, Medicaid is the payer of last resort and other reimbursement sources, including Medicare, must be billed first: "Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid." [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023]. The provided policy chunks do not include a specific rule on whether a provider may seek copayment from a dual-eligible member; they only address billing order and related Medicare/Medicaid billing guidance. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2023]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The provider manual is more specific and current: it says the Medicare and Medicaid payment, if any, "must be accepted as payment in full" and that "a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance amounts from Me..." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]. It also states that providers may try to collect outstanding copayments, but "Pharmacy providers must not reduce the amount charged on a Medicaid claim by the copayment that is collected from a Medicaid member" and that "Each claim that requires a co-payment will have the co-payment automatically deducted from the final pa..." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:40 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources treat Medicare as primary to Medicaid for dual-eligible billing, with Medicaid paying only after other coverage is used: "Medicaid is the payer of last resort... potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid." [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023] and "All claims for dual-eligible members su..." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources are concerned with how payment responsibility is allocated for dual-eligible members and how providers should bill or collect amounts related to Medicare/Medicaid claims. [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 40 — Oct 01, 2025]
differences: The policy excerpt is general and focuses on billing order and payer-of-last-resort rules, while the provider manual gives a more specific current rule that Medicare and Medicaid payment "must be accepted as payment in full" and that providers cannot seek certain Medicare deductible/coinsurance amounts from the member. [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]

The provider manual includes explicit copayment-collection instructions for pharmacy providers—"Providers may attempt to collect outstanding copayments" but "must not reduce the amount charged on a Medicaid claim by the copayment that is collected"—whereas the policy chunks provided do not state a comparable copayment rule for dual-eligible members. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:40 — Oct 01, 2025] Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents.
answers.caveats: The policy excerpts provided do not contain a direct copayment-responsibility rule for dual-eligible members, so the comparison is limited to billing-order guidance and general payer-of-last-resort language. The provider manual excerpt is more recent and more specific, so it should guide current practice unless the user is asking about historical policy. [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 40 — Oct 01, 2025]

policy.text:
August 2019 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 6

Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require
Administration by a Practitioner
New York State (NYS) Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs requiring administration by a practitioner
to be available to members by way of both the Medical Benefit and Pharmacy Benefit. Such practitioner-
administered drugs are listed on the Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs
(https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx) and may be billed directly to the Medicaid Fee-for-Service (FFS)
program by a pharmacy. The intent of this article is to provide guidance for proper dispensing and delivery of
such drugs. Nothing in this policy is meant to suggest that all practitioner-administered drugs must be
dispensed as a Pharmacy Benefit. The policy regarding practitioner-administered drug billing is addressed in
the Physician Manual found at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Physician/index.aspx.
Practitioner-administered drugs dispensed as a Pharmacy Benefit must be delivered by the pharmacy directly to
the site of administration.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
December 2022 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 10

Pharmacy
Pharmacy Dispensing Drugs that Require Practitioner
Administration
The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program recognizes the need for drugs that must be administered by a
practitioner to be billable to NYS Medicaid members through both the Medical Benefit and Pharmacy Benefit.
Such practitioner administered drugs (PADs) are listed on the NYS Department of Health (DOH) List of
Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs document, located on the eMedNY “Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable
Drugs” web page at: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx, and may be billed directly to NYRx, formerly
known as the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy program, by a pharmacy.
This guidance provides information for the proper dispensing and delivery of such drugs and is not
meant to suggest that all PADs must be dispensed as a Pharmacy Benefit. The policy regarding
pharmacy billing of drugs that require practitioner-administration can be found in the New York State Medicaid
Fee-for-Service Program Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines, located at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf, under the “Delivery” section.

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
January 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Providers should refer to the NYRx the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy
Guidelines, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Policy_Guide
lines.pdf, for further guidance on origin code and serial number values that must be submitted on the claim
for “pharmacy dispensing” when applicable for non-patient specific orders. Enter a value of “5” in field 419-
DJ (Prescription Origin Code) and a value of “99999999” in field 454- EK (Scheduled Prescription ID
Number). Additionally, providers should refer to the NYS Department of Health (DOH) Office of Health
Insurance Programs (OHIP) Standard Companion Guide – Transaction Information, located at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
Reminder for Pharmacy and Medical Claims
The National Provider Identifier (NPI) of the ordering provider is required on the NYS Medicaid claim.
Additionally, providers must not bill for the provision of the RSV vaccine to NYS Medicaid members who are
also enrolled in Medicare.

provider_manual.text:
Drugs will be evaluated on a case-by-case basis to determine if they are appropriate to add to the
pharmacy formulary.
Certain practitioner-administered drugs are listed on the Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs and may be
billed directly to NYRx by a pharmacy. Nothing in this policy is meant to suggest that practitioner-administered drugs
must be dispensed as a Pharmacy benefit. The policy regarding practitioner-administered drug billing is addressed in
the Physician’s Manual found here: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Physician/index.aspx.
Practitioner-administered drugs dispensed as a pharmacy benefit must be delivered by the pharmacy directly to the
site of administration. This is considered “white bagging” and is acceptable under the following guidelines:
• Drugs should only be dispensed by the pharmacy directly to the patient when they are to be self-
administered. The policy surrounding self-administered drug delivery is found in section titled Delivery
above.

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.

policy.text:
August 2019 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 6

Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require
Administration by a Practitioner
New York State (NYS) Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs requiring administration by a practitioner
to be available to members by way of both the Medical Benefit and Pharmacy Benefit. Such practitioner-
administered drugs are listed on the Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs
(https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx) and may be billed directly to the Medicaid Fee-for-Service (FFS)
program by a pharmacy. The intent of this article is to provide guidance for proper dispensing and delivery of
such drugs. Nothing in this policy is meant to suggest that all practitioner-administered drugs must be
dispensed as a Pharmacy Benefit. The policy regarding practitioner-administered drug billing is addressed in
the Physician Manual found at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Physician/index.aspx.
Practitioner-administered drugs dispensed as a Pharmacy Benefit must be delivered by the pharmacy directly to
the site of administration.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Information
regarding Medicaid fee-for-service covered “Practitioner Administered Drugs” can be found in the
Provider Manuals at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/index.aspx within the “Procedure
Code and Fee Schedule” sections for Drugs, and in “Provider Communications”.
• For coverage information regarding Practitioner Administered Drugs for managed care enrollees,
please refer to the managed care plan's medical benefit of policies and procedures.
• Questions should be directed to the eMedNY Call Center at (800) 343-9000.

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
January 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Providers should refer to the NYRx the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy
Guidelines, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Policy_Guide
lines.pdf, for further guidance on origin code and serial number values that must be submitted on the claim
for “pharmacy dispensing” when applicable for non-patient specific orders. Enter a value of “5” in field 419-
DJ (Prescription Origin Code) and a value of “99999999” in field 454- EK (Scheduled Prescription ID
Number). Additionally, providers should refer to the NYS Department of Health (DOH) Office of Health
Insurance Programs (OHIP) Standard Companion Guide – Transaction Information, located at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
Reminder for Pharmacy and Medical Claims
The National Provider Identifier (NPI) of the ordering provider is required on the NYS Medicaid claim.
Additionally, providers must not bill for the provision of the RSV vaccine to NYS Medicaid members who are
also enrolled in Medicare.

Pharmacy Dispensing Drugs that Require Practitioner
Administration
The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program recognizes the need for drugs that must be administered by a
practitioner to be billable to NYS Medicaid members through both the Medical Benefit and Pharmacy Benefit.
Such practitioner administered drugs (PADs) are listed on the NYS Department of Health (DOH) List of
Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs document, located on the eMedNY “Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable
Drugs” web page at: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx, and may be billed directly to NYRx, formerly
known as the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy program, by a pharmacy.
This guidance provides information for the proper dispensing and delivery of such drugs and is not
meant to suggest that all PADs must be dispensed as a Pharmacy Benefit. The policy regarding
pharmacy billing of drugs that require practitioner-administration can be found in the New York State Medicaid
Fee-for-Service Program Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines, located at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf, under the “Delivery” section.

policy.text:
August 2019 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 6

Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require
Administration by a Practitioner
New York State (NYS) Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs requiring administration by a practitioner
to be available to members by way of both the Medical Benefit and Pharmacy Benefit. Such practitioner-
administered drugs are listed on the Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs
(https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx) and may be billed directly to the Medicaid Fee-for-Service (FFS)
program by a pharmacy. The intent of this article is to provide guidance for proper dispensing and delivery of
such drugs. Nothing in this policy is meant to suggest that all practitioner-administered drugs must be
dispensed as a Pharmacy Benefit. The policy regarding practitioner-administered drug billing is addressed in
the Physician Manual found at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Physician/index.aspx.
Practitioner-administered drugs dispensed as a Pharmacy Benefit must be delivered by the pharmacy directly to
the site of administration.

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.

pharmacy formulary.
Certain practitioner-administered drugs are listed on the Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs and may be

Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, provider guidance is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. The provider manual says Medicaid may pay only for Medicare Part D-excluded drugs that are on the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs, while the newest policy source dated 2023-03-01 is more general and does not specifically address Part D-excluded drugs. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — Oct 01, 2025] [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4:13-14 — Mar 01, 2023]
answers.policy_definition: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents. The newest policy excerpt only states that the “Provider Manuals” tab has “billing and policy related guidance” and links to manuals, but it does not define handling of Medicare Part D-excluded drugs: “The ‘Provider Manuals’ tab has billing and policy related guidance (e.g., billing guidelines, procedure codes, fee schedules, etc.)” [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4:13-14 — Mar 01, 2023].
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual says: “It is not appropriate to bill Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.” It then limits Medicaid payment to “drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being covered under the Part D plans, such as: • Select prescription vitamins. and • Certain non-prescription drugs.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources are part of NYS Medicaid pharmacy guidance and point users to billing/policy guidance rather than creating a separate benefit rule: “The ‘Provider Manuals’ tab has billing and policy related guidance” [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4:13-14 — Mar 01, 2023]; “It is not appropriate to bill Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources indicate that pharmacy coverage/billing is tied to Medicaid program guidance and reimbursable-drug lists: “Provider Manuals” include “billing guidelines, procedure codes, fee schedules, etc.” [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4:13-14 — Mar 01, 2023]; Medicaid pays only for drugs “from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs” when the Part D exclusion applies [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — Oct 01, 2025]
differences: The provider manual gives a specific rule for Medicare Part D-excluded drugs, while the policy excerpt does not: “Medicaid only pays for drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being covered under the Part D plans” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — Oct 01, 2025]; the policy excerpt only says provider manuals contain “billing and policy related guidance” and does not define Part D-excluded drug handling [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4:13-14 — Mar 01, 2023].

The provider manual identifies examples of Part D-excluded drugs—“Select prescription vitamins” and “Certain non-prescription drugs” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18-19 — Oct 01, 2025]—but no comparable examples appear in the policy excerpt [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4:13-14 — Mar 01, 2023].
answers.caveats: The policy excerpts provided do not contain a direct definition of Medicare Part D-excluded drug handling, so the comparison is one-sided on that point. The provider manual is newer (2025-10-01) than the newest policy excerpt (2023-03-01), so it should guide current handling unless a historical question is intended.

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, provider guidance is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. The provider manual says Medicaid may pay only for Medicare Part D-excluded drugs on the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list, while the newest policy excerpt only addresses a copayment override and does not define Part D-excluded drug coverage. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,19 — 2025-10-01] [New York State Medicaid Update February 2025 Volume 41 Number 2:12 — 2025-02-01]
answers.policy_definition: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents. The newest policy excerpt only states: "Pharmacies may use the following override to remove the copayment for these scenarios: In the National Council for Prescription Drugs Program field 461-EU, enter a value "04" (Exempt Copay and/or Coinsurance)." [New York State Medicaid Update February 2025 Volume 41 Number 2:12 — 2025-02-01]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual says: "It is not appropriate to bill Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below." It further states that "Medicaid only pays for drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being covered under the Part D plans, such as: • Select prescription vitamins. and • Certain non-prescription drugs." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,19 — 2025-10-01]
similarities: Both sources address Medicaid pharmacy billing/payment rules rather than general clinical coverage concepts: the policy excerpt discusses a billing override for copayment/coinsurance, and the provider manual discusses when Medicaid may be billed for a Medicare Part D member. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2025 Volume 41 Number 2:12 — 2025-02-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,19 — 2025-10-01]

Both sources are limited to specific billing scenarios and do not provide a broad standalone definition of all Medicare Part D-excluded drugs in the policy excerpt. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2025 Volume 41 Number 2:12 — 2025-02-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,19 — 2025-10-01]
differences: The provider manual expressly defines the handling of Medicare Part D-excluded drugs: "Medicaid only pays for drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being covered under the Part D plans." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,19 — 2025-10-01] The policy excerpt does not define Part D-excluded drug coverage. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2025 Volume 41 Number 2:12 — 2025-02-01]

The policy excerpt instead addresses a copayment override: "Pharmacies may use the following override to remove the copayment... enter a value "04" (Exempt Copay and/or Coinsurance)." [New York State Medicaid Update February 2025 Volume 41 Number 2:12 — 2025-02-01] That is different from the provider manual’s coverage/billing rule for Part D-excluded drugs. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,19 — 2025-10-01]

The policy excerpt may still be relevant for copayment override mechanics, but it does not answer the Part D-excluded drug coverage question directly. policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.

provider_manual.text:
Medicare/Medicaid Reimbursement
Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program as a billing provider are required to demonstrate participation in the
Medicare Program. Medicaid pharmacy enrollment information can be accessed online at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/index.aspx.
Analogous to TPL in the section above, Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid. Services
covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare (See below for Parts A, B, D).
It is the pharmacy’s responsibility:
• To bill the appropriate Medicare benefit.
• To notify both the member and the prescriber of any needed authorizations or changes the Medicare Plan
requires.
Pharmacy providers can bill Medicaid only after payment information is received from Medicare. Pharmacy providers
may not circumvent Medicare billing procedures for a Medicare covered member and must bill by the Healthcare
Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code (NDC) as the Medicare Plan requires.

provider_manual.text:
Workers’ Compensation and Coordination of Benefits
If a member has a work-related injury, for which they have an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’
compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.
If the drug claim is NOT related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an
unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field
309-C9. Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on the
claim, if applicable.
The Workers’ Compensation Board can be accessed here: https://www.wcb.ny.gov/content/main/hcpp/hcpp.jsp.

The newest policy source dated 2025-04-01 is broader and says third parties liable for a claim must be billed before NYS Medicaid; because the provider manual is more recent, it should receive more weight for current pharmacy billing guidance. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — Apr 01, 2025]
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy guidance says NYS Medicaid providers must bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid, because “NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort” and “all other available resources” must be used before Medicaid pays. The policy excerpt does not specifically single out Medicare in the quoted text, so it is a general third-party-before-Medicaid rule rather than a Medicare-specific pharmacy billing instruction. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — Apr 01, 2025]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance is Medicare-specific: “Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program as a billing provider are required to demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program,” and “Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid.” It further states that “Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources require billing other available coverage before Medicaid pays: the policy says providers must bill “applicable third parties” before NYS Medicaid, and the manual says “Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid.” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources reflect Medicaid as secondary/payer of last resort: the policy says “NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort,” and the manual requires Medicare to be maximized before Medicaid. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]
differences: The policy is broader and does not specifically mention Medicare in the quoted text; it applies to “applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim” before Medicaid. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — Apr 01, 2025]

The provider manual is more specific and directly addresses Medicare, stating that pharmacies must demonstrate Medicare participation and that “Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]

The provider manual is newer (2025-10-01) than the policy source (2025-04-01), so it should guide current pharmacy billing practice unless you are asking about the earlier policy language. policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.

provider_manual.text:
Medicare/Medicaid Reimbursement
Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program as a billing provider are required to demonstrate participation in the
Medicare Program. Medicaid pharmacy enrollment information can be accessed online at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/index.aspx.
Analogous to TPL in the section above, Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid. Services
covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare (See below for Parts A, B, D).
It is the pharmacy’s responsibility:
• To bill the appropriate Medicare benefit.
• To notify both the member and the prescriber of any needed authorizations or changes the Medicare Plan
requires.
Pharmacy providers can bill Medicaid only after payment information is received from Medicare. Pharmacy providers
may not circumvent Medicare billing procedures for a Medicare covered member and must bill by the Healthcare
Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code (NDC) as the Medicare Plan requires.
