provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Workers’ Compensation and Coordination of Benefits
If a member has a work-related injury, for which they have an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’
compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.
If the drug claim is NOT related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an
unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field
309-C9. Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on the
claim, if applicable.
The Workers’ Compensation Board can be accessed here: https://www.wcb.ny.gov/content/main/hcpp/hcpp.jsp.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the pharmacy rule is that "Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid" and "Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]. The newest policy source dated 2025-04-01 is consistent that NYS Medicaid is the payer of last resort and providers must bill liable third parties before billing Medicaid, but the provider manual is more recent and should receive more weight by default for current pharmacy billing guidance [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — Apr 01, 2025].
answers.policy_definition: The policy source says NYS Medicaid providers "are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid" and that "NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort"; it also states that "Federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — Apr 01, 2025]. Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for the policy source beyond that general third-party-liability rule [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — Apr 01, 2025].
answers.provider_manual_definition: The provider manual is more specific for pharmacy billing: "Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program as a billing provider are required to demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program" and "Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid"; it further states that "Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]. It also says the combined Medicare/Medicaid payment cannot exceed the Medicaid-only amount and that the Medicare and Medicaid payment, if any, "must be accepted as payment in full" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025].
similarities: Both sources require Medicaid to be billed only after other applicable coverage is used first: the policy says providers must bill "applicable third parties" before Medicaid, and the manual says "Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — Apr 01, 2025; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources reflect Medicaid as secondary/payer of last resort: the policy says "NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort," and the manual requires Medicare to be billed first when a service is covered by both programs [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — Apr 01, 2025; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The policy source states a broad coordination-of-benefits rule for "applicable third parties" and "all other available resources," without naming Medicare specifically in the provided excerpt [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — Apr 01, 2025].

The provider manual is pharmacy-specific and expressly names Medicare, stating that "Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare" and that pharmacies enrolled as billing providers must "demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].
answers.caveats: The provided policy excerpt is general COB guidance, not a pharmacy-specific Medicare billing rule, so it does not fully define the Medicare-before-Medicaid requirement on its own [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — Apr 01, 2025]. The provider manual is newer and more specific for current pharmacy billing practice, so it should guide the user now unless the question is about historical policy [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5

policy.text:
2

Policy and Billing
Reminder: Medicaid Requires Coordination of Benefits
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid, as Medicaid is always the payor of last resort and federal regulations
require that all other available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. The November
2021 issue of the Medicaid Update article titled Reminder: NYS Requires Coordination of Benefits, located
at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2021/no13_2021-11.htm#cob, directed
providers to exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the Medicaid program. If a Medicaid member has third-
party insurance coverage, the benefits of that coverage must be fully used before billing the NYS Medicaid
program. Providers should always ask a Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure
the proper coordination of benefits.
All claims submitted for members with Medicare and/or other third-party insurance must accurately reflect
payments and denials received from other insurers to allow correct calculation of Medicaid reimbursement
amounts.

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.

Medicare/Medicaid Reimbursement
Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program as a billing provider are required to demonstrate participation in the
Medicare Program. Medicaid pharmacy enrollment information can be accessed online at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/index.aspx.
Analogous to TPL in the section above, Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid. Services
covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare (See below for Parts A, B, D).
It is the pharmacy’s responsibility:
• To bill the appropriate Medicare benefit.
• To notify both the member and the prescriber of any needed authorizations or changes the Medicare Plan
requires.
Pharmacy providers can bill Medicaid only after payment information is received from Medicare. Pharmacy providers
may not circumvent Medicare billing procedures for a Medicare covered member and must bill by the Healthcare
Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code (NDC) as the Medicare Plan requires.

Workers’ Compensation and Coordination of Benefits
If a member has a work-related injury, for which they have an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’
compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.
If the drug claim is NOT related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an
unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field
309-C9. Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on the
claim, if applicable.

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2025-04-01 and the provider manual both require billing other liable coverage before Medicaid, but the provider manual specifically says Medicare benefits must be maximized before billing Medicaid and that services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — Apr 01, 2025]
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy source says NYS Medicaid providers must bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid, because NYS Medicaid is the payer of last resort and all other available resources must be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — Apr 01, 2025] The older policy reminder also says pharmacies must bill timely and to the appropriate party, including Medicare or Medicaid, but it does not state a Medicare-before-Medicaid rule as specifically as the provider manual. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual says pharmacies enrolled as Medicaid billing providers are required to demonstrate participation in Medicare, and that Medicare benefits must be maximized prior to billing Medicaid. It further states that services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025] The manual also states that the total Medicare/Medicaid payment will not exceed the Medicaid-only amount and that Medicare and Medicaid payment, if any, must be accepted as payment in full. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources require billing other available coverage before Medicaid: the policy says providers must bill "applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid" and that Medicaid is "the payer of last resort" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — Apr 01, 2025]; the provider manual says "Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources place Medicaid after other payers in the billing sequence, with the policy requiring use of "all other available resources" before Medicaid payment and the provider manual requiring Medicare to be billed first when a service is covered by both programs. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]
differences: The provider manual is more specific about Medicare: it says "Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare," which directly answers the Medicare-before-Medicaid question. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]

The policy source is broader and does not expressly single out Medicare; it says only that providers must bill "applicable third parties" before Medicaid and that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — Apr 01, 2025]
answers.caveats: The policy excerpts provided do not contain a Medicare-specific billing rule as explicit as the provider manual, so the provider manual should guide current pharmacy billing practice on this point. The older 2020 reminder mentions billing Medicare or Medicaid to the appropriate party, but it is background only and does not override the newer 2025 provider manual guidance. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020]

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.

Policy and Billing
Reminder: Medicaid Requires Coordination of Benefits
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid, as Medicaid is always the payor of last resort and federal regulations
require that all other available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. The November
2021 issue of the Medicaid Update article titled Reminder: NYS Requires Coordination of Benefits, located
at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2021/no13_2021-11.htm#cob, directed
providers to exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the Medicaid program. If a Medicaid member has third-
party insurance coverage, the benefits of that coverage must be fully used before billing the NYS Medicaid
program. Providers should always ask a Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure
the proper coordination of benefits.
All claims submitted for members with Medicare and/or other third-party insurance must accurately reflect
payments and denials received from other insurers to allow correct calculation of Medicaid reimbursement
amounts.

Both sources require Medicaid to be billed after other liable coverage, but the provider manual specifically says Medicare must be maximized first for services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — Apr 01, 2025]
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy source says NYS Medicaid providers must bill liable third parties before billing Medicaid: "NYS Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid" and "NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort." [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — Apr 01, 2025] Earlier policy updates say the same general rule: "providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid" and Medicaid is "the payor of last resort." [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:4 — May 01, 2024]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual source is more specific for Medicare: "Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program as a billing provider are required to demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program" and "Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid. Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025] The manual also states that "The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient" and that "The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources require other liable coverage to be used before Medicaid: "bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — Apr 01, 2025] and "Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources treat Medicaid as secondary/payer of last resort: "NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — Apr 01, 2025] and the manual requires Medicare to be billed first for dual-covered services [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].

The provider manual is more specific to pharmacy/Medicare claims and expressly says: "Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].

policy.text:
Notification to Providers of Requirement to Attach Explanation of
Benefits from Third-Party Payors to Medicaid Claims
To ensure that New York State (NYS) Medicaid is the payor of last resort when a Medicaid recipient is eligible
for Medicare or has other third-party insurance benefits, providers must first bill Medicare or the other third-
party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid. In the coming months, providers will be
required to attach an Explanation of Benefits (EOB) for fee-for-service (FFS) claims when submitting a claim
to Medicaid for services rendered to a recipient who is also eligible for Medicare or has other third-party
coverage. If an EOB is not submitted with the claim, the claim will be denied. The Office of the Medicaid
Inspector General (OMIG) will be providing additional information regarding the timeline and requirements for
implementation of this initiative.
Please Note: Pharmacy claims, Medicare crossover claims, and non-covered procedure codes identified by
NYS Medicaid are not subject to this requirement.
Questions regarding this notice should be directed to OMIG’s Bureau of Third Party and Payment Oversight
at ThirdP@omig.ny.gov.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior
authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary insurer does
not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will
result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
When a member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs (Part B coverage information can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers), or
• Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C)
for other prescription drugs (Part D coverage information can be found at: https://www.medicare
gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
When a member has Medicare Parts A or B or both and is not enrolled in Medicare Part D, providers will bill
Part D covered claims to the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET) program per: https://www.
cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
New York State Medicaid is always the payor of last resort; every effort must be taken to
obtain correct billing information.
If TPL is applicable, providers must the third-party insurer first, including commercial plans or Medicare,
and bill Medicaid as secondary, if appropriate. Any claim issues must be resolved with the third-party insurer,
including prior authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary
insurer does not cover a specific medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the
third-party insurer will result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
If the member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs. Information on Part B covered drugs can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers, or
• Medicare Part D for other prescription drugs. Information on Part D covered drugs can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
If the member has Medicare Parts A or B (or both) and does not have Medicare Part D, providers will bill:
• Part D covered claims to Medicare Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET).

policy.text:
12

Medicaid fee-for-service (FFS) will require prior authorization (PA) for most medically necessary therapy visits,
unless the member receiving the service meets one or more of the criteria listed below:
• Children from birth to age 21 (until their 21st birthday)
• Members with developmental disabilities (members with restriction/exception code "95" on file)
• Members with a traumatic brain injury (TBI) [members with TBI* (use procedure code modifier ST on
claims) or restriction/exception code "81" on file]
• Members with both Medicare Part B and Medicaid (dually eligible) when the service is covered by
Medicare
• Members receiving rehabilitation services as a hospital inpatient
• Members receiving rehabilitation services in a nursing home in which they reside
• Members receiving rehabilitation services provided by a certified home health agency (CHHA)
*TBI as defined in Public Health Law Article 27-cc: §2741.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2021 Volume 37 Number 5
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Members must meet certain conditions to
receive these benefits.

Medicare Part B
Medicare Part B covers doctors’ services, outpatient care and some other medical services that Part A does not cover.
Information regarding Medicare Part B outpatient covered drugs can be found here:
https://www.medicare.gov/coverage/prescription-drugs-outpatient.
Medicare Part B covers certain drugs such as:
• Drugs used with an item of durable medical equipment.
• Some antigens,
• Injectable osteoporosis drugs,
• Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents,
• Blood clotting factors,
• Injectable and infused drugs,
• Oral End-Stage Renal Disease (ESRD) drugs,
• Parenteral and enteral nutrition (intravenous and tube feeding)
• Intravenous Immune Globulin (IVIG) provided in the home,
• Vaccinations,
• Immunosuppressive drugs following a Medicare paid transplant,
• Oral cancer drugs,
• Oral anti-nausea drugs,
• Self-administered drugs in hospital outpatient setting.
For Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims, even for a procedure that would have required Medicaid prior approval, prior
approval is not required if Medicare approved and paid for a service and/or procedure.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted.

provider_manual.text:
Prior Authorization
• Per the September 2015 NYS Medicaid Drug Utilization Review (DUR) Board recommendation and the
November 2018 Medicaid Update article titled, Update on Medicaid Fee-for-Service Prior Authorization of
Topical Compounded Drug Products, Prior Authorization (PA) and editing on prescriptions for all topical
compounded drug products is required and will be implemented on certain NYRX Medicaid Fee-for-Service
(FFS) claims. This process will ensure that compounded topical drug products meet State and Federal

regulations and that the compound ingredients are Food and Drug Administration (FDA)-approved or
compendia-supported for topical use when submitted to NYRx.

provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Prior Authorization Programs
The Medicaid program requires prior authorization for certain drugs under the following programs:
• Preferred Drug Program (PDP)
• Brand When Less Than Generic Program (BLTG)
• Dose Optimization Initiative
• Clinical Drug Review Program (CDRP)
• Drug Utilization Review (DUR) Program
• Mandatory Generic Drug Program (MGDP)
Prescribers may need to obtain prior authorizations for certain drugs. Only prescribers or their authorized agent may
submit a request for a prior authorization. Third party requests are prohibited: prescribers may not contract with or
assign authority to dispensing pharmacists, manufacturers, or other persons or companies to initiate their PA requests.
General information on the prescription drug prior authorizations, including the above programs, can be found at the
following website: https://newyork.fhsc.com.
Note: If a prior authorization number has not been obtained by the prescriber and the pharmacist is unable to reach
the prescriber, the pharmacist may obtain a prior authorization for up to a 72-hour emergency supply for any drug
that requires prior authorization, subject to state laws and Medicaid restrictions.

Both sources treat Medicaid as secondary/payor of last resort: "To ensure that New York State (NYS) Medicaid is the payor of last resort" [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — 2020-10-01] and "all available resources [must] be used before Medicaid considers payment" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The policy excerpts specifically say third-party claim issues, including prior authorization requirements, must be resolved before submitting the claim to Medicaid: "Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid." [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01] The provider manual excerpt does not specifically mention crossover claims or resolving third-party prior authorization issues before Medicaid submission in the same way. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]

The provider manual adds a more explicit regulatory statement that "Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibilit..." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01], while the policy excerpts do not include that statement. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01]
answers.caveats: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents as a specific crossover-claims prior-approval-after-Medicaid-payment rule. The provider manual excerpt is truncated at "responsibilit...", so the exact full sentence about prior authorization cannot be fully quoted from the provided text.

policy.text:
Notification to Providers of Requirement to Attach Explanation of
Benefits from Third-Party Payors to Medicaid Claims
To ensure that New York State (NYS) Medicaid is the payor of last resort when a Medicaid recipient is eligible
for Medicare or has other third-party insurance benefits, providers must first bill Medicare or the other third-
party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid. In the coming months, providers will be
required to attach an Explanation of Benefits (EOB) for fee-for-service (FFS) claims when submitting a claim
to Medicaid for services rendered to a recipient who is also eligible for Medicare or has other third-party
coverage. If an EOB is not submitted with the claim, the claim will be denied. The Office of the Medicaid
Inspector General (OMIG) will be providing additional information regarding the timeline and requirements for
implementation of this initiative.
Please Note: Pharmacy claims, Medicare crossover claims, and non-covered procedure codes identified by
NYS Medicaid are not subject to this requirement.
Questions regarding this notice should be directed to OMIG’s Bureau of Third Party and Payment Oversight
at ThirdP@omig.ny.gov.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15

policy.text:
Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior
authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary insurer does
not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will
result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
When a member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs (Part B coverage information can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers), or
• Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C)
for other prescription drugs (Part D coverage information can be found at: https://www.medicare
gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
When a member has Medicare Parts A or B or both and is not enrolled in Medicare Part D, providers will bill
Part D covered claims to the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET) program per: https://www.
cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.

policy.text:
July 2020 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 8

Updated Pharmacy Guidance for Long Term Care Providers and
Prior Authorization Requirements
Effective June 25, 2020, system changes have been made to allow for providers to initiate a bypass from
prior authorization (PA) when:
 a member is a resident of a Long Term Care (LTC) facility which is either a Private Skilled Nursing
Facility, Public Skilled Nursing Facility, Private Health Related Facility, or Public Health Related
Facility (identified as “NH” on an eligibility response), and
 a member first obtains Medicaid eligibility after 90 days from the prescription date of service/fill date
for claims not included in the rate. Please see in this Medicaid Update, article titled Pharmacy Fee-
For-Service Billing Guidance: Long Term Care and Foster Care (Child Care) Facilities for the full
guidance for LTC facilities.

provider_manual.text:
Members must meet certain conditions to
receive these benefits.

Medicare Part B
Medicare Part B covers doctors’ services, outpatient care and some other medical services that Part A does not cover.
Information regarding Medicare Part B outpatient covered drugs can be found here:
https://www.medicare.gov/coverage/prescription-drugs-outpatient.
Medicare Part B covers certain drugs such as:
• Drugs used with an item of durable medical equipment.
• Some antigens,
• Injectable osteoporosis drugs,
• Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents,
• Blood clotting factors,
• Injectable and infused drugs,
• Oral End-Stage Renal Disease (ESRD) drugs,
• Parenteral and enteral nutrition (intravenous and tube feeding)
• Intravenous Immune Globulin (IVIG) provided in the home,
• Vaccinations,
• Immunosuppressive drugs following a Medicare paid transplant,
• Oral cancer drugs,
• Oral anti-nausea drugs,
• Self-administered drugs in hospital outpatient setting.
For Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims, even for a procedure that would have required Medicaid prior approval, prior
approval is not required if Medicare approved and paid for a service and/or procedure.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted.

compounded drug products is required and will be implemented on certain NYRX Medicaid Fee-for-Service
(FFS) claims. This process will ensure that compounded topical drug products meet State and Federal

regulations and that the compound ingredients are Food and Drug Administration (FDA)-approved or
compendia-supported for topical use when submitted to NYRx.

provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Prior Authorization Programs
The Medicaid program requires prior authorization for certain drugs under the following programs:
• Preferred Drug Program (PDP)
• Brand When Less Than Generic Program (BLTG)
• Dose Optimization Initiative
• Clinical Drug Review Program (CDRP)
• Drug Utilization Review (DUR) Program
• Mandatory Generic Drug Program (MGDP)
Prescribers may need to obtain prior authorizations for certain drugs. Only prescribers or their authorized agent may
submit a request for a prior authorization. Third party requests are prohibited: prescribers may not contract with or
assign authority to dispensing pharmacists, manufacturers, or other persons or companies to initiate their PA requests.
General information on the prescription drug prior authorizations, including the above programs, can be found at the
following website: https://newyork.fhsc.com.
Note: If a prior authorization number has not been obtained by the prescriber and the pharmacist is unable to reach
the prescriber, the pharmacist may obtain a prior authorization for up to a 72-hour emergency supply for any drug
that requires prior authorization, subject to state laws and Medicaid restrictions.

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, Medicaid requires providers to pursue available other coverage before Medicaid considers payment, and failure to obtain prior authorization does not override that responsibility; this provider manual is more up to date overall and should guide current practice by default. The newest policy source dated 2021-03-01 is older and says claims should be billed to the third party first, with claim issues including prior authorization requirements resolved with the third party before Medicaid submission. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01] [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: Pharmacy Carve-Out:5 — 2021-03-01]
answers.policy_definition: Policy documents state that for third-party coverage, providers must bill the third party before Medicaid and resolve claim issues, including prior authorization requirements, before submitting the claim to Medicaid: "providers must first bill Medicare or the other third-party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — 2020-10-01]; "Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01]. The newer policy excerpt also says items in certain carve-out sections "are not subject to the carve-out and should continue to be billed to the member’s MC plan" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: Pharmacy Carve-Out:5 — 2021-03-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: The provider manual states that Medicaid requires use of available other coverage before Medicaid payment: "Federal regulations require that all available resources must be used before Medicaid considers payment" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]. It further says a provider must pursue other coverage before submitting a claim to Medicaid and that if another party is the primary payor, "that responsible third party must pay first" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]. It also states, "Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibilit..." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources require third-party coverage to be addressed before Medicaid payment: "providers must first bill Medicare or the other third-party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — 2020-10-01] and "a provider [must] pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources tie prior authorization/claim issues to the third party rather than Medicaid first: "Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01] and "Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibilit..." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The provider manual is newer and states the rule in broader terms of federal and regulatory payment order: "all available resources must be used before Medicaid considers payment" and "that responsible third party must pay first" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01], while the policy excerpts focus on billing sequence and resolving third-party claim issues before Medicaid submission [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — 2020-10-01] [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01].

The policy excerpt explicitly says claim issues, "including prior authorization (PA) requirements," must be resolved with the third party before Medicaid submission [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01], while the provider manual excerpt only states that failure to obtain prior authorization does not override the responsibility to use other coverage first [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].
answers.caveats: The provider manual page excerpt is truncated after "responsibilit...", so the exact full sentence about prior authorization cannot be fully quoted from the provided chunk [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]. Also, none of the provided excerpts specifically define "crossover claims" by that term; the comparison is grounded in third-party liability and prior authorization language relevant to claims after Medicare/other insurance payment.

policy.text:
New York State Medicaid is always the payor of last resort; every effort must be taken to
obtain correct billing information.
If TPL is applicable, providers must the third-party insurer first, including commercial plans or Medicare,
and bill Medicaid as secondary, if appropriate. Any claim issues must be resolved with the third-party insurer,
including prior authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary
insurer does not cover a specific medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the
third-party insurer will result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
If the member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs. Information on Part B covered drugs can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers, or
• Medicare Part D for other prescription drugs. Information on Part D covered drugs can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
If the member has Medicare Parts A or B (or both) and does not have Medicare Part D, providers will bill:
• Part D covered claims to Medicare Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET).

cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.

policy.text:
New York State Medicaid is always the payor of last resort; every effort must be taken to
obtain correct billing information.
If TPL is applicable, providers must the third-party insurer first, including commercial plans or Medicare,
and bill Medicaid as secondary, if appropriate. Any claim issues must be resolved with the third-party insurer,
including prior authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary
insurer does not cover a specific medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the
third-party insurer will result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
If the member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs. Information on Part B covered drugs can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers, or
• Medicare Part D for other prescription drugs. Information on Part D covered drugs can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
If the member has Medicare Parts A or B (or both) and does not have Medicare Part D, providers will bill:
• Part D covered claims to Medicare Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET).

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted.

Members must meet certain conditions to
receive these benefits.

Medicare Part B
Medicare Part B covers doctors’ services, outpatient care and some other medical services that Part A does not cover.
Information regarding Medicare Part B outpatient covered drugs can be found here:
https://www.medicare.gov/coverage/prescription-drugs-outpatient.
Medicare Part B covers certain drugs such as:
• Drugs used with an item of durable medical equipment.
• Some antigens,
• Injectable osteoporosis drugs,
• Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents,
• Blood clotting factors,
• Injectable and infused drugs,
• Oral End-Stage Renal Disease (ESRD) drugs,
• Parenteral and enteral nutrition (intravenous and tube feeding)
• Intravenous Immune Globulin (IVIG) provided in the home,
• Vaccinations,
• Immunosuppressive drugs following a Medicare paid transplant,
• Oral cancer drugs,

For Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims, even for a procedure that would have required Medicaid prior approval, prior
approval is not required if Medicare approved and paid for a service and/or procedure.

provider_manual.text:
Prior Authorization Programs
The Medicaid program requires prior authorization for certain drugs under the following programs:
• Preferred Drug Program (PDP)
• Brand When Less Than Generic Program (BLTG)
• Dose Optimization Initiative
• Clinical Drug Review Program (CDRP)
• Drug Utilization Review (DUR) Program
• Mandatory Generic Drug Program (MGDP)
Prescribers may need to obtain prior authorizations for certain drugs. Only prescribers or their authorized agent may
submit a request for a prior authorization. Third party requests are prohibited: prescribers may not contract with or
assign authority to dispensing pharmacists, manufacturers, or other persons or companies to initiate their PA requests.
General information on the prescription drug prior authorizations, including the above programs, can be found at the
following website: https://newyork.fhsc.com.
Note: If a prior authorization number has not been obtained by the prescriber and the pharmacist is unable to reach
the prescriber, the pharmacist may obtain a prior authorization for up to a 72-hour emergency supply for any drug
that requires prior authorization, subject to state laws and Medicaid restrictions.

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. For crossover claims, the current manual says Medicaid is secondary to Medicare/other coverage and that failure to obtain prior authorization does not override the responsible third party’s obligation to pay first, while the newest policy source dated 2022-03-01 says Medicare crossover claims are a special case for certain codes. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update March 2022 Volume 38 Number 3:7 — Mar 01, 2022]
answers.policy_definition: Policy documents state that for third-party coverage, "Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid" and that "providers will bill: • Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs"; the March 2022 update also says "HCPCS codes “G2082” and “G2083” should not be billed to the NYS Medicaid program... These codes are only authorized for use on Medicare crossover claims." [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020] [New York State Medicaid Update March 2022 Volume 38 Number 3:7 — Mar 01, 2022]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The provider manual says Medicaid requires use of all available resources before payment and that a provider must pursue other coverage before submitting a claim to Medicaid: "Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment" and "If there is a responsible third party who should be paying... that responsible third party must pay first." It also states, "Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibilit..." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources require other coverage to be addressed before Medicaid payment: "providers must first bill Medicare or the other third-party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — Oct 01, 2020] and "all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources tie Medicaid billing to prior authorization/claim issue resolution with the primary payer: "Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020] and "Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibilit..." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The policy source specifically identifies a crossover-claim exception for certain codes: "HCPCS codes “G2082” and “G2083”... are only authorized for use on Medicare crossover claims" [New York State Medicaid Update March 2022 Volume 38 Number 3:7 — Mar 01, 2022], while the provider manual excerpt does not define crossover-claim code exceptions.

The provider manual is newer and gives broader current third-party-liability guidance: "Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment" and "that responsible third party must pay first" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025], whereas the policy excerpts focus on billing Medicare/other insurance first and resolving PA issues before Medicaid submission [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020].
answers.caveats: The provided provider manual excerpts do not contain a specific, explicit definition of "crossover claims" or a crossover-specific prior-approval rule; they provide general third-party-liability and prior-authorization guidance. The policy excerpts include one crossover-specific statement for G2082/G2083, but not a full rule set for all crossover claims.

policy.text:
Notification to Providers of Requirement to Attach Explanation of
Benefits from Third-Party Payors to Medicaid Claims
To ensure that New York State (NYS) Medicaid is the payor of last resort when a Medicaid recipient is eligible
for Medicare or has other third-party insurance benefits, providers must first bill Medicare or the other third-
party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid. In the coming months, providers will be
required to attach an Explanation of Benefits (EOB) for fee-for-service (FFS) claims when submitting a claim
to Medicaid for services rendered to a recipient who is also eligible for Medicare or has other third-party
coverage. If an EOB is not submitted with the claim, the claim will be denied. The Office of the Medicaid
Inspector General (OMIG) will be providing additional information regarding the timeline and requirements for
implementation of this initiative.
Please Note: Pharmacy claims, Medicare crossover claims, and non-covered procedure codes identified by
NYS Medicaid are not subject to this requirement.
Questions regarding this notice should be directed to OMIG’s Bureau of Third Party and Payment Oversight
at ThirdP@omig.ny.gov.

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. For crossover claims, the current manual says prior authorization does not override third-party liability: the responsible third party must pay first, and Medicaid only considers payment after other coverage is pursued [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025].
answers.policy_definition: Policy documents state that providers must pursue other coverage before Medicaid and that any claim issues, including prior authorization requirements, must be resolved with the third party before submitting to Medicaid: “Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid.” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020] The later policy update also states that “providers will be required to attach an Explanation of Benefits” from third-party payors to Medicaid claims and that Medicaid is the “payor of last resort” when Medicare or other third-party insurance exists [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — Oct 01, 2020]. The newest policy excerpt available also says providers must pursue available other coverage before submitting a claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:15-16 — Nov 01, 2021].
answers.provider_manual_definition: The provider manual states that Medicaid requires all available resources to be used before payment is considered and that a provider must pursue any available other coverage before submitting a claim to Medicaid: “Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025] It further states that if a responsible third party is primary, “that responsible third party must pay first,” and that “Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibilit…” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025].
similarities: Both sources require other coverage to be pursued before Medicaid payment: “providers must first bill Medicare or the other third-party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid” [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — Oct 01, 2020] and “require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources treat Medicaid as secondary/payor of last resort: “NYS Medicaid is the payor of last resort” [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — Oct 01, 2020] and “all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The policy excerpts explicitly mention third-party prior authorization issues must be resolved before Medicaid submission: “Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020], while the provider manual excerpt only says that “Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibilit…” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025].

The provider manual is newer and more current for this issue: it is dated “Oct 01, 2025” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025], while the newest policy excerpt provided is dated “Nov 01, 2021” [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:15-16 — Nov 01, 2021].
answers.caveats: The provider manual excerpt is truncated at “responsibilit…,” so the full sentence about prior authorization is not fully visible in the provided text [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]. Also, the question asks about “prior approval after Medicaid payment,” but the provided excerpts address prior authorization and third-party billing before Medicaid payment; no excerpt expressly defines a post-payment prior approval requirement.

test_id_31.query_content: What are the requirements for Coordination of Benefits (COB)?

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.

www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2022/docs/mu_no12_oct22_pr.pdf;

Medicaid Update, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2022/
docs/mu_no4_apr22_pr.pdf;
• Reminder: Pharmacy Billing for Medicaid Patients with Other Coverage article published in the
November 2021 issue of the Medicaid Update, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/

• Attention Pharmacy Providers: Coordination of Benefits (COB) Processing Change for Other Payer

located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2017/oct17_mu.pdf.

policy.text:
Fee-for-Service Dental Claim Submission Guidance
When Third-Party Liability Payment Involved
New York State (NYS) Medicaid continues to work to increase provider compliance with properly reporting
correct Coordination of Benefits (COB) information on claims submitted for beneficiaries who have primary
insurance. The COB claim to NYS Medicaid must accurately reflect the primary payers’ adjudication of the
claim as reported in the Explanation of Benefits (EOB).
When the primary insurance is a Medicare Advantage Plan / Medicare Part C with dental coverage:
• Claims for services involving a third-party payment by a Medicare Advantage Plan greater than zero
dollars (>$0) must be submitted using the electronic claim format. This also includes electronic
submission of by-report procedure codes if a payment was made by a Medicare Pact C Plan.
• The payor code for Medicare Advantage Plans is the 16-health maintenance organization (HMO) and
must be indicated on all electronic claims when Medicare Advantage is primary.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
6

Coordination of Benefits (COB) ensures the correct party pays first. NYS Medicaid is always the payor of
last resort; federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid
considers payment. If there is a responsible third-party that should be paying for the health benefits of the
patient such as a health insurance provider, the responsible third-party should pay first. NYS Medicaid pays
the lesser of Patient Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee, regardless of the PR amount. For
pharmacies, this rule applies to all PR, which includes deductible, co-insurance, copay, and other patient
responsibility.
Effective November 1, 2022, NYS Medicaid will not accept a combination of Other Coverage Code of
"3" in National Council for Prescription Drug Program (NCPDP) field 308-C8 (Other Coverage Code)
with any reject code in field 472-6E when another third party is responsible for payment. The pre-
adjudication edit will be expanded for this field and will return the NCPDP Reject Code (DE 3988) (6E - M/I
Other Payer Reject Code).
The provider must work with the primary insurance to obtain coverage for the member.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Workers’ Compensation and Coordination of Benefits
If a member has a work-related injury, for which they have an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’
compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.
If the drug claim is NOT related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an
unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field
309-C9. Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on the
claim, if applicable.
The Workers’ Compensation Board can be accessed here: https://www.wcb.ny.gov/content/main/hcpp/hcpp.jsp.

provider_manual.text:
o Medicaid members younger than 21 years old.
o Medicaid members during pregnancy and for the two months after the month in which the
pregnancy ends.
o Residents of Adult Care Facilities licensed by the New York State Department of Health (DOH).
o Residents of nursing homes.
o Residents of Intermediate Care Facilities for the Developmentally Disabled (ICF/DD).
o Residents of Office of Mental Health (OMH) or Office of Persons with Developmental Disabilities
(OPWDD) certified residences.
o Members in Comprehensive Medicaid Case Management (CMCM) or Service Coordination
Programs.
o Members in OMH or OPWDD Home and Community Based Services (HCBS) Waiver Programs. and
o Members in a DOH HCBS Waiver Program for Persons with Traumatic Brain Injury (TBI).
o Members with incomes below 100 percent of the federal poverty level.

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, provider manual guidance is more up to date overall for COB and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2025-04-01 and the provider manual both require other available/third-party coverage to be used first because Medicaid is the payer of last resort. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]
answers.policy_definition: Policy documents define COB as requiring NYS Medicaid providers to bill applicable third parties before billing Medicaid: “NYS Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment.” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] Earlier policy language states: “Coordination of Benefits (COB) ensures the correct party pays first. NYS Medicaid is always the payor of last resort; federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment.” [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6-7 — Oct 01, 2022]
answers.provider_manual_definition: Provider manual guidance defines COB/third-party liability as requiring available resources to be used before Medicaid pays: “Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025] The manual also gives a specific COB example for workers’ compensation: “Workers’ Compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources require other coverage/third parties to pay before Medicaid: “providers are required to bill applicable third parties” and “all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment.” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources state Medicaid is the payer/payor of last resort: “NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort” and “If there is a responsible third party ... that responsible third party must pay first.” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]
differences: The provider manual is more specific about the provider obligation and legal basis, stating that “18NYCRR §540.6(e) require[s] a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid,” while the policy update uses broader billing language about applicable third parties. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]

The provider manual adds a workers’ compensation-specific COB rule: “Workers’ Compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program,” which is not stated in the cited policy excerpts. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]
answers.caveats: The provided policy excerpts do not include a full standalone definition beyond payer-order and last-resort language, and one policy chunk only points to older COB articles rather than restating the rule. [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Four: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 9:7 — May 01, 2023]

test_id_32.query_content: What are the requirements for Coordination of Benefits (COB)?

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Fee-for-Service Dental Claim Submission Guidance
When Third-Party Liability Payment Involved
New York State (NYS) Medicaid continues to work to increase provider compliance with properly reporting
correct Coordination of Benefits (COB) information on claims submitted for beneficiaries who have primary
insurance. The COB claim to NYS Medicaid must accurately reflect the primary payers’ adjudication of the
claim as reported in the Explanation of Benefits (EOB).
When the primary insurance is a Medicare Advantage Plan / Medicare Part C with dental coverage:
• Claims for services involving a third-party payment by a Medicare Advantage Plan greater than zero
dollars (>$0) must be submitted using the electronic claim format. This also includes electronic
submission of by-report procedure codes if a payment was made by a Medicare Pact C Plan.
• The payor code for Medicare Advantage Plans is the 16-health maintenance organization (HMO) and
must be indicated on all electronic claims when Medicare Advantage is primary.
