policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Four: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
6

Coordination of Benefits (COB) ensures the correct party pays first. NYS Medicaid is always the payor of
last resort; federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid
considers payment. If there is a responsible third-party that should be paying for the health benefits of the
patient such as a health insurance provider, the responsible third-party should pay first. NYS Medicaid pays
the lesser of Patient Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee, regardless of the PR amount. For
pharmacies, this rule applies to all PR, which includes deductible, co-insurance, copay, and other patient
responsibility.
Effective November 1, 2022, NYS Medicaid will not accept a combination of Other Coverage Code of
"3" in National Council for Prescription Drug Program (NCPDP) field 308-C8 (Other Coverage Code)
with any reject code in field 472-6E when another third party is responsible for payment. The pre-
adjudication edit will be expanded for this field and will return the NCPDP Reject Code (DE 3988) (6E - M/I
Other Payer Reject Code).
The provider must work with the primary insurance to obtain coverage for the member.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Workers’ Compensation and Coordination of Benefits
If a member has a work-related injury, for which they have an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’
compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.
If the drug claim is NOT related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an
unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field
309-C9. Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on the
claim, if applicable.
The Workers’ Compensation Board can be accessed here: https://www.wcb.ny.gov/content/main/hcpp/hcpp.jsp.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text: If all three conditions are not met, the billing provider may call the Department for assistance.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for COB guidance and should receive more weight by default. The policy sources dated 2025-04-01 and earlier say Medicaid is the payer of last resort and that other liable coverage must be billed first, but the provider manual adds more specific current billing instructions for third-party liability and Workers’ Compensation.
answers.policy_definition: Policy documents define COB as requiring NYS Medicaid providers to bill applicable third parties that may be liable before billing Medicaid, because “NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort” and “all other available resources [must] be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]. They also state that “COB ensures the correct party pays first,” that “the responsible third-party should pay first,” and that NYS Medicaid “pays the lesser of Patient Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee” [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6-7 — Oct 01, 2022].
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance says “Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment,” and that “a provider [must] pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid”; if “a responsible third party” is the primary payor, “that responsible third party must pay first” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]. It also adds a specific COB rule for Workers’ Compensation: if a member has an open work-related case, “Workers’ compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program,” but if the drug claim is unrelated, “NYRx may be billed as the primary payer” with the specified eligibility clarification code [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].
similarities: Both sources require other available coverage to be used before Medicaid pays: “all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] and “all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources state that a responsible third party must pay first: “the responsible third-party should pay first” [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6-7 — Oct 01, 2022] and “that responsible third party must pay first” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The provider manual is more specific and current, adding Workers’ Compensation instructions: “Workers’ compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program,” with an exception when the claim is unrelated to the work injury [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]. The policy excerpts provided do not include this Workers’ Compensation-specific rule [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025].

The policy excerpts explicitly describe Medicaid as “always the payer of last resort” and say COB “ensures the correct party pays first” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025; New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6-7 — Oct 01, 2022], while the provider manual uses the broader phrasing that “all available resources” must be used before Medicaid pays [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025].
answers.caveats: The provider manual is newer overall and should guide current COB billing questions, but the policy excerpts still confirm the core payer-of-last-resort rule. Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents beyond the quoted COB/payment-first requirements, and the provided provider manual excerpts are limited to pharmacy guidance and Workers’ Compensation.

policy.text:
6

Coordination of Benefits (COB) ensures the correct party pays first. NYS Medicaid is always the payor of
last resort; federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid
considers payment. If there is a responsible third-party that should be paying for the health benefits of the
patient such as a health insurance provider, the responsible third-party should pay first. NYS Medicaid pays
the lesser of Patient Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee, regardless of the PR amount. For
pharmacies, this rule applies to all PR, which includes deductible, co-insurance, copay, and other patient
responsibility.
Effective November 1, 2022, NYS Medicaid will not accept a combination of Other Coverage Code of
"3" in National Council for Prescription Drug Program (NCPDP) field 308-C8 (Other Coverage Code)
with any reject code in field 472-6E when another third party is responsible for payment. The pre-
adjudication edit will be expanded for this field and will return the NCPDP Reject Code (DE 3988) (6E - M/I
Other Payer Reject Code).
The provider must work with the primary insurance to obtain coverage for the member.

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted.

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for Coordination of Benefits (COB) and should receive more weight by default. The policy source dated 2025-04-01 is consistent on the core rule that other available coverage must be billed first because NYS Medicaid is the payer of last resort. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]; [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy guidance says NYS Medicaid providers "are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid" and that "NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort"; it also states that "Federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment." [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] Earlier policy guidance similarly says COB "ensures the correct party pays first" and that "If there is a responsible third-party that should be paying for the health benefits of the patient such as a health insurance provider, the responsible third-party should pay first." [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6-7 — Oct 01, 2022]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance says "Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment" and that "a provider [must] pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid." It further states that "If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025] The manual also gives a specific COB example for workers’ compensation: "Workers’ compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program" when the claim is related to an open workers’ compensation case. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources require other available coverage to be used before Medicaid pays: policy says providers must bill liable third parties first and Medicaid is "the payer of last resort," while the manual says "all available resources" must be used before Medicaid considers payment. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources place primary payment responsibility on the responsible third party before Medicaid: policy says "the responsible third-party should pay first," and the manual says the responsible third party "must pay first." [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6-7 — Oct 01, 2022]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]
differences: The provider manual is newer and more operationally specific: it adds that a provider must "pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid," which is not stated in the April 2025 policy excerpt. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]; [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]

The provider manual includes a workers’ compensation COB rule: "Workers’ compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program" for related claims, while the policy excerpts provided do not include a workers’ compensation-specific COB rule. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]; [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]
answers.caveats: The provided policy excerpts do not include a full standalone definition of COB beyond the payer-order rule, and the provider manual excerpt is limited to pharmacy policy guidance. Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for any broader Medicaid program context beyond the quoted COB payment-order requirements. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-17 — Oct 01, 2025]

test_id_35.query_content: What are the requirements for Coordination of Benefits (COB)?

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for Coordination of Benefits (COB) and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2025-04-01 and the provider manual both require other available coverage to pay first and Medicaid to act as payer of last resort, but the provider manual adds a specific Workers’ Compensation rule. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]; [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy source says NYS Medicaid providers must bill liable third parties before billing Medicaid: “NYS Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid” and “NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort.” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] It also states that “Federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment.” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] Older policy language is consistent: “COB ensures the correct party pays first” and “If there is a responsible third-party that should be paying for the health benefits of the patient such as a health insurance provider, the responsible third-party should pay first.” [New York York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6-7 — Oct 01, 2022]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual source states: “Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment” and “a provider [must] pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025] It further says that if “there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025] The provider manual also adds a Workers’ Compensation-specific rule: if a member has a work-related injury with an open Workers’ Compensation case, “Workers’ compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources require other available coverage/resources to be used before Medicaid pays: “all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment” and “all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment.” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources place Medicaid last in line for payment: “NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort” and the manual requires the responsible third party to “pay first.” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]
differences: The provider manual is more specific and current on Workers’ Compensation: it says that for a work-related injury with an open Workers’ Compensation case, “Workers’ compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.” The policy excerpt provided does not include this Workers’ Compensation-specific rule. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]

The policy source frames COB as “the correct party pays first” and says a responsible third party such as a health insurance provider should pay first, while the provider manual frames the rule as a provider duty to “pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid.” [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6-7 — Oct 01, 2022]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]
answers.caveats: The policy excerpts provided are limited to general COB/payment-order language and do not include the full current policy text beyond the April 2025 update; the provider manual is newer and should guide current practice unless you are researching historical policy. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-17 — Oct 01, 2025]

test_id_36.query_content: What responsibilities do pharmacists have when required patient or prescriber information is missing?
test_id_36.concept: pharmacist responsibilities when required patient or prescriber information is missing

policy.text:
Reminder: Submitting for Prior Authorization of Non-Preferred Drugs
Prior authorization (PA) requests for non-preferred drugs must be submitted by the prescriber or by an
authorized agent of the prescriber. An authorized agent of the prescriber is considered an employee of the
prescribing practitioner and has access to the patient's medical records (i.e., nurse, medical assistant).
When presented with a prescription for a non-preferred drug that requires PA, the pharmacist must notify
the prescriber that PA is required and notify the patient once the prescriber has obtained the PA. If the
pharmacist is unable to reach the prescriber and a PA number has not yet been obtained by the prescriber,
the pharmacist may initiate a PA for a 72-hour emergency supply only.
Pharmacists are not authorized to submit for a PA nor enter into an agreement with a prescriber to
effectuate PAs and appeals on the prescriber’s behalf (except for the 72-hour emergency supply mentioned
above or when employed by a prescriber).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
A supervising pharmacist must adhere to the following
responsibilities:
• The supervising pharmacist is responsible for the proper conduct of the pharmacy.
• The supervising pharmacist oversees the practice of other employed pharmacists and ensures that
those unlicensed persons do not engage in unlawful activity.
• The supervising pharmacist must have an active role in the conduct of the pharmacy and not a passive
role.
• The supervising pharmacist may not assign their responsibility to others.
• The supervising pharmacist must be aware of all laws and regulations and must instruct and direct
others to ensure compliance.
• The supervising pharmacist must work full-time (at least 30 hours per week). If the establishment is
operating less than 30 hours per week, the supervising pharmacist must work the majority of the hours.
• If the supervising pharmacist is to be absent from a pharmacy at any time, the supervising pharmacist
must ensure another qualified pharmacist is present in their absence.
• The supervising pharmacist cannot supervise more than one pharmacy establishment at a time.

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacists may work with the patient and contact their prescriber to either change the
prescription to another drug or supply or obtain a prior authorization (PA) following the transition fill.
New York State Medicaid Update Special Edition – Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition pg. 4

Transition fills will be identified in the eMedNY claim response using NCPDP field 548-6F, Approved
Message Code, with code “005” — Claim paid under the plan's transition benefit period, otherwise claim
would have rejected as PA is required.
Vaccines Administered by Pharmacists
As indicated in the Scope of Benefits document, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/
redesign/mrt2/pharmacy_transition/repository/docs/rx_scope_of_benefits.pdf, vaccines that are administered
by pharmacists are subject to the benefit transition and should be billed to NYRx. Policy and billing guidance
for vaccines can be found within the New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Program Pharmacists as
Immunizers Fact Sheet, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/docs/phar
_immun_fact.pdf.

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
If the address, age, or CIN of the Medicaid member are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter any of these
items on the prescription if the information:

• Is otherwise readily available in the records of the pharmacy and the pharmacist knows the person who is
requesting that the prescription be filled, or
• The pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate.
Prescriptions written for controlled substances must meet the requirements of Article 33 of the Public Health Law. In
accordance with New York State Department of Health Codes, Rules, and Regulations Title 10, Part 80, pharmacists are
permitted to add or change only certain information on controlled substance prescriptions.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacies are not required to generate and keep a hard copy of electronic prescriptions and fiscal orders if they are
securely stored and maintained. When stored electronically, the electronic imaging of prescriptions and fiscal orders
that were e-prescribed must result in an exact reproduction of the original order and may be required to be
authenticated.
Telephone Orders
Prescribers may telephone prescriptions and fiscal orders for drugs directly to a pharmacy unless otherwise prohibited
by state or federal law or regulations. The pharmacist is responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the
prescriber's identity and validity of the prescription if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist.
• Telephoned non-controlled prescription drug orders are considered original.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pick Up / Receipt
• Pharmacies/DME providers must obtain a legible signature from the Medicaid member, their caregiver, or
their designee to confirm receipt of the prescription drugs, over-the-counter products, medical/surgical
supplies, and DME items when picked up from the provider. The pharmacy must have documentation
confirming the prescription number(s), date of pick-up and signature. One signature is sufficient for
multiple prescriptions being picked up at one time. Claim submission is not proof that the prescription or
fiscal order was actually furnished.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation. Reminder, counseling must include proper storage and use of the drug or item
dispensed.
• The pharmacy is accountable to provide intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled, defective, or
otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

[Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf:4,5 — 2025-10-01] Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define pharmacist responsibilities for missing prescriber information in the provider manual excerpts. [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf:4,5 — 2025-10-01]
similarities: Both sources address pharmacist action when required information is missing, but only in limited contexts: the policy excerpt references pharmacist contact/notification in the PA transition/non-preferred drug context, and the provider manual addresses missing member address, age, or CIN. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — 2023-07-01] [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf:4,5 — 2025-10-01]

Both sources leave part of the question unanswered in the provided excerpts: neither excerpt fully defines responsibilities for missing patient or prescriber information across all prescriptions. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — 2023-07-01] [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf:4,5 — 2025-10-01]
differences: The provider manual gives a specific rule for missing patient information: "the pharmacist is not required to enter any of these items" when the listed conditions are met. [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf:4,5 — 2025-10-01] The policy excerpt does not provide a comparable rule for missing patient information. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — 2023-07-01]

The policy excerpt focuses on a PA-related responsibility, stating that "the pharmacist must notify the prescr..." and may "work with the patient and contact their prescriber" to change the prescription or obtain PA after a transition fill. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — 2023-07-01] The provider manual excerpt does not describe that PA notification/contact duty. [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf:4,5 — 2025-10-01]
answers.caveats: The policy excerpt is truncated, so the exact prescriber-notification wording is incomplete. The provided provider manual excerpt addresses missing patient identifiers (address, age, CIN) but does not ground a rule for missing prescriber information; therefore, that part of the question cannot be fully answered from the supplied excerpts. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — 2023-07-01] [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf:4,5 — 2025-10-01]

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Reminder: Submitting for Prior Authorization of Non-Preferred Drugs
Prior authorization (PA) requests for non-preferred drugs must be submitted by the prescriber or by an
authorized agent of the prescriber. An authorized agent of the prescriber is considered an employee of the
prescribing practitioner and has access to the patient's medical records (i.e., nurse, medical assistant).
When presented with a prescription for a non-preferred drug that requires PA, the pharmacist must notify
the prescriber that PA is required and notify the patient once the prescriber has obtained the PA. If the
pharmacist is unable to reach the prescriber and a PA number has not yet been obtained by the prescriber,
the pharmacist may initiate a PA for a 72-hour emergency supply only.
Pharmacists are not authorized to submit for a PA nor enter into an agreement with a prescriber to
effectuate PAs and appeals on the prescriber’s behalf (except for the 72-hour emergency supply mentioned
above or when employed by a prescriber).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
A supervising pharmacist must adhere to the following
responsibilities:
• The supervising pharmacist is responsible for the proper conduct of the pharmacy.
• The supervising pharmacist oversees the practice of other employed pharmacists and ensures that
those unlicensed persons do not engage in unlawful activity.
• The supervising pharmacist must have an active role in the conduct of the pharmacy and not a passive
role.
• The supervising pharmacist may not assign their responsibility to others.
• The supervising pharmacist must be aware of all laws and regulations and must instruct and direct
others to ensure compliance.
• The supervising pharmacist must work full-time (at least 30 hours per week). If the establishment is
operating less than 30 hours per week, the supervising pharmacist must work the majority of the hours.
• If the supervising pharmacist is to be absent from a pharmacy at any time, the supervising pharmacist
must ensure another qualified pharmacist is present in their absence.
• The supervising pharmacist cannot supervise more than one pharmacy establishment at a time.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Reminder: Record Retention, Furnishing of Records, and Permitting Audit and Cooperation
with Governmental Agencies
As New York State (NYS) Medicaid-enrolled providers, in accordance with Title 18 of the New York Codes,
Rules and Regulations (NYCRR) §504.3(a) and §504.3(g), located at: https://regs.health.ny.gov/content/
section-5043-duties-provider, the pharmacy owner and the supervising pharmacist are responsible for:
• preparing and maintaining contemporaneous records demonstrating the right to receive payment
under the medical assistance program [refer to 18 NYCRR §504.3(a)];
• keeping for a period of six years from the date the care, services or supplies were furnished, all records
necessary to disclose the nature and extent of services furnished and all information regarding claims
for payment submitted by, or on behalf of, the provider [refer to 18 NYCRR §504.3(a)];
Please note: Providers continue to be subject to any additional record retention requirements
outlined in their prior or current contracts with Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) or Managed
Long Term Care (MLTC) Plans or Subcontractors thereof, including retention for 10 years or
more as delineated therein.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
If the address, age, or CIN of the Medicaid member are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter any of these
items on the prescription if the information:

• Is otherwise readily available in the records of the pharmacy and the pharmacist knows the person who is
requesting that the prescription be filled, or
• The pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate.
Prescriptions written for controlled substances must meet the requirements of Article 33 of the Public Health Law. In
accordance with New York State Department of Health Codes, Rules, and Regulations Title 10, Part 80, pharmacists are
permitted to add or change only certain information on controlled substance prescriptions.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacies are not required to generate and keep a hard copy of electronic prescriptions and fiscal orders if they are
securely stored and maintained. When stored electronically, the electronic imaging of prescriptions and fiscal orders
that were e-prescribed must result in an exact reproduction of the original order and may be required to be
authenticated.
Telephone Orders
Prescribers may telephone prescriptions and fiscal orders for drugs directly to a pharmacy unless otherwise prohibited
by state or federal law or regulations. The pharmacist is responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the
prescriber's identity and validity of the prescription if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist.
• Telephoned non-controlled prescription drug orders are considered original.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The pharmacist is responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the
prescriber’s identity if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist. Electronic imaging of prescriptions and fiscal
orders must result in an exact reproduction of the original order and may be required to be authenticated.
Storage Requirements – Vaccine and Medication
Medicaid Pharmacy providers are required to follow Regulations set forth by the Commissioner of Education,
section 63.6(b)(3) and the CDC guidelines in the CDC Storage and Handling Toolkit for vaccines and medication
requiring cold storage. Pharmacies must have a purpose-built or pharmaceutical grade refrigerator with adequate
storage space with fan-forced air circulation; a dorm-style or bar-style unit with an evaporator plate/cooling coil
does not qualify.

Additionally, Medicaid providers who are part of the Vaccine for Children (VFC) program must meet criteria for
refrigeration and vaccine storage set forth in the guidelines outlined in the Stand-Alone Storage Unit Purchasing
Guidance and Temperature Monitoring Requirements documents.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the pharmacist is not required to enter missing Medicaid member address, age, or CIN if the information is otherwise readily available in pharmacy records and the pharmacist knows the requester, or if the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied the prescription is legitimate. The newest policy source dated 2023-07-01 is about prior authorization requests and says the pharmacist must notify the prescriber when a non-preferred drug requires PA, so the provider manual is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default.
answers.policy_definition: The newest grounded policy evidence does not define a pharmacist’s duties when required patient or prescriber information is missing; instead, it addresses prior authorization for non-preferred drugs: “When presented with a prescription for a non-preferred drug that requires PA, the pharmacist must notify the prescr...” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — 2023-07-01]. Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided policy documents to define this concept for policy documents.
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance says: “If the address, age, or CIN of the Medicaid member are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter any of these items on the prescription if the information: • Is otherwise readily available in the records of the pharmacy and the pharmacist knows the person who is requesting that the prescription be filled, or • The pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4, 5 — 2025-10-01]. It also states that controlled substances must still meet Article 33 requirements: “Prescriptions written for controlled substances must meet the requirements of Article 33 of the Public Hea...” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4, 5 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources place pharmacist action in the context of prescription processing and legitimacy-related requirements: the policy source says, “When presented with a prescription for a non-preferred drug that requires PA, the pharmacist must notify the prescr...” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — 2023-07-01], and the provider manual says the pharmacist may rely on being “otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4, 5 — 2025-10-01]

Both sources are operational guidance for pharmacists in Medicaid pharmacy practice, but only the provider manual directly addresses missing member information: “If the address, age, or CIN of the Medicaid member are missing...” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4, 5 — 2025-10-01]
differences: The provider manual directly answers the missing-information question by stating the pharmacist is “not required to enter any of these items” when the stated conditions are met, while the policy source does not provide that rule. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4, 5 — 2025-10-01] [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — 2023-07-01]

The policy source’s relevant language concerns prior authorization and prescriber notification for non-preferred drugs—“the pharmacist must notify the prescr...” — rather than missing patient or prescriber information, so it is not controlling for this concept. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — 2023-07-01]
answers.caveats: The policy excerpts provided do not include a complete rule on missing patient or prescriber information, so the policy-side definition is insufficiently grounded. The provider manual excerpt is the more recent and more directly applicable source for this question, but the quoted text is limited to missing address, age, or CIN and does not expressly address every possible type of missing patient or prescriber information. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4, 5 — 2025-10-01]

policy.text:
• The pharmacy is responsible to replace lost, stolen, or mis-directed drugs at no additional cost to the
member or the Medicaid Program.
• Pharmacy providers who deliver medication without member or authorized individual consent will be
required to accept the return of the medication, credit Medicaid the claim, and destroy those drugs per
State Law.
• All Medicaid claims for drugs not picked up or delivered must be reversed within 60 days or sooner as
required by the mandatory part of New York State’s compliance programs under 18 NYCRR §521.

provider_manual.text:
Pick Up / Receipt
• Pharmacies/DME providers must obtain a legible signature from the Medicaid member, their caregiver, or
their designee to confirm receipt of the prescription drugs, over-the-counter products, medical/surgical
supplies, and DME items when picked up from the provider. The pharmacy must have documentation
confirming the prescription number(s), date of pick-up and signature. One signature is sufficient for
multiple prescriptions being picked up at one time. Claim submission is not proof that the prescription or
fiscal order was actually furnished.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation. Reminder, counseling must include proper storage and use of the drug or item
dispensed.
• The pharmacy is accountable to provide intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled, defective, or
otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the pharmacist may omit missing member address, age, or CIN from the prescription if the information is readily available in pharmacy records and the pharmacist knows the requester, or if the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied the prescription is legitimate; this provider manual is more up to date overall and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2023-07-01 addresses a related but narrower issue for non-preferred drugs, requiring the pharmacist to notify the prescriber when prior authorization is needed, but it does not define the pharmacist’s duties when patient or prescriber information is missing.
answers.policy_definition: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents. The newest policy chunk only states that for non-preferred drugs requiring prior authorization, "the pharmacist must notify the prescr..." [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — Jul 01, 2023], which does not describe responsibilities when required patient or prescriber information is missing.
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, if "the address, age, or CIN of the Medicaid member are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter any of these items on the prescription" when the information "Is otherwise readily available in the records of the pharmacy and the pharmacist knows the person who is requesting that the prescription be filled," or when "The pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4-5 — Oct 01, 2025] The same chunk also states that "Prescriptions written for controlled substances must meet the requirements of Article 33 of the Public Hea..." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4-5 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources place a pharmacist-facing responsibility in the prescription workflow rather than shifting the issue away from the pharmacist: the policy says "the pharmacist must notify the prescr..." when PA is needed [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — Jul 01, 2023], and the provider manual says the pharmacist may rely on records or legitimacy judgment when member information is missing [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4-5 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources are limited to specific circumstances and do not provide a broad, all-purpose definition for every missing-information scenario; the policy excerpt is limited to non-preferred drugs requiring PA [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — Jul 01, 2023], while the provider manual excerpt is limited to missing address, age, or CIN [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4-5 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The provider manual gives an explicit rule for missing member information: "the pharmacist is not required to enter any of these items on the prescription" if the stated conditions are met [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4-5 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy excerpt does not provide a comparable missing-information rule [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — Jul 01, 2023].

The policy excerpt focuses on prior authorization for non-preferred drugs and says "the pharmacist must notify the prescr..." [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — Jul 01, 2023], whereas the provider manual focuses on whether missing address, age, or CIN must be entered and on the pharmacist’s satisfaction that the prescription is legitimate [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4-5 — Oct 01, 2025].
answers.caveats: The policy excerpt is truncated and does not fully state the pharmacist’s instruction, so it is insufficient to define the concept for policy documents. The provider manual excerpt addresses missing member address, age, or CIN, not missing prescriber information, so it only partially answers the user’s question.

requiring cold storage. Pharmacies must have a purpose-built or pharmaceutical grade refrigerator with adequate
storage space with fan-forced air circulation; a dorm-style or bar-style unit with an evaporator plate/cooling coil
does not qualify.

Additionally, Medicaid providers who are part of the Vaccine for Children (VFC) program must meet criteria for
refrigeration and vaccine storage set forth in the guidelines outlined in the Stand-Alone Storage Unit Purchasing
Guidance and Temperature Monitoring Requirements documents.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

The newest policy source dated 2023-07-01 instead addresses a different issue (prior authorization for non-preferred drugs), so the provider manual is the more up-to-date source overall for this question and should receive more weight by default.

[New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4, 5 — 2025-10-01]
similarities: Both sources place responsibility on the pharmacist in prescription-processing workflows, but the policy excerpt provided is limited to prior authorization notification and does not address missing member information. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — 2023-07-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4, 5 — 2025-10-01]

Both sources concern pharmacist actions tied to prescription legitimacy or compliance, but only the provider manual excerpt gives a specific rule for missing member data. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — 2023-07-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4, 5 — 2025-10-01]

test_id_40.query_content: What responsibilities do pharmacists have when required patient or prescriber information is missing?
test_id_40.concept: pharmacist responsibilities when required patient or prescriber information is missing

policy.text:
A supervising pharmacist must adhere to the following
responsibilities:
• The supervising pharmacist is responsible for the proper conduct of the pharmacy.
• The supervising pharmacist oversees the practice of other employed pharmacists and ensures that
those unlicensed persons do not engage in unlawful activity.
• The supervising pharmacist must have an active role in the conduct of the pharmacy and not a passive
role.
• The supervising pharmacist may not assign their responsibility to others.
• The supervising pharmacist must be aware of all laws and regulations and must instruct and direct
others to ensure compliance.
• The supervising pharmacist must work full-time (at least 30 hours per week). If the establishment is
operating less than 30 hours per week, the supervising pharmacist must work the majority of the hours.
• If the supervising pharmacist is to be absent from a pharmacy at any time, the supervising pharmacist
must ensure another qualified pharmacist is present in their absence.
• The supervising pharmacist cannot supervise more than one pharmacy establishment at a time.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Reminder: Submitting for Prior Authorization of Non-Preferred Drugs
Prior authorization (PA) requests for non-preferred drugs must be submitted by the prescriber or by an
authorized agent of the prescriber. An authorized agent of the prescriber is considered an employee of the
prescribing practitioner and has access to the patient's medical records (i.e., nurse, medical assistant).
When presented with a prescription for a non-preferred drug that requires PA, the pharmacist must notify
the prescriber that PA is required and notify the patient once the prescriber has obtained the PA. If the
pharmacist is unable to reach the prescriber and a PA number has not yet been obtained by the prescriber,
the pharmacist may initiate a PA for a 72-hour emergency supply only.
Pharmacists are not authorized to submit for a PA nor enter into an agreement with a prescriber to
effectuate PAs and appeals on the prescriber’s behalf (except for the 72-hour emergency supply mentioned
above or when employed by a prescriber).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacists may work with the patient and contact their prescriber to either change the
prescription to another drug or supply or obtain a prior authorization (PA) following the transition fill.
New York State Medicaid Update Special Edition – Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition pg. 4

Transition fills will be identified in the eMedNY claim response using NCPDP field 548-6F, Approved
Message Code, with code “005” — Claim paid under the plan's transition benefit period, otherwise claim
would have rejected as PA is required.
Vaccines Administered by Pharmacists
As indicated in the Scope of Benefits document, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/
redesign/mrt2/pharmacy_transition/repository/docs/rx_scope_of_benefits.pdf, vaccines that are administered
by pharmacists are subject to the benefit transition and should be billed to NYRx. Policy and billing guidance
for vaccines can be found within the New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Program Pharmacists as
Immunizers Fact Sheet, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/docs/phar
_immun_fact.pdf.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
If the address, age, or CIN of the Medicaid member are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter any of these
items on the prescription if the information:

• Is otherwise readily available in the records of the pharmacy and the pharmacist knows the person who is
requesting that the prescription be filled, or
• The pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate.
Prescriptions written for controlled substances must meet the requirements of Article 33 of the Public Health Law. In
accordance with New York State Department of Health Codes, Rules, and Regulations Title 10, Part 80, pharmacists are
permitted to add or change only certain information on controlled substance prescriptions.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacies are not required to generate and keep a hard copy of electronic prescriptions and fiscal orders if they are
securely stored and maintained. When stored electronically, the electronic imaging of prescriptions and fiscal orders
that were e-prescribed must result in an exact reproduction of the original order and may be required to be
authenticated.
Telephone Orders
Prescribers may telephone prescriptions and fiscal orders for drugs directly to a pharmacy unless otherwise prohibited
by state or federal law or regulations. The pharmacist is responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the
prescriber's identity and validity of the prescription if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist.
• Telephoned non-controlled prescription drug orders are considered original.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The pharmacist is responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the
prescriber’s identity if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist. Electronic imaging of prescriptions and fiscal
orders must result in an exact reproduction of the original order and may be required to be authenticated.
Storage Requirements – Vaccine and Medication
Medicaid Pharmacy providers are required to follow Regulations set forth by the Commissioner of Education,
section 63.6(b)(3) and the CDC guidelines in the CDC Storage and Handling Toolkit for vaccines and medication
requiring cold storage. Pharmacies must have a purpose-built or pharmaceutical grade refrigerator with adequate
storage space with fan-forced air circulation; a dorm-style or bar-style unit with an evaporator plate/cooling coil
does not qualify.

Additionally, Medicaid providers who are part of the Vaccine for Children (VFC) program must meet criteria for
refrigeration and vaccine storage set forth in the guidelines outlined in the Stand-Alone Storage Unit Purchasing
Guidance and Temperature Monitoring Requirements documents.

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the pharmacist’s current guidance is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. The provider manual addresses missing member information on the prescription, while the newest policy source dated 2023-07-01 addresses a pharmacist’s duty to notify the prescriber when prior authorization is needed; neither source fully defines a pharmacist’s responsibilities for missing required patient or prescriber information.
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy evidence says that when a prescription for a non-preferred drug requires prior authorization, “the pharmacist must notify the prescr...” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — Jul 01, 2023]. The provided policy excerpts do not contain sufficient grounded evidence to define the pharmacist’s responsibilities when required patient or prescriber information is missing beyond that notification duty; “Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents.”
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance says that if “the address, age, or CIN of the Medicaid member are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter any of these items on the prescription” when the information “is otherwise readily available in the records of the pharmacy and the pharmacist knows the person who is requesting that the prescription be filled,” or when “The pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4-5 — Oct 01, 2025]. This is the only grounded provider-manual guidance provided on missing patient information; “Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for provider manual documents” for missing prescriber information specifically.
similarities: Both sources place a duty on the pharmacist to act when prescription-related information is incomplete, rather than ignoring the issue: the policy says “the pharmacist must notify the prescr...” when PA is required [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — Jul 01, 2023], and the provider manual says the pharmacist is “not required to enter” missing member information only under stated conditions [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4-5 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources are conditional and limited in scope, not blanket rules: the policy excerpt is limited to “non-preferred drugs” requiring prior authorization [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — Jul 01, 2023], while the provider manual excerpt is limited to missing “address, age, or CIN of the Medicaid member” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4-5 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The provider manual gives a specific rule for missing patient information, stating the pharmacist “is not required to enter” missing address, age, or CIN if the information is already available or the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied the prescription is legitimate [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4-5 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy excerpt does not provide a comparable rule for missing patient information [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — Jul 01, 2023].

The policy excerpt focuses on a communication duty tied to prior authorization—“the pharmacist must notify the prescr...” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — Jul 01, 2023]—whereas the provider manual focuses on when the pharmacist may proceed without entering certain missing member data [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4-5 — Oct 01, 2025].
answers.caveats: The provided policy excerpt is truncated (“notify the prescr...”), so the exact full instruction cannot be fully quoted from the supplied text. The provider manual excerpt addresses missing patient/member information, not missing prescriber information, so there is insufficient grounded evidence here to define the pharmacist’s responsibilities for missing prescriber information specifically.

test_id_41.query_content: Are weight loss drugs an excluded benefit?

policy.text:
This article serves as a reminder of those exclusions for both the FFS and MMC program areas, which are
as follows:
• agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain [additionally pursuant to
New York Code, Rules and Regulations (NYCRR) Title 18 §505.3(g)(3)];
• agents when used for the treatment of sexual dysfunction unless such agents are used to treat a
condition, other than sexual or erectile dysfunction, for which the agents have been approved by the
Food and Drug Administration (FDA) [additionally pursuant to New York Social Services Law §365-
a(4)(f)]; and
• agents when used for cosmetic use or hair growth (additionally pursuant to NYCRR Title 18
§505.2(l)(5)).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 6

• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as
per the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), located at: https://www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title
19/1927.htm, including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or for
cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYRx, the Medicaid Pharmacy Program
Preferred Drug List, located at: https://newyork.fhsc.com/downloads/providers/NYRx_PDP_PDL.pdf.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to
safety reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved or
compendia-supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient
additives to a commercially available product.
• Submitted compound claims may not include packaging materials or containers, syringes, or other
items utilized or necessary in the preparation or use of final compounded product.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
December 2020 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 9

• Compounds may not contain drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) including but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual
dysfunction or for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or
compendia-supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes or other non-active ingredient
additives to a commercially available product.
• Submitted compound claims may not include packaging materials or containers, syringes, or other
items utilized or necessary in the preparation of final compounded product.
• Compounds must be adjudicated with the appropriate and matching final compounded product route
code for the dosage form.
• Prepared compounds that mimic a commercial product must include on the prescription and in the
members medical chart documentation of the reason for compounding (i.e., sensitivity or
contraindication to dyes, preservatives, or fillers or lack of availability of a commercial product).

policy.text:
*Requires additional MEVS.
Denial code “722”: NDC DESI code is invalid.
NDC Excluded from State Plan Coverage
NYS Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State Department of Health
List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs, located at: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx (unless provided
by a facility which includes the cost of drugs in their all-inclusive rate). The following are examples of
drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by NYS Medicaid in accordance with policy and/or state or federal
legislation:
• Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2),
located at: https://www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title19/1927.htm.
• Drugs for the treatment of sexual dysfunction pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), located at: https://
www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title19/1927.htm, and SOS §365-a(4)(f), located at: https://www.
nysenate.gov/legislation/laws/SOS/365-A.
• Drugs indicated for cosmetic use or hair growth pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), located at: https://
www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title19/1927.htm.

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Refills of compounds must be specifically requested by the patient or patient´s designee before the item is
prepared and submitted for payment.
• Ingredients in a compound claim will only be reimbursed if available on the NYS Medicaid List of Reimbursable
Drugs.
• Ingredients will only be reimbursed if they are compendia-supported for the intended route of administration
and indication.
• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or
for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYS Medicaid Fee-for-Service Programs.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to safety
reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or compendia-
supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient additives to
a commercially available product.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Note: Reimbursable drugs are listed alphabetically in sections by Rx Type.
Drug Coverage Limitations
Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs
(unless provided by a facility which includes the cost of drugs in their all-inclusive rate). The following are examples of
drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by Medicaid in accordance with Policy and/or state or federal Legislation:
• Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs for the treatment of sexual dysfunction pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), and Social Services Law §365-a(4)(f).
• Drugs without a federal rebate agreement pursuant to SSA §1927(a).
• Drugs indicated for cosmetic use or hair growth pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs that are not approved as safe and effective by the FDA pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(4)(C).
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, weight-loss drugs are treated as non-reimbursable when used for excluded indications, and this source is more up to date overall than the newest policy source dated 2025-01-01, so it should receive more weight by default. The policy and provider manual both support that drugs used for weight loss are excluded from Medicaid coverage, subject to the specific wording in each source.
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy evidence says NYS Medicaid does not reimburse drugs used for weight loss: “The following are examples of drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by NYS Medicaid in accordance …” [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:13 — Jan 01, 2025]. Earlier policy guidance is more explicit that the exclusion includes “agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:1 — Apr 01, 2022].
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual evidence says Medicaid does not reimburse drugs used for excluded indications: “The following are examples of drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by Medicaid in accordance with Policy and/or state or federal Legislation: • Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, ...” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — Oct 01, 2025]. The same manual also states compounds may not be made for excluded indications, including “drugs to treat weight loss” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 — Oct 01, 2025].
similarities: Both sources treat weight-loss-related drug use as non-reimbursable/excluded: policy says drugs used for weight loss are “not reimbursable” [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:13 — Jan 01, 2025], and the provider manual says drugs used for excluded indications are “not reimbursable” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources also identify weight loss as an excluded indication in compound-related guidance: policy says compounds may not be made for excluded indications including “drugs to treat weight loss” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:6-7 — Apr 01, 2024], and the provider manual says compounds may not be made for excluded indications including “drugs to treat weight loss” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The provider manual is newer and more current for this issue: it is dated “Oct 01, 2025” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — Oct 01, 2025], while the newest policy source provided is dated “Jan 01, 2025” [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:13 — Jan 01, 2025].

The policy excerpt provided is less specific in the newest source, stating only that certain drugs/drug uses are “not reimbursable” without the full weight-loss example in that 2025 chunk [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:13 — Jan 01, 2025], whereas the provider manual explicitly ties non-reimbursability to excluded indications and separately names “drugs to treat weight loss” in the compound context [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28,34 — Oct 01, 2025].
answers.caveats: The newest policy chunk provided is truncated and does not fully display the weight-loss example in that excerpt, so the clearest policy wording comes from the older 2022 policy update [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:13 — Jan 01, 2025; New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:1 — Apr 01, 2022].
