• Drugs for the treatment of sexual dysfunction pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), located at: https://
www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title19/1927.htm, and SOS §365-a(4)(f), located at: https://www.
nysenate.gov/legislation/laws/SOS/365-A.
• Drugs indicated for cosmetic use or hair growth pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), located at: https://
www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title19/1927.htm.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Refills of compounds must be specifically requested by the patient or patient´s designee before the item is
prepared and submitted for payment.
• Ingredients in a compound claim will only be reimbursed if available on the NYS Medicaid List of Reimbursable
Drugs.
• Ingredients will only be reimbursed if they are compendia-supported for the intended route of administration
and indication.
• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or
for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYS Medicaid Fee-for-Service Programs.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to safety
reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or compendia-
supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient additives to
a commercially available product.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Note: Reimbursable drugs are listed alphabetically in sections by Rx Type.
Drug Coverage Limitations
Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs
(unless provided by a facility which includes the cost of drugs in their all-inclusive rate). The following are examples of
drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by Medicaid in accordance with Policy and/or state or federal Legislation:
• Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs for the treatment of sexual dysfunction pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), and Social Services Law §365-a(4)(f).
• Drugs without a federal rebate agreement pursuant to SSA §1927(a).
• Drugs indicated for cosmetic use or hair growth pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs that are not approved as safe and effective by the FDA pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(4)(C).
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, drugs used for weight loss are not reimbursable, and this provider manual is more up to date overall than the newest policy source dated 2025-01-01, so it should receive more weight by default. The policy sources are consistent with that exclusion, but the provider manual is the current controlling guidance for this issue. [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0114 — Oct 01, 2025]; [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:0047 — Jan 01, 2025]
answers.policy_definition: Policy documents state that drugs used for weight loss are excluded/non-reimbursable: the January 2025 Medicaid Update says, "The following are examples of drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by NYS Medicaid in accordance ..." [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:0047 — Jan 01, 2025], and the April 2022 Medicaid Update specifically lists "agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain" as exclusions [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:0001 — Apr 01, 2022]. The April 2024 Medicaid Update also says compounds may not be made for excluded indications, "including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:0017 — Apr 01, 2024].
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual says Medicaid reimburses only drugs on the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list and identifies non-reimbursable examples, including "Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, ..." [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0114 — Oct 01, 2025]. It also states that compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for excluded indications [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0139 — Oct 01, 2025]. Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for provider manual beyond those exclusions. [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0114 — Oct 01, 2025]; [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0139 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources treat weight-loss-related drugs/uses as excluded or non-reimbursable: policy says "agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain" are exclusions [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:0001 — Apr 01, 2022], and the provider manual lists non-reimbursable drugs/drug uses and compounds that may not contain excluded drugs [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0114 — Oct 01, 2025]; [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0139 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources tie the exclusion to Medicaid reimbursement limits rather than creating a separate covered benefit category: policy says such drugs/drug uses are "not reimbursable by NYS Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:0047 — Jan 01, 2025], and the provider manual says "Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs" [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0114 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The provider manual is newer and therefore more current for this issue: its relevant guidance is dated 2025-10-01 [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0114 — Oct 01, 2025], while the newest policy source provided is dated 2025-01-01 [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:0047 — Jan 01, 2025].

The policy excerpts are more explicit about weight loss by name, stating "weight loss or weight gain" and "drugs to treat weight loss" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:0001 — Apr 01, 2022]; [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:0017 — Apr 01, 2024], while the provider manual excerpt provided names "Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia" and then refers more generally to excluded drugs/indications [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0114 — Oct 01, 2025]; [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0139 — Oct 01, 2025].
answers.caveats: The January 2025 policy excerpt is truncated and does not show the full list item after "not reimbursable by NYS Medicaid in accordance ..." [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:0047 — Jan 01, 2025]. The provider manual excerpt provided is also truncated at the specific examples list, so the exact full wording for weight-loss drugs is not fully visible in the supplied chunk [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0114 — Oct 01, 2025].

policy.text:
This article serves as a reminder of those exclusions for both the FFS and MMC program areas, which are
as follows:
• agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain [additionally pursuant to
New York Code, Rules and Regulations (NYCRR) Title 18 §505.3(g)(3)];
• agents when used for the treatment of sexual dysfunction unless such agents are used to treat a
condition, other than sexual or erectile dysfunction, for which the agents have been approved by the
Food and Drug Administration (FDA) [additionally pursuant to New York Social Services Law §365-
a(4)(f)]; and
• agents when used for cosmetic use or hair growth (additionally pursuant to NYCRR Title 18
§505.2(l)(5)).

policy.text:
December 2020 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 9

• Compounds may not contain drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) including but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual
dysfunction or for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or
compendia-supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes or other non-active ingredient
additives to a commercially available product.
• Submitted compound claims may not include packaging materials or containers, syringes, or other
items utilized or necessary in the preparation of final compounded product.
• Compounds must be adjudicated with the appropriate and matching final compounded product route
code for the dosage form.
• Prepared compounds that mimic a commercial product must include on the prescription and in the
members medical chart documentation of the reason for compounding (i.e., sensitivity or
contraindication to dyes, preservatives, or fillers or lack of availability of a commercial product).

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. Both sources indicate that drugs used for weight loss are not reimbursable/excluded, so the answer is yes. [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0139 — Oct 01, 2025] [mu_no01_jan25_pr.pdf::0047 — Jan 01, 2025]
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy evidence says NYS Medicaid does not reimburse drugs used for weight loss: “The following are examples of drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by NYS Medicaid in accordance ...” and includes “drugs to treat weight loss” among excluded examples [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:13 — Jan 01, 2025]. Earlier policy also states exclusions include “agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:1 — Apr 01, 2022].
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual evidence states Medicaid only reimburses drugs on the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list and gives excluded examples, including “Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, ...” [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf:28 — Oct 01, 2025]. It also states compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for excluded indications, including “drugs to treat weight loss” [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf:34 — Oct 01, 2025].
similarities: Both sources treat weight-loss-related drugs as excluded/non-reimbursable: policy lists “drugs to treat weight loss” as not reimbursable, and the provider manual says compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for excluded indications, including “drugs to treat weight loss” [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:13 — Jan 01, 2025] [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf:34 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources tie coverage to the Medicaid reimbursable drugs framework rather than treating weight-loss drugs as covered by default: policy says NYS Medicaid only reimburses drugs included on the state list, and the provider manual says Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:13 — Jan 01, 2025] [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf:28 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The provider manual is newer and more current for this issue: it is dated 2025-10-01, while the newest policy chunk is dated 2025-01-01 [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf:34 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:13 — Jan 01, 2025].

The policy excerpt states the exclusion directly for “drugs to treat weight loss,” while the provider manual excerpt frames the rule through compound/excluded-indication language and a general non-reimbursable examples list that includes “Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, ...” [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:13 — Jan 01, 2025] [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf:28,34 — Oct 01, 2025].
answers.caveats: The provider manual excerpt available here is partly truncated, so the exact full wording of the excluded-drug examples list is not fully visible; however, the grounded text still clearly includes weight-loss-related exclusions in the compound section [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf:34 — Oct 01, 2025].

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, Medicaid does not reimburse drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain, so the provider manual is the more up-to-date guidance and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2025-01-01 is consistent in excluding drugs to treat weight loss, so the answer is yes: weight loss drugs are an excluded/nonreimbursable benefit under both sources. [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf:28 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:13 — Jan 01, 2025]
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy evidence states that NYS Medicaid does not reimburse drugs/drug uses that are excluded from state plan coverage, and the examples include “drugs to treat weight loss.” [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:13 — Jan 01, 2025] Earlier policy also states that “agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain” are excluded for both FFS and MMC. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:1 — Apr 01, 2022]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual states that Medicaid only reimburses drugs on the NYS List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs, and lists as nonreimbursable examples “Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, ...” [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf:28 — Oct 01, 2025] The same manual also states that compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications, including excluded indications referenced under the Social Security Act. [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf:34 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources exclude weight-loss-related drug use from Medicaid reimbursement: policy says “drugs to treat weight loss” are not reimbursable, and the provider manual lists “Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, ...” among nonreimbursable examples. [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:13 — Jan 01, 2025] [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf:28 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources tie the exclusion to broader excluded-drug / excluded-indication rules rather than creating a separate special coverage category. [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:13 — Jan 01, 2025] [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf:34 — Oct 01, 2025]
differences: The provider manual is newer (2025-10-01) than the newest policy source (2025-01-01), so it should guide current operational use by default. [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf:28 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:13 — Jan 01, 2025]

The policy excerpt expressly names “weight loss” as an excluded use, while the provider manual excerpt provided here names “anorexia” and does not, in the quoted line, explicitly repeat “weight loss.” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:1 — Apr 01, 2022] [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf:28 — Oct 01, 2025]
answers.caveats: The provider manual excerpt available here is truncated in the quoted line, so the exact full list of examples in that section is not fully visible; however, the available evidence is sufficient to conclude that weight-loss-related drugs are excluded/nonreimbursable under the policy excerpts and the provider manual’s excluded-drug framework. [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf:28 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:13 — Jan 01, 2025]

test_id_45.query_content: Are weight loss drugs an excluded benefit?
test_id_45.concept: weight loss drugs excluded benefit status

policy.text:
This article serves as a reminder of those exclusions for both the FFS and MMC program areas, which are
as follows:
• agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain [additionally pursuant to
New York Code, Rules and Regulations (NYCRR) Title 18 §505.3(g)(3)];
• agents when used for the treatment of sexual dysfunction unless such agents are used to treat a
condition, other than sexual or erectile dysfunction, for which the agents have been approved by the
Food and Drug Administration (FDA) [additionally pursuant to New York Social Services Law §365-
a(4)(f)]; and
• agents when used for cosmetic use or hair growth (additionally pursuant to NYCRR Title 18
§505.2(l)(5)).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 6

• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as
per the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), located at: https://www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title
19/1927.htm, including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or for
cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYRx, the Medicaid Pharmacy Program
Preferred Drug List, located at: https://newyork.fhsc.com/downloads/providers/NYRx_PDP_PDL.pdf.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to
safety reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved or
compendia-supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient
additives to a commercially available product.
• Submitted compound claims may not include packaging materials or containers, syringes, or other
items utilized or necessary in the preparation or use of final compounded product.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
December 2020 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 9

• Compounds may not contain drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) including but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual
dysfunction or for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or
compendia-supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes or other non-active ingredient
additives to a commercially available product.
• Submitted compound claims may not include packaging materials or containers, syringes, or other
items utilized or necessary in the preparation of final compounded product.
• Compounds must be adjudicated with the appropriate and matching final compounded product route
code for the dosage form.
• Prepared compounds that mimic a commercial product must include on the prescription and in the
members medical chart documentation of the reason for compounding (i.e., sensitivity or
contraindication to dyes, preservatives, or fillers or lack of availability of a commercial product).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update December 2020 Volume 36 Number 18
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
*Requires additional MEVS.
Denial code “722”: NDC DESI code is invalid.
NDC Excluded from State Plan Coverage
NYS Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State Department of Health
List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs, located at: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx (unless provided
by a facility which includes the cost of drugs in their all-inclusive rate). The following are examples of
drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by NYS Medicaid in accordance with policy and/or state or federal
legislation:
• Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2),
located at: https://www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title19/1927.htm.
• Drugs for the treatment of sexual dysfunction pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), located at: https://
www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title19/1927.htm, and SOS §365-a(4)(f), located at: https://www.
nysenate.gov/legislation/laws/SOS/365-A.
• Drugs indicated for cosmetic use or hair growth pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), located at: https://
www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title19/1927.htm.

• Refills of compounds must be specifically requested by the patient or patient´s designee before the item is
prepared and submitted for payment.
• Ingredients in a compound claim will only be reimbursed if available on the NYS Medicaid List of Reimbursable
Drugs.
• Ingredients will only be reimbursed if they are compendia-supported for the intended route of administration
and indication.
• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or
for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYS Medicaid Fee-for-Service Programs.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to safety
reasons.

supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient additives to
a commercially available product.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Note: Reimbursable drugs are listed alphabetically in sections by Rx Type.
Drug Coverage Limitations
Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs
(unless provided by a facility which includes the cost of drugs in their all-inclusive rate). The following are examples of
drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by Medicaid in accordance with Policy and/or state or federal Legislation:
• Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs for the treatment of sexual dysfunction pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), and Social Services Law §365-a(4)(f).
• Drugs without a federal rebate agreement pursuant to SSA §1927(a).
• Drugs indicated for cosmetic use or hair growth pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs that are not approved as safe and effective by the FDA pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(4)(C).
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, NYS Medicaid says drugs used for weight loss are not reimbursable, so the provider manual should receive more weight by default for current guidance. The newest policy source dated 2025-01-01 is consistent and also treats drugs used for weight loss as excluded. [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0139 — Oct 01, 2025]; [mu_no01_jan25_pr.pdf::0047 — Jan 01, 2025]
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy evidence says NYS Medicaid does not reimburse drugs used for weight loss: "The following are examples of drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by NYS Medicaid in accordance ..." [mu_no01_jan25_pr.pdf::0047 — Jan 01, 2025]. Earlier policy guidance states the exclusions include "agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain" [mu_no4_apr22_pr.pdf::0001 — Apr 01, 2022].
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance says Medicaid does not reimburse drugs used for weight loss: "Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs ... The following are examples of drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by Medicaid in accordance with Policy and/or state or federal Legislation: • Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, ..." [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0114 — Oct 01, 2025]. The companion compound guidance also says compounds may not be made for excluded indications, including "drugs to treat weight loss" [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0139 — Oct 01, 2025].
similarities: Both sources treat weight loss drugs as not reimbursable/excluded: policy says "agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain" [mu_no4_apr22_pr.pdf::0001 — Apr 01, 2022]; provider manual says "Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, ..." are not reimbursable [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0114 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources tie the exclusion to Medicaid reimbursement limits rather than creating a separate covered category: policy says the exclusions are "for both the FFS and MMC program areas" [mu_no4_apr22_pr.pdf::0001 — Apr 01, 2022]; provider manual says "Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs" [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0114 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The provider manual is newer and more current for this issue: it is dated "2025-10-01" [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0114 — Oct 01, 2025], while the newest policy excerpt is dated "2025-01-01" [mu_no01_jan25_pr.pdf::0047 — Jan 01, 2025].

The provider manual includes compound-specific language that weight-loss exclusions apply to compounds: "Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications ... including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss" [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0139 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy excerpts provided do not include that compound-specific detail [mu_no01_jan25_pr.pdf::0047 — Jan 01, 2025].
answers.caveats: The January 2025 policy excerpt is truncated and does not show the full sentence after "not reimbursable by NYS Medicaid in accordance ..." [mu_no01_jan25_pr.pdf::0047 — Jan 01, 2025]. The conclusion that weight loss drugs are excluded is still grounded because both the older policy and the newer provider manual expressly state weight-loss treatment is excluded [mu_no4_apr22_pr.pdf::0001 — Apr 01, 2022]; [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0114 — Oct 01, 2025].

policy.text:
The information in this article is intended to advise
providers, who may be new to submitting claims to NYRx, the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy program, that
all NCPDP-submitted 340B-purchased drug claims when NYS Medicaid is primary payor must be
submitted with claim level identifiers and at actual acquisition cost.
Claim level identifiers are required for any 340B-purchased drug billed to NYRx, Medicaid Managed Care
(MMC) Plan, or as a secondary claim, whether it is a medical or pharmacy claim. It is the responsibility of the
covered entity (CE) and their contracted pharmacies, if applicable, to correctly identify claims dispensed with
the 340B stock of the CE for 340B-eligible NYS Medicaid patients to ensure rebates are not collected for
these drugs. If rebates are received by the NYS DOH for drugs obtained via the 340B program due to
incorrect or missing claim level identifiers, the CE is responsible for reimbursing manufacturers the
340B discounts.
The following table outlines the necessary fields and their values for various claim types to identify 340B-
purchased drug claims.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

(CE) and their contracted pharmacies, if applicable, to correctly identify claims dispensed with the 340B stock

policy.text:
4

Pharmacy
Reminder: 340B Providers, Covered Entities, and Contract Pharmacies
A review of 340B claims submitted via the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0 format
indicates confusion as to which field should contain the ingredient cost and which should contain the Usual and
Customary (U&C). 340B drug claims need to be properly identified as 340B as well as submitted at costs in the
appropriate fields. Claims submitted with incorrect data, including cost in the wrong field, are subject to
audit and recovery.
The following fields are required on each Medicaid 340B drug claim submitted NCPDP format:
Medicaid Primary Medicaid Secondary Claim
Field
Claim (Primary: Medicare; Commercial)
420-DK, Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20 20
423-DN, Basis of Cost Determination (BCD) 08 Not applicable
409-D9, Ingredient Cost Submitted * 340B Acquisition
Not applicable
Cost
426-DQ, Usual and Customary Cost (U&C) * Lowest Net Charge Lowest Net Charge to Cash
to Cash Customers Customers
*MMC plans should be consulted on their requirements for this field.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2019 Volume 35 Number 6
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
National Drug Code Reporting Requirements
Providers are required to report the National Drug Code (NDC) for all PADs billed by a private practitioner to
NYS Medicaid and for all ordered ambulatory claims billed by clinics. If the NDC is not referenced on a claim,
NYS Medicaid will not provide reimbursement for the drug.
Drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line
on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims. Providers are required to report the NDC on FFS claims for the
reimbursement of 340B drugs. Providers must also include the number of units administered and their actual
acquisition cost, by invoice, in the charges field of a claim. Please note: Medicare requires either the JG or
TB modifiers be appended to the 340B drug line for Medicare/NYS Medicaid crossover claims (no UD modifier
is needed on crossover claims).
An accurate NDC must be reported for all PADs billed to NYS Medicaid FFS on an institutional claim that
uses Ambulatory Patient Groups (APGs) payment methodology.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.

provider_manual.text:
6.0 Definitions
The following terms are defined for the purposes of the NY Medicaid program and are included to help clarify policies
as provided in this Manual:
340B Ceiling Price
The 340B ceiling price is statutorily defined as the Average Manufacturer Price (AMP) reduced by the rebate
percentage, which is commonly referred to as the Unit Rebate Amount (URA). HRSA obtains the AMP and URA data
from the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) as part of quarterly reporting for the Medicaid Drug
Rebate Program. This figure is then multiplied by the package size and case package size to produce a price that is
used in the marketplace for purchasing covered outpatient drugs. For example, the AMP minus the URA indicates the
cost of one pill.
Actual Acquisition Cost
Refers to the price paid for a drug or supply by the provider or in the case of a Covered Agency for a 340B purchased
drug, from a supplier with no added costs, delivery fees or dispensing fees, or means the agency's determination of the
pharmacy providers' actual prices paid to acquire drug products marketed or sold by specific manufacturers.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, 340B drug claims are governed by federal duplicate-discount rules and HRSA requirements for covered entities using 340B drugs for Medicaid members, so the provider manual should receive more weight by default for current guidance. The newest policy source dated 2023-06-01 says 340B-purchased drug claims must be submitted with claim-level identifiers and at actual acquisition cost when NYS Medicaid is primary payor, with claim-level identifiers also required for 340B-purchased drugs billed to NYRx, MMC, or as secondary claims.
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy guidance says that “all NCPDP-submitted 340B-purchased drug claims when NYS Medicaid is primary payor must be submitted with claim level identifiers and at actual acquisition cost” and that “Claim level identifiers are required for any 340B-purchased drug billed to NYRx, Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) Plan, or as a secondary claim, whether it is a medical or pharmacy claim” [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:6 — Jun 01, 2023]. Earlier policy also says the CE and contracted pharmacies must “correctly identify claims dispensed with the 340B stock of the CE for 340B-eligible Medicaid patients to ensure rebates are not collected for these drugs,” and if rebates are received due to “incorrect or missing claim level identifiers,” the CE must reimburse manufacturers the 340B discounts [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:14 — Nov 01, 2021].
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance says that “340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for their Medicaid patients,” and that “Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — Oct 01, 2025]. It also states that HRSA has “rules and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — Oct 01, 2025].
similarities: Both sources require 340B claims to be handled in a way that prevents duplicate discounts or rebates for the same drug: policy says claims must be identified so “rebates are not collected for these drugs,” and the manual says “Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts” [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:14 — Nov 01, 2021; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources tie 340B reimbursement/claim handling to Medicaid billing rules for covered entities using 340B drugs for Medicaid patients or members [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:6 — Jun 01, 2023; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The policy source gives specific reimbursement mechanics: 340B-purchased drug claims must be submitted “at actual acquisition cost” and with “claim level identifiers” [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:6 — Jun 01, 2023], while the provider manual excerpt does not state the reimbursement amount or claim-level billing method [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — Oct 01, 2025].

The provider manual excerpt is broader and more general, focusing on covered entities deciding whether to use 340B drugs for Medicaid patients and on federal duplicate-discount/HRSA requirements, whereas the policy excerpt is operational and billing-specific [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — Oct 01, 2025; New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:6 — Jun 01, 2023].
answers.caveats: The provider manual excerpt provided does not include the full operational reimbursement rule for 340B drug claims, so the policy excerpt supplies the clearest grounded instruction on how they are reimbursed. Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for some older policy details beyond claim identification and actual acquisition cost.

policy.text:
National Drug Code Reporting Requirements
Providers are required to report the National Drug Code (NDC) for all PADs billed by a private practitioner to
NYS Medicaid and for all ordered ambulatory claims billed by clinics. If the NDC is not referenced on a claim,
NYS Medicaid will not provide reimbursement for the drug.
Drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line
on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims. Providers are required to report the NDC on FFS claims for the
reimbursement of 340B drugs. Providers must also include the number of units administered and their actual
acquisition cost, by invoice, in the charges field of a claim. Please note: Medicare requires either the JG or
TB modifiers be appended to the 340B drug line for Medicare/NYS Medicaid crossover claims (no UD modifier
is needed on crossover claims).
An accurate NDC must be reported for all PADs billed to NYS Medicaid FFS on an institutional claim that
uses Ambulatory Patient Groups (APGs) payment methodology.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
November 2021 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 14

Physician-Administered Drugs (837P/837I)
An accurate National Drug Code (NDC) and “UD” modifier must be reported for any physician-administered
drug obtained at the 340B price that are billed on the Institutional claim form for either FFS or MMC. A
physician-administered drug will not be paid under Ambulatory Patient Groups (APGs) if the claim does not
include an accurate NDC. Please note: Any APGs fee schedule drug will still require a provider to code the
number of units and the acquisition cost for the claim line to be paid.
Non-Drug Items
As communicated in the December 2016 issue of the Medicaid Update cited and linked in the beginning of
this article, 340B claim level identifiers are required on all 340B purchased drug claims for Medicaid members.
Pharmacies should not submit claim level identifiers on non-340B eligible items, such as test strips. Diabetic
test strips are not covered outpatient drugs and are not part of the Medicaid Drug Rebate Program (MDRP),
and therefore are not eligible for a 340B discount. Only 340B purchased drugs should be tagged when claim
eligible with the 340B claim level identifiers.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Effective September 12, 2019, for Medicaid FFS primary claims only, system editing will compare the
ingredient cost submitted (NCPDP field 409-DK) with the 340B ceiling price for the product, as defined by
Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA). The 340B ceiling price refers to the maximum amount
that a manufacturer can charge a covered entity for the purchase of a 340B covered outpatient drug.
The 340B ceiling price is statutorily defined as the Average Manufacturer Price (AMP) reduced by the rebate
percentage, which is commonly referred to as the Unit Rebate Amount (URA). HRSA obtains the AMP and URA
data from the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) as part of quarterly reporting for the Medicaid
Drug Rebate Program. This figure is then multiplied by the package size and case package size to produce a
price that is used in the marketplace for purchasing covered outpatient drugs. For example, the AMP minus the
URA indicates the cost of one pill.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
It is the responsibility of the covered entity
(CE) and their contracted pharmacies, if applicable, to correctly identify claims dispensed with the 340B stock
of the CE for 340B-eligible Medicaid patients to ensure rebates are not collected for these drugs. If rebates
are received by the NYS Department of Health (DOH) for drugs obtained via the 340B program due to
incorrect or missing claim level identifiers, the CE is responsible for reimbursing manufacturers the
340B discounts.
The following table outlines the necessary fields and their values for various claim types to identify 340B-
purchased drug claims. Please note: 340B claims billed to Medicaid FFS as secondary claims also
require identifiers, as well as 340B claims billed to MMC Plans.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The pricing methodology is systematically determined as follows:
Drug Type If NADAC is available If NADAC is unavailable, Professional Dispensing Fee*
reimburse at: reimburse at:
Lower of NADAC, FUL, Lower of WAC – 17.5%,
Generics SMAC or U&C FUL, SMAC, or U&C $10.18
Lower of NADAC or

Lower of NADAC, FUL,
OTCs SMAC or U&C Lower of WAC, FUL, $10.18
SMAC, or U&C

dispensing fee.
* Professional Dispensing Fee applies if the drug meets the definition of Covered Outpatient Drug and is not paid at
U&C.
**When claim is eligible for submission as a 340B purchased drug claim. See subsection 340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
below for more information.
Federal Upper Limit (FUL) is determined by the Secretary of Health and Human Services, a price ceiling used by Centers
for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) to control prices for certain medications paid to pharmacies.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
These orders must be
kept on file at the pharmacy.
• If billing for both the drug and administration, these claims must be submitted separately.
• For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field. The
pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using
reimbursement methodology.
• For the administration, the claim must be submitted using procedure code 96372, and the pharmacy’s NPI
in the Prescriber ID field. A pharmacy will be reimbursed the current administration fee.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, 340B drugs are reimbursed at “340B Acquisition Cost” for 340B purchased drugs, and the provider manual should guide current billing guidance by default. The newest policy source dated 2022-07-01 is older and says 340B drugs must be identified with the UD modifier on NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims, so it is background unless you need the earlier policy framing.
answers.policy_definition: Policy documents say drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims, and if the NDC is not referenced on a claim, NYS Medicaid will not provide reimbursement for the drug. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — Jul 01, 2022] Earlier policy also required an accurate NDC and “UD” modifier for physician-administered drugs obtained at the 340B price, and stated a physician-administered drug will not be paid under APGs if the claim does not include an accurate NDC. [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:14-15 — Nov 01, 2021]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The provider manual states that 340B drugs are reimbursed using “340B Acquisition Cost” for “340B**Purchased” drugs, with the pricing methodology table listing “340B Acquisition Cost” as the reimbursement amount. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — Oct 01, 2025] It also says 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for Medicaid patients and that federal law prohibits duplicate discounts. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources tie 340B drug billing/reimbursement to correct claim identification and compliance controls: the policy requires the UD modifier and NDC reporting, while the provider manual addresses 340B program use and duplicate-discount prevention. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — Jul 01, 2022] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources indicate that 340B drugs are not treated like ordinary claims without special handling; the policy says reimbursement is withheld if the NDC is missing, and the provider manual assigns a specific 340B reimbursement methodology. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — Jul 01, 2022] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — Oct 01, 2025]
differences: The policy documents focus on claim-level identifiers and payment conditions, stating that 340B drugs “must be identified by appending the UD modifier” and that missing NDCs mean no reimbursement. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — Jul 01, 2022] The provider manual instead states the reimbursement amount/method as “340B Acquisition Cost” for 340B purchased drugs. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — Oct 01, 2025]

The policy excerpts do not provide a reimbursement rate or acquisition-cost methodology for 340B drugs, while the provider manual does. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — Jul 01, 2022] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — Oct 01, 2025]
answers.caveats: The provided policy excerpts do not fully define the reimbursement methodology for 340B drugs, so the policy-side answer is limited to identification and payment conditions. The provider manual is newer and should receive more weight for current guidance, but the exact operational billing rules may still depend on claim type and other manual sections not included here.

policy.text:
National Drug Code Reporting Requirements
Providers are required to report the National Drug Code (NDC) for all PADs billed by a private practitioner to
NYS Medicaid and for all ordered ambulatory claims billed by clinics. If the NDC is not referenced on a claim,
NYS Medicaid will not provide reimbursement for the drug.
Drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line
on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims. Providers are required to report the NDC on FFS claims for the
reimbursement of 340B drugs. Providers must also include the number of units administered and their actual
acquisition cost, by invoice, in the charges field of a claim. Please note: Medicare requires either the JG or
TB modifiers be appended to the 340B drug line for Medicare/NYS Medicaid crossover claims (no UD modifier
is needed on crossover claims).
An accurate NDC must be reported for all PADs billed to NYS Medicaid FFS on an institutional claim that
uses Ambulatory Patient Groups (APGs) payment methodology.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Effective September 12, 2019, for Medicaid FFS primary claims only, system editing will compare the
ingredient cost submitted (NCPDP field 409-DK) with the 340B ceiling price for the product, as defined by
Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA). The 340B ceiling price refers to the maximum amount
that a manufacturer can charge a covered entity for the purchase of a 340B covered outpatient drug.
The 340B ceiling price is statutorily defined as the Average Manufacturer Price (AMP) reduced by the rebate
percentage, which is commonly referred to as the Unit Rebate Amount (URA). HRSA obtains the AMP and URA
data from the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) as part of quarterly reporting for the Medicaid
Drug Rebate Program. This figure is then multiplied by the package size and case package size to produce a
price that is used in the marketplace for purchasing covered outpatient drugs. For example, the AMP minus the
URA indicates the cost of one pill.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
November 2021 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 14

Physician-Administered Drugs (837P/837I)
An accurate National Drug Code (NDC) and “UD” modifier must be reported for any physician-administered
drug obtained at the 340B price that are billed on the Institutional claim form for either FFS or MMC. A
physician-administered drug will not be paid under Ambulatory Patient Groups (APGs) if the claim does not
include an accurate NDC. Please note: Any APGs fee schedule drug will still require a provider to code the
number of units and the acquisition cost for the claim line to be paid.
Non-Drug Items
As communicated in the December 2016 issue of the Medicaid Update cited and linked in the beginning of
this article, 340B claim level identifiers are required on all 340B purchased drug claims for Medicaid members.
Pharmacies should not submit claim level identifiers on non-340B eligible items, such as test strips. Diabetic
test strips are not covered outpatient drugs and are not part of the Medicaid Drug Rebate Program (MDRP),
and therefore are not eligible for a 340B discount. Only 340B purchased drugs should be tagged when claim
eligible with the 340B claim level identifiers.

provider_manual.text:
6.0 Definitions
The following terms are defined for the purposes of the NY Medicaid program and are included to help clarify policies
as provided in this Manual:
340B Ceiling Price
The 340B ceiling price is statutorily defined as the Average Manufacturer Price (AMP) reduced by the rebate
percentage, which is commonly referred to as the Unit Rebate Amount (URA). HRSA obtains the AMP and URA data
from the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) as part of quarterly reporting for the Medicaid Drug
Rebate Program. This figure is then multiplied by the package size and case package size to produce a price that is
used in the marketplace for purchasing covered outpatient drugs. For example, the AMP minus the URA indicates the
cost of one pill.
Actual Acquisition Cost
Refers to the price paid for a drug or supply by the provider or in the case of a Covered Agency for a 340B purchased
drug, from a supplier with no added costs, delivery fees or dispensing fees, or means the agency's determination of the
pharmacy providers' actual prices paid to acquire drug products marketed or sold by specific manufacturers.

Lower of NADAC or

Lower of NADAC, FUL,

SMAC, or U&C

dispensing fee.
* Professional Dispensing Fee applies if the drug meets the definition of Covered Outpatient Drug and is not paid at
U&C.
**When claim is eligible for submission as a 340B purchased drug claim. See subsection 340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
below for more information.
Federal Upper Limit (FUL) is determined by the Secretary of Health and Human Services, a price ceiling used by Centers
for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) to control prices for certain medications paid to pharmacies.

answers.policy_definition: The newest policy excerpt requires identification, not a payment rate: “Drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims.” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — Jul 01, 2022] The older policy excerpt explains that for Medicaid FFS primary claims only, “system editing will compare the ingredient cost submitted (NCPDP field 409-DK) with the 340B ceiling price for the product” and defines the 340B ceiling price as “the maximum amount that a manufacturer can charge a covered entity for the purchase of a 340B covered outpatient drug.” [New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:6 — Aug 01, 2019]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual excerpt states the reimbursement methodology directly: “340B**Purchased 340B Acquisition Cost 340B Acquisition Cost $10.18” and indicates that 340B drugs are reimbursed at “340B Acquisition Cost” for that drug type. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — Oct 01, 2025] The manual also defines the 340B ceiling price as “the Average Manufacturer Price (AMP) reduced by the rebate percentage, which is commonly referred to as the Unit Rebate Amount (URA).” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:44 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources tie 340B handling to special claim identification/processing rather than ordinary drug billing: the policy says 340B drugs “must be identified by appending the UD modifier” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — Jul 01, 2022], and the manual includes a distinct 340B reimbursement category “340B**Purchased” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources reference the 340B price framework: the policy says claims are compared with “the 340B ceiling price” [New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:6 — Aug 01, 2019], and the manual defines the “340B ceiling price” as AMP reduced by URA [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:44 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The provider manual gives the reimbursement amount/method: “340B**Purchased 340B Acquisition Cost 340B Acquisition Cost $10.18” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy excerpt provided does not state a reimbursement rate and instead only requires the UD modifier on 340B drug lines [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — Jul 01, 2022].

The policy excerpt focuses on claim editing and ceiling-price comparison—“system editing will compare the ingredient cost submitted ... with the 340B ceiling price” [New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:6 — Aug 01, 2019]—whereas the provider manual excerpt states the actual reimbursement methodology for 340B purchased drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — Oct 01, 2025].
answers.caveats: The provided policy excerpts do not contain a direct reimbursement formula for 340B drugs, so the policy-side answer is limited to identification and ceiling-price comparison language. The provider manual is newer (2025-10-01) than the newest policy excerpt (2022-07-01), so it should guide current reimbursement questions unless the user needs historical policy context.

policy.text:
National Drug Code Reporting Requirements
Providers are required to report the National Drug Code (NDC) for all PADs billed by a private practitioner to
NYS Medicaid and for all ordered ambulatory claims billed by clinics. If the NDC is not referenced on a claim,

on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims. Providers are required to report the NDC on FFS claims for the
reimbursement of 340B drugs. Providers must also include the number of units administered and their actual
acquisition cost, by invoice, in the charges field of a claim. Please note: Medicare requires either the JG or
TB modifiers be appended to the 340B drug line for Medicare/NYS Medicaid crossover claims (no UD modifier
is needed on crossover claims).
An accurate NDC must be reported for all PADs billed to NYS Medicaid FFS on an institutional claim that
uses Ambulatory Patient Groups (APGs) payment methodology.

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
The information in this article is intended to advise
providers, who may be new to submitting claims to NYRx, the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy program, that
all NCPDP-submitted 340B-purchased drug claims when NYS Medicaid is primary payor must be
submitted with claim level identifiers and at actual acquisition cost.
Claim level identifiers are required for any 340B-purchased drug billed to NYRx, Medicaid Managed Care
(MMC) Plan, or as a secondary claim, whether it is a medical or pharmacy claim. It is the responsibility of the
covered entity (CE) and their contracted pharmacies, if applicable, to correctly identify claims dispensed with
the 340B stock of the CE for 340B-eligible NYS Medicaid patients to ensure rebates are not collected for
these drugs. If rebates are received by the NYS DOH for drugs obtained via the 340B program due to
incorrect or missing claim level identifiers, the CE is responsible for reimbursing manufacturers the
340B discounts.
The following table outlines the necessary fields and their values for various claim types to identify 340B-
purchased drug claims.

The 340B ceiling price is statutorily defined as the Average Manufacturer Price (AMP) reduced by the rebate
percentage, which is commonly referred to as the Unit Rebate Amount (URA). HRSA obtains the AMP and URA
data from the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) as part of quarterly reporting for the Medicaid

policy.text:
June 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Non-Drug Items
As communicated in the NYS Medicaid Change in 340B Claim Identification Effective 04/01/2017 article
published in the December 2016 issue of the Medicaid Update, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health
_care/medicaid/program/update/2016/dec16_mu.pdf, 340B claim level identifiers are required on all 340B-
purchased drug claims for NYS Medicaid members. Pharmacies should not submit claim level identifiers on
non-340B eligible items such as test strips. Diabetic test strips are not covered outpatient drugs and are not
part of the Medicaid Drug Rebate Program (MDRP) and, therefore, are not eligible for a 340B discount. Only
340B-purchased drugs should be tagged when claim-eligible with the 340B claim level identifiers. Tagging
test strips, supplies, or any other non-340B eligible item as a 340B drug causes a false claim, which may be
recovered during an audit.

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, 340B drugs are reimbursed at 340B Acquisition Cost for 340B-purchased claims, so the provider manual should receive more weight by default for current guidance. The newest policy source dated 2023-06-01 is consistent in requiring 340B-purchased drug claims to be submitted with claim-level identifiers and actual acquisition cost, but it is older than the provider manual.
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy guidance says that “all NCPDP-submitted 340B-purchased drug claims when NYS Medicaid is primary payor must be submitted with claim level identifiers and at actual acquisition cost” and that “Claim level identifiers are required for any 340B-purchased drug billed to NYRx, Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) Plan, or as a secondary claim, whether it is a medical or pharmacy claim.” [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:6 — Jun 01, 2023] Earlier policy also states that “Drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims.” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — Jul 01, 2022]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance states that “340B**Purchased 340B Acquisition Cost 340B Acquisition Cost $10.18”” in the reimbursement methodology table, indicating 340B-purchased drugs are reimbursed at 340B Acquisition Cost. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — Oct 01, 2025] The manual also explains that “Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources require special 340B claim identification rather than treating 340B drugs like ordinary claims: policy says 340B-purchased claims “must be submitted with claim level identifiers” and “identified by appending the UD modifier,” while the manual addresses “340B Pharmacy Drug Claims” and HRSA-related 340B requirements. [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:6 — Jun 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — Jul 01, 2022] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources tie 340B reimbursement to acquisition-cost concepts rather than standard drug pricing: policy requires claims “at actual acquisition cost,” and the manual lists “340B Acquisition Cost” as the reimbursement amount for “340B**Purchased” drugs. [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:6 — Jun 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — Oct 01, 2025]
differences: The provider manual is more explicit about the reimbursement amount, stating 340B-purchased drugs reimburse at “340B Acquisition Cost,” while the policy excerpt focuses on submission requirements and says claims must be “at actual acquisition cost” without the reimbursement table detail. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11:6 — Jun 01, 2023]

The policy excerpts include a specific FFS-only UD-modifier instruction for 340B drugs, stating they “must be identified by appending the UD modifier,” while the provider manual excerpt provided here does not include that modifier instruction in the quoted text. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — Jul 01, 2022] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — Oct 01, 2025]
answers.caveats: The provided provider manual excerpt is the more recent source overall and should guide current reimbursement interpretation, but the policy excerpts still add claim-submission requirements. Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for any provider-manual detail beyond reimbursement at 340B Acquisition Cost and the duplicate-discount statement.

policy.text:
It is the responsibility of the covered entity
(CE) and their contracted pharmacies, if applicable, to correctly identify claims dispensed with the 340B stock
of the CE for 340B-eligible Medicaid patients to ensure rebates are not collected for these drugs. If rebates
are received by the NYS Department of Health (DOH) for drugs obtained via the 340B program due to
incorrect or missing claim level identifiers, the CE is responsible for reimbursing manufacturers the
340B discounts.
The following table outlines the necessary fields and their values for various claim types to identify 340B-
purchased drug claims. Please note: 340B claims billed to Medicaid FFS as secondary claims also
require identifiers, as well as 340B claims billed to MMC Plans.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
National Drug Code Reporting Requirements
Providers are required to report the National Drug Code (NDC) for all PADs billed by a private practitioner to
NYS Medicaid and for all ordered ambulatory claims billed by clinics. If the NDC is not referenced on a claim,
NYS Medicaid will not provide reimbursement for the drug.
Drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line
on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims. Providers are required to report the NDC on FFS claims for the
reimbursement of 340B drugs. Providers must also include the number of units administered and their actual
acquisition cost, by invoice, in the charges field of a claim. Please note: Medicare requires either the JG or
TB modifiers be appended to the 340B drug line for Medicare/NYS Medicaid crossover claims (no UD modifier
is needed on crossover claims).
An accurate NDC must be reported for all PADs billed to NYS Medicaid FFS on an institutional claim that
uses Ambulatory Patient Groups (APGs) payment methodology.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
The information in this article is intended to advise
providers, who may be new to submitting claims to NYRx, the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy program, that
all NCPDP-submitted 340B-purchased drug claims when NYS Medicaid is primary payor must be
submitted with claim level identifiers and at actual acquisition cost.
Claim level identifiers are required for any 340B-purchased drug billed to NYRx, Medicaid Managed Care
(MMC) Plan, or as a secondary claim, whether it is a medical or pharmacy claim. It is the responsibility of the
covered entity (CE) and their contracted pharmacies, if applicable, to correctly identify claims dispensed with
the 340B stock of the CE for 340B-eligible NYS Medicaid patients to ensure rebates are not collected for
these drugs. If rebates are received by the NYS DOH for drugs obtained via the 340B program due to
incorrect or missing claim level identifiers, the CE is responsible for reimbursing manufacturers the
340B discounts.
The following table outlines the necessary fields and their values for various claim types to identify 340B-
purchased drug claims.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Ingredient cost submitted exceeds 340B ceiling price
2. Invalid codes submitted for 340B pharmacy drugs
The 340B ceiling price refers to the maximum amount that a manufacturer can charge the covered entity for
the purchase of a 340B drug. A claim submitted to NYS Medicaid that is higher than the 340B ceiling price
will be denied. Claims that are denied must be resubmitted with the correct ingredient cost. Alternatively, the
pharmacy may dispense a non-340B drug and remove the claim level identifiers and submit at the usual and
customary charge.
Claims for 340B drugs submitted to NYS Medicaid via the NCPDP D.0 format must also adhere to the
following:
• must be properly identified as 340B for both fee-for-service (FFS) members and Medicaid Managed
Care (MMC) enrollees; and
• submitted at the 340B acquisition cost by invoice to the provider for FFS members and MMC enrollees,
net any manufacturer discounts and/or other price reductions.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.

Note: Claims will pay at the pharmacy's Usual and Customary (U&C) pricing if lower than drug ingredient cost plus

* Professional Dispensing Fee applies if the drug meets the definition of Covered Outpatient Drug and is not paid at

**When claim is eligible for submission as a 340B purchased drug claim. See subsection 340B Pharmacy Drug Claims

Federal Upper Limit (FUL) is determined by the Secretary of Health and Human Services, a price ceiling used by Centers
for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) to control prices for certain medications paid to pharmacies.

provider_manual.text:
These orders must be
kept on file at the pharmacy.
• If billing for both the drug and administration, these claims must be submitted separately.
• For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field. The
pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using
reimbursement methodology.
• For the administration, the claim must be submitted using procedure code 96372, and the pharmacy’s NPI
