provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, 340B drugs are reimbursed at the 340B Acquisition Cost, and this newer provider manual should guide current billing unless a historical policy question is intended. The newest policy source dated 2024-02-01 is consistent in that claims above the 340B ceiling price are denied and must be resubmitted with the correct ingredient cost, but it is older than the provider manual.
answers.policy_definition: Policy documents indicate that 340B drugs must be identified correctly on claims and that reimbursement is tied to the correct ingredient cost/claim submission rules: "Drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims." [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — Jul 01, 2022] The newest policy source also states that "A claim submitted to NYS Medicaid that is higher than the 340B ceiling price will be denied. Claims that are denied must be resubmitted with the correct ingredient cost." [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — Feb 01, 2024]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual source states that 340B drugs are reimbursed at acquisition cost: "340B**Purchased 340B Acquisition Cost 340B Acquisition Cost $10.18" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — Oct 01, 2025] It also explains that "Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources require correct 340B claim handling to avoid duplicate discounts or improper payment: "ensure rebates are not collected for these drugs" [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:14 — Nov 01, 2021] and "Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources tie payment to the proper 340B cost basis rather than an inflated amount: "A claim submitted to NYS Medicaid that is higher than the 340B ceiling price will be denied" [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — Feb 01, 2024] and "340B Acquisition Cost" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The provider manual gives the clearest current reimbursement rule by stating 340B drugs reimburse at "340B Acquisition Cost" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy excerpts focus on claim identification and denial/resubmission rules rather than a direct reimbursement table [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — Feb 01, 2024].

The policy excerpts emphasize operational claim requirements such as "appending the UD modifier" for 340B drugs on FFS-only claims [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — Jul 01, 2022], while the provider manual excerpt emphasizes reimbursement methodology and duplicate-discount prohibition [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38,32 — Oct 01, 2025].
answers.caveats: The provided policy excerpts do not contain a single explicit reimbursement formula for 340B drugs; they mainly address claim identification, ceiling price denial, and rebate avoidance. The provider manual is newer and should receive more weight for current guidance, but the exact operational details for all claim types are not fully shown in the excerpts.

test_id_51.query_content: How are home infusion drugs covered, and who is responsible for billing
test_id_51.concept: home infusion drugs coverage and billing responsibilities

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
NYS Medicaid FFS policy for drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in
the practitioner’s office are covered for Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved indications, or where
the medically accepted indication is supported in any of the compendia described in Social Security Act
§1927(g)(1)(B)(i) for a medically necessary NYS Medicaid-covered service. In the absence of such a
recognized indication, an approved Institutional Review Board (IRB) protocol would be required with
documentation maintained in the patient's clinical file. Drugs are not covered for investigational or
experimental use.
The NYS Medicaid program has also issued policies and billing guidance for certain drugs/drug classes.
These drugs are eligible for reimbursement when the clinical criteria outlined on the NYS Department of
Health (DOH) New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Practitioner Administered Drug Policies and Billing
Guidance web page, located at https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/practitioner_
administered/ffs_practitioner_administer.htm, and listed web page Medicaid Update articles, are met.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Per New York State Medicaid policy, drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous
methods are covered when provided for Federal Drug Administration (FDA)-approved indications and those
recognized off-label indications listed in the drug compendia. Additional information regarding coverage criteria
and billing guidelines for LUXTURNA™ are outlined in the March 2018 issue of the Medicaid Update.
Questions
• Medicaid fee-for-service (FFS) coverage and policy questions may be directed to the Office of Health
Insurance Programs (OHIP) Division of Program Development and Management at (518) 473-2160.
• Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) general coverage questions may be directed to OHIP Division of Health
Plan Contracting and Oversight at covques@health.ny.gov or (518) 473-1134.
• MMC reimbursement and/or billing requirement questions should be directed to the enrollee's MMC plan.
• FFS claim questions should be directed to the eMedNY call center at (800) 343–9000.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
FFS Billing:
• Payment for the drug’s administration will be made through the outpatient Ambulatory Patient Groups
(APG) payment when administered in a clinic setting or, if administered on an inpatient basis, the All
Patient Refined Diagnosis Related Groups (APR-DRG) payment.
• Facilities administering ZOLGENSMA® will be reimbursed for the cost of the drug using the ordered
ambulatory fee schedule. The ordered ambulatory claim should be submitted on paper (using the
eMedNY 150003 claim form) and should include the facility’s actual acquisition cost by invoice.
Documentation of medical necessity that includes the criteria listed above must accompany the
claim. Ordered ambulatory billing guidelines can be found at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
OrderedAmbulatory/PDFS/OrderedAmbulatory_Billing_Guidelines.pdf
• The following documentation must be included with the claim:
Manufacturer’s invoice showing the acquisition cost of the biologic, including all discounts, rebates
or incentives;
Documentation of the medication administration; and
Documentation of the criteria listed under NYS FFS Coverage Policy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update November 2019 Volume 35 Number 11
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Home Infusion
The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services
associated with home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical
benefit.
The list of Medicaid reimbursable drugs available as a pharmacy benefit may be accessed at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx.
The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) requires coverage of home infusion drugs under Medicare Part
D that are not currently covered under Parts A and B of Medicare. Information on Medicare coverage of home
infusion can be found here: https://www.cms.gov/Medicare/Medicare-Fee-for-Service-Payment/Home-Infusion-
Therapy/Overview.html.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, New York State Medicaid does not provide a bundled payment for home infusion and home infusion drugs/supplies must be billed as either a pharmacy or medical benefit; this newer provider manual should guide current billing questions by default. The newest policy source dated 2022-07-01 is older and addresses coverage of certain administered drugs in practitioner offices, so it is background rather than the most current home-infusion billing guidance.
answers.policy_definition: Policy documents do not provide a direct home-infusion-specific definition in the provided excerpts. The newest policy excerpt says: "NYS Medicaid FFS policy for drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in the practitioner’s office are covered for Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved indications, or where the medically accepted indication is supported in any of the compendia described in Social Security Act §1927(g)(1)(B)(i) for a medically necessary NYS Medicaid-covered service." [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — Jul 01, 2022] Older policy language also states: "Per New York State Medicaid policy, drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods are covered when provided for Federal Drug Administration (FDA)-approved indications and those recognized off-label indications listed in the drug compendia." [New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3:3 — Feb 01, 2019] For billing responsibility, an older policy reminder says: "It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid." [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance states: "The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services associated with home infusion treatments." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025] It further says: "Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025] The manual also recognizes that "certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a health care provider" may be available "by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — Oct 01, 2025] For billing responsibility, the provider manual says: "It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for." [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — Jul 23, 2025]
similarities: "drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods are covered" under Medicaid coverage rules, and the provider manual likewise recognizes "certain drugs, including home infusion drugs" as available through Medicaid benefit pathways [New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3:3 — Feb 01, 2019; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources place billing responsibility on the provider/pharmacy side rather than describing a bundled home-infusion payment: the policy says "It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party" and the provider manual says home infusion drugs and supplies "must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025]
differences: The provider manual is more specific and current for home infusion: it says there is no bundled payment for "drugs, supplies and services associated with home infusion treatments," while the policy excerpts do not provide a home-infusion-specific bundled-payment rule [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025; New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — Jul 01, 2022]

The policy excerpts focus on coverage for drugs administered in a "practitioner’s office" and on FDA-approved or compendia-supported indications, whereas the provider manual explicitly addresses "home infusion" and billing as either a "pharmacy or medical benefit" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — Jul 01, 2022; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025]
answers.caveats: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents beyond general administered-drug coverage and pharmacy billing responsibility. The provider manual is the more recent and more directly applicable source for current home infusion guidance, dated 2025-10-01.

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
New York State Medicaid is always the payor of last resort; every effort must be taken to
obtain correct billing information.
If TPL is applicable, providers must the third-party insurer first, including commercial plans or Medicare,
and bill Medicaid as secondary, if appropriate. Any claim issues must be resolved with the third-party insurer,
including prior authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary
insurer does not cover a specific medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the
third-party insurer will result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
If the member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs. Information on Part B covered drugs can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers, or
• Medicare Part D for other prescription drugs. Information on Part D covered drugs can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
If the member has Medicare Parts A or B (or both) and does not have Medicare Part D, providers will bill:
• Part D covered claims to Medicare Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET).

*Article 31 and 32 clinics operated by FQHCs should follow guidance from NYS OMH and OASAS and should bill according to the
guidance outlined under the Article 31/32 sections of the above grid.
**42 CFR §440.90 defines clinic services as those occurring within the clinic’s four walls. NYS Medicaid does not currently have a
mechanism to allow freestanding clinics / diagnostic and treatment centers to bill when there is no on-site presence at the clinic.
New York State Medicaid Update Special Edition pg. 17

N. Hospital Inpatient Billing for Audio-Visual Telehealth
When a telehealth consult is being provided by a distant-site physician to a NYS Medicaid member who is an
inpatient in the hospital, payment for the telehealth encounter may be billed by the distant-site physician.
Other than physician services, all other practitioner services are included in the All Patient Revised - Diagnosis
Related Group (APR-DRG) payment to the facility.
O. Skilled Nursing Facility Billing for Audio-Visual Telehealth
When the services of the telehealth practitioner are included in the nursing home rate, the telehealth
practitioner must bill the nursing home.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Paper claim submission and multiple drug procedure codes, including wastage, must be submitted for the
same date of service on separate claim forms. Additional information on paper claim submission can be found
in the Billing Instructions for Physician-Administered Drugs (J-codes) Submitted on Paper Claims article,
published in the December 2008 issue of the Medicaid Update, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2008/2008-12.htm#ins.
Practitioner Billing
Reimbursement for drugs, including vaccines and immune globulins, furnished by practitioners to their
patients, is based on the acquisition cost to the practitioner. For all items furnished in this fashion, practitioners
are expected to maintain auditable records of the actual itemized invoice cost of the drug. This includes the
numbers of doses of the drug represented on the invoice. NYS Medicaid FFS does not intend to pay more
than the actual acquisition cost of the drug dosage, as established by invoice, to the practitioner.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Home Infusion
The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services
associated with home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical
benefit.
The list of Medicaid reimbursable drugs available as a pharmacy benefit may be accessed at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx.
The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) requires coverage of home infusion drugs under Medicare Part
D that are not currently covered under Parts A and B of Medicare. Information on Medicare coverage of home
infusion can be found here: https://www.cms.gov/Medicare/Medicare-Fee-for-Service-Payment/Home-Infusion-
Therapy/Overview.html.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

Prescribers should use their professional judgement to determine the best method for members to obtain practitioner

appropriately dispensed.

including those billed as a secondary payment.

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, home infusion drugs are not covered under a bundled payment; they must be billed as either a pharmacy benefit or a medical benefit, and the provider is responsible for determining other insurance coverage. The newest policy source dated 2023-02-01 is older, so the provider manual should receive more weight by default for current guidance on this issue.
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy evidence provided is from 2023-02-01, but it does not specifically define home infusion drug coverage; it only states that “It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid.” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020] It also states that “New York State Medicaid is always the payor of last resort; every effort must be taken to obtain correct billing information” and that providers must bill the third-party insurer first when TPL applies. [New York State Medicaid Update June 2019 Volume 35 Number 7:5 — Jun 01, 2019]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual evidence dated 2025-10-01 states: “The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services associated with home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025] It also states that “NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — Oct 01, 2025] For billing responsibility, the manual says “It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18-19 — Jul 23, 2025]
similarities: Both sources place billing responsibility on the provider/pharmacy rather than on Medicaid alone: “It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020] and “It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18-19 — Jul 23, 2025].

Both sources require attention to other coverage before billing Medicaid: “New York State Medicaid is always the payor of last resort” and providers must bill the third-party insurer first when applicable [New York State Medicaid Update June 2019 Volume 35 Number 7:5 — Jun 01, 2019], while the manual requires the provider to determine whether other insurance covers the service being billed [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18-19 — Jul 23, 2025].
differences: The provider manual gives a direct current coverage rule for home infusion: “does not provide a bundled payment” and “must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy excerpts provided do not specifically define home infusion coverage.

The policy excerpts focus on general billing hierarchy and timely billing—“bill timely and to the appropriate party” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020]—whereas the provider manual is more specific to home infusion drugs and supplies and identifies the benefit pathways [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025].
answers.caveats: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided policy documents to define home infusion drug coverage specifically; the policy excerpts only address general billing/TPL rules. The provider manual is newer overall for this issue (2025-10-01 vs. 2023-02-01) and should guide current interpretation unless the user needs historical policy context.

• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.

submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Prescribers and Enrollment
Providers who prescribe drugs and supplies for NYS Medicaid members and/or MMC enrollees must be
enrolled in NYS Medicaid as either an “Individual Billing” provider or as an “Ordering, Prescribing, Referring,
or Attending” (OPRA) provider for NYS Medicaid to cover the products. NYRx will pay for covered products
and services for NYS Medicaid members when their prescriber is enrolled in NYRx. There are two paths
of enrollment, as a biller or OPRA privder:
Physicians, Nurse Practitioners (NPs), Physician Assistants (PAs), Podiatrists, Dentists,
Optometrists, Audiologists, and Certified Nurse Midwives Providers who wish to:
1. Enroll and receive payment for covered NYS Medicaid services should apply as an “Individual Billing
Medicaid”. Information regarding how to enroll is available on the eMedNY “Provider Enrollment and
Maintenance” web page, located at: https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/; or
2. Enroll as an OPRA provider (non-billing) should alternatively enroll as an OPRA provider on the
eMedNY “Provider Enrollment and Maintenance” web page, located at: https://www.emedny.org/
info/ProviderEnrollment/.

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, New York State Medicaid does not provide a bundled payment for home infusion and home infusion drugs/supplies must be billed as either a pharmacy or medical benefit; this newer provider manual should guide current billing questions by default. The newest policy source dated 2023-05-01 is older and focuses on enrollment/billing status for covered products rather than home infusion specifically, so it is background unless you need the transition-era policy context. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01] [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Four: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 9:5 — 2023-05-01]
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy evidence provided does not specifically define home infusion drug coverage; it states that prescribers of drugs and supplies for NYS Medicaid members and/or MMC enrollees must be enrolled as either an “Individual Billing” provider or an “Ordering, Prescribing, Referring, or Attending” (OPRA) provider for NYS Medicaid to cover the products, and that “NYRx will pay for covered products and services for NYS Medicaid members when their prescriber is enrolled in NYRx.” [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Four: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 9:5 — 2023-05-01] Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents. [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Four: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 9:5 — 2023-05-01]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual states that “The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services associated with home infusion treatments” and that “Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01] It also says NYS Medicaid “recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01]
similarities: Both sources tie coverage/payment to Medicaid enrollment and billing status rather than describing an automatic bundled home-infusion payment: the policy says prescribers must be enrolled for Medicaid to cover products, and the manual says home infusion drugs/supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit. [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Four: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 9:5 — 2023-05-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01]

Both sources place responsibility on providers/pharmacies to bill correctly: the policy says “It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party,” and the manual requires home infusion drugs and supplies to be billed as the correct benefit. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01]
differences: The provider manual is explicit that there is no bundled payment for home infusion and that drugs/supplies must be billed as either a pharmacy or medical benefit, while the policy excerpts provided do not specifically address home infusion coverage. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01] [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Four: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 9:5 — 2023-05-01]

The policy excerpts focus on who must be enrolled for coverage and who must bill timely to the appropriate party, including the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid, whereas the provider manual focuses on benefit classification for home infusion and does not assign that same broad billing-party list. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01]
answers.caveats: The provided policy excerpts do not contain a direct home-infusion coverage rule, so the policy-side definition is incomplete for this specific concept. The provider manual is newer and should receive more weight for current guidance on home infusion billing and coverage. [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Four: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 9:5 — 2023-05-01] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01]

policy.text:
Per New York State Medicaid policy, drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous
methods are covered when provided for Federal Drug Administration (FDA)-approved indications and those
recognized off-label indications listed in the drug compendia. Additional information regarding coverage criteria
and billing guidelines for LUXTURNA™ are outlined in the March 2018 issue of the Medicaid Update.
Questions
• Medicaid fee-for-service (FFS) coverage and policy questions may be directed to the Office of Health
Insurance Programs (OHIP) Division of Program Development and Management at (518) 473-2160.
• Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) general coverage questions may be directed to OHIP Division of Health
Plan Contracting and Oversight at covques@health.ny.gov or (518) 473-1134.
• MMC reimbursement and/or billing requirement questions should be directed to the enrollee's MMC plan.
• FFS claim questions should be directed to the eMedNY call center at (800) 343–9000.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
NYS Medicaid FFS policy for drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in
the practitioner’s office are covered for Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved indications, or where
the medically accepted indication is supported in any of the compendia described in Social Security Act
§1927(g)(1)(B)(i) for a medically necessary NYS Medicaid-covered service. In the absence of such a
recognized indication, an approved Institutional Review Board (IRB) protocol would be required with
documentation maintained in the patient's clinical file. Drugs are not covered for investigational or
experimental use.
The NYS Medicaid program has also issued policies and billing guidance for certain drugs/drug classes.
These drugs are eligible for reimbursement when the clinical criteria outlined on the NYS Department of
Health (DOH) New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Practitioner Administered Drug Policies and Billing
Guidance web page, located at https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/practitioner_
administered/ffs_practitioner_administer.htm, and listed web page Medicaid Update articles, are met.

Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, New York State Medicaid does not provide a bundled payment for home infusion and home infusion drugs/supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit; this newer provider manual should guide current billing questions by default. The newest policy source dated 2022-07-01 is older and addresses coverage of certain administered drugs and billing responsibility, so it is background unless you need the historical policy context.
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy evidence says NYS Medicaid FFS covers drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in the practitioner’s office for FDA-approved indications or medically accepted indications supported in the compendia described in Social Security Act §1927(g)(1)(B)(i) for a medically necessary NYS Medicaid-covered service: “NYS Medicaid FFS policy for drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in the practitioner’s office are covered for Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved indications, or where the medically accepted indication is supported in any of the compendia described in Social Security Act §1927(g)(1)(B)(i) for a medically necessary NYS Medicaid-covered service.” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — Jul 01, 2022] For billing responsibility, the policy states: “It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid.” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual evidence states: “The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services associated with home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025] It also says NYS Medicaid recognizes home infusion drugs as needing administration by a health care provider and available through both benefits: “NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources tie coverage/billing to the pharmacy and medical/practitioner-administered drug framework rather than a single bundled home-infusion payment: “Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025] and “drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in the practitioner’s office are covered” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — Jul 01, 2022].

Both sources place billing responsibility on the provider/pharmacy side rather than on the member: “It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020] and “It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18,19 — Jul 23, 2025].
differences: The provider manual is more explicit and current on home infusion coverage, stating there is no bundled payment and that home infusion drugs/supplies must be billed as pharmacy or medical benefit: “does not provide a bundled payment” and “must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025] The policy excerpts do not specifically define home infusion coverage in those terms.

The policy excerpt specifically assigns billing responsibility to the pharmacy: “It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020], while the provider manual excerpt is broader and says the provider must determine other insurance coverage and whether it covers the service being billed [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18,19 — Jul 23, 2025].
answers.caveats: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for the policy source beyond the quoted practitioner-administered drug coverage and pharmacy billing responsibility; the policy excerpts do not specifically address home infusion drugs as such. The provider manual is the more up-to-date source overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default.

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
NYS Medicaid FFS policy for drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in
the practitioner’s office are covered for Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved indications, or where
the medically accepted indication is supported in any of the compendia described in Social Security Act
§1927(g)(1)(B)(i) for a medically necessary NYS Medicaid-covered service. In the absence of such a
recognized indication, an approved Institutional Review Board (IRB) protocol would be required with
documentation maintained in the patient's clinical file. Drugs are not covered for investigational or
experimental use.
The NYS Medicaid program has also issued policies and billing guidance for certain drugs/drug classes.
These drugs are eligible for reimbursement when the clinical criteria outlined on the NYS Department of
Health (DOH) New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Practitioner Administered Drug Policies and Billing
Guidance web page, located at https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/practitioner_
administered/ffs_practitioner_administer.htm, and listed web page Medicaid Update articles, are met.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Per New York State Medicaid policy, drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous
methods are covered when provided for Federal Drug Administration (FDA)-approved indications and those
recognized off-label indications listed in the drug compendia. Additional information regarding coverage criteria
and billing guidelines for LUXTURNA™ are outlined in the March 2018 issue of the Medicaid Update.
Questions
• Medicaid fee-for-service (FFS) coverage and policy questions may be directed to the Office of Health
Insurance Programs (OHIP) Division of Program Development and Management at (518) 473-2160.
• Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) general coverage questions may be directed to OHIP Division of Health
Plan Contracting and Oversight at covques@health.ny.gov or (518) 473-1134.
• MMC reimbursement and/or billing requirement questions should be directed to the enrollee's MMC plan.
• FFS claim questions should be directed to the eMedNY call center at (800) 343–9000.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Paper claim submission and multiple drug procedure codes, including wastage, must be submitted for the
same date of service on separate claim forms. Additional information on paper claim submission can be found
in the Billing Instructions for Physician-Administered Drugs (J-codes) Submitted on Paper Claims article,
published in the December 2008 issue of the Medicaid Update, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2008/2008-12.htm#ins.
Practitioner Billing
Reimbursement for drugs, including vaccines and immune globulins, furnished by practitioners to their
patients, is based on the acquisition cost to the practitioner. For all items furnished in this fashion, practitioners
are expected to maintain auditable records of the actual itemized invoice cost of the drug. This includes the
numbers of doses of the drug represented on the invoice. NYS Medicaid FFS does not intend to pay more
than the actual acquisition cost of the drug dosage, as established by invoice, to the practitioner.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Home Infusion
The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services
associated with home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical
benefit.
The list of Medicaid reimbursable drugs available as a pharmacy benefit may be accessed at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx.
The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) requires coverage of home infusion drugs under Medicare Part
D that are not currently covered under Parts A and B of Medicare. Information on Medicare coverage of home
infusion can be found here: https://www.cms.gov/Medicare/Medicare-Fee-for-Service-Payment/Home-Infusion-
Therapy/Overview.html.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Members must meet certain conditions to
receive these benefits.

Medicare Part B
Medicare Part B covers doctors’ services, outpatient care and some other medical services that Part A does not cover.
Information regarding Medicare Part B outpatient covered drugs can be found here:
https://www.medicare.gov/coverage/prescription-drugs-outpatient.
Medicare Part B covers certain drugs such as:
• Drugs used with an item of durable medical equipment.
• Some antigens,
• Injectable osteoporosis drugs,
• Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents,
• Blood clotting factors,
• Injectable and infused drugs,
• Oral End-Stage Renal Disease (ESRD) drugs,
• Parenteral and enteral nutrition (intravenous and tube feeding)
• Intravenous Immune Globulin (IVIG) provided in the home,
• Vaccinations,
• Immunosuppressive drugs following a Medicare paid transplant,
• Oral cancer drugs,
• Oral anti-nausea drugs,
• Self-administered drugs in hospital outpatient setting.
For Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims, even for a procedure that would have required Medicaid prior approval, prior
approval is not required if Medicare approved and paid for a service and/or procedure.

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, home infusion drugs and supplies are not covered under a bundled payment and must be billed as either a pharmacy or medical benefit; this provider manual is more up to date overall and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2022-07-01 addresses coverage for drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in a practitioner’s office, but it does not specifically define home infusion billing responsibility.
answers.policy_definition: The newest policy evidence says NYS Medicaid FFS covers “drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in the practitioner’s office” for “FDA approved indications” or “where the medically accepted indication is supported in any of the compendia described in Social Security Act §1927(g)(1)(B)(i) for a medically necessary NYS Medicaid-covered service.” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — Jul 01, 2022] An older policy reminder states, “It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid.” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual states: “The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services associated with home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025] It also says NYS Medicaid recognizes “certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a health care provider” to be available “by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources tie drug coverage to specific benefit/payment pathways rather than a single bundled home-infusion payment: the policy covers certain administered drugs under Medicaid FFS criteria, and the provider manual says home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — Jul 01, 2022] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources place billing responsibility on the pharmacy side for proper claim handling: the policy says “It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party,” and the provider manual requires home infusion drugs and supplies to be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025]
differences: The provider manual is explicit that there is “no bundled payment” for home infusion treatments, while the policy excerpts do not specifically mention bundled payment for home infusion drugs. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025]

The policy excerpt focuses on coverage criteria for drugs administered in a practitioner’s office and does not specifically address home infusion drugs or identify who bills for them, whereas the provider manual specifically addresses home infusion drugs and supplies and says they “must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit.” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — Jul 01, 2022] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025]
answers.caveats: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents beyond the quoted administered-drug coverage criteria and the older general pharmacy billing reminder. The provider manual is the more recent and more directly on-point source for current home infusion billing guidance.

policy.text:
February 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 4

Reminder: Pharmacy Coverage Includes Over the Counter Naloxone
New York State (NYS) Medicaid covers naloxone over the counter (OTC) when prescribed by the practitioner
of a NYS Medicaid member or dispensed to a NYS Medicaid member in compliance with the State-issued
naloxone standing order. Information regarding the naloxone standing order can be found in the NYS
Department of Health (DOH) Non-Patient Specific Prescription for Naloxone with Pharmacy Dispensing
Protocol document, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/diseases/aids/general/opioid_overdose_prevention
/docs/naloxone_standing_order_pharmacies.pdf.
To comply with NYS laws, regulations, and NYS Medicaid policy, a pharmacy must submit a claim and
dispense naloxone OTC pursuant to a fiscal order or via a standing order.

September 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Coverage Expanded to Include Over the Counter
Naloxone
New York State (NYS) Medicaid now covers naloxone over-the-counter (OTC) when either prescribed by the
practitioner of a NYS Medicaid member or dispensed to a NYS Medicaid member in compliance with the
State-issued naloxone standing order. Information regarding the naloxone standing order can be found on
the NYS Department of Health (DOH) “Standing Order for Naloxone in Pharmacies” web page, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/diseases/aids/general/opioid_overdose_prevention/pharmacy_standing_order.ht
m#:~:text=All%20pharmacies%20in%20New%20York,and%20opioid%2Dbased%20pain%20killers. A
pharmacy, in compliance with NYS law and regulations, and NYS Medicaid policy, may submit a claim and
dispense naloxone OTC pursuant to a fiscal order or via a standing order.

policy.text:
April 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 2

Policy and Billing
For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the National Provider Identifier (NPI) of the ordering prescriber
in the Prescriber ID field. The pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug
Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology. For the administration, the claim must be submitted using
procedure code “96372” and the pharmacy NPI in the Prescriber ID field. A pharmacy will be reimbursed
the administration fee of $13.36.
Questions and Additional Information:
• Pharmacists should refer to the NYSED “Pharmacists” web page, located at: https://www.op.
nysed.gov/pharmacist, for additional information.
• NYRx claim questions should be directed to the eMedNY Call Center at (800) 343-9000.
• NYRx Pharmacy coverage and policy questions should be directed to the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy
Policy Unit by telephone at (518) 486-3209 or by email at NYRx@health.ny.gov.

These orders must be
kept on file at the pharmacy.
• If billing for both the drug and administration, these claims must be submitted separately.
• For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field. The

provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy
providers who receive a “no TPL reimbursement” must ensure that the claim was adjudicated within the TPL’s claim
process requirements. This includes submitting the claim under the appropriate coverage benefit, medical or
pharmacy, such as with the Healthcare Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code
(NDC). Additionally, pharmacies not contracted with the member’s TPL Plan may need to attempt an override with the
plan, or enroll with the plan, or advise the member and prescriber of the need to change dispensing to a network
pharmacy.
It is the responsibility of the prescriber to prescribe, according to the plan’s formulary, and to pursue any claim issues
such as, but not limited to:
• Necessary prior authorizations or appeals, or
• Prescription alternatives (for a non-formulary or non-preferred drug) or
• Necessary changes (quantity or day supply, etc.) or
• Selecting an in-network pharmacy that is agreeable to the member, or
• Billing the appropriate benefit (medical vs. pharmacy or HCPCS vs NDC).
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• The NYRx provides reimbursement for prescription drugs included on the NY Medicaid Pharmacy List of
Reimbursable Drugs, which can be found at: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx.
• Prescriptions must be dispensed and billed by a Medicaid enrolled pharmacy, using the member’s individual
Medicaid Client Identification Number (CIN).
Smoking Cessation Policy
• Smoking cessation therapy consists of certain prescription and non-prescription agents.
• Some smoking cessation therapies may be used together. Professional judgment should be exercised when
dispensing multiple smoking cessation products.
• For all smoking cessation products, the member must have a prescription or fiscal order.
• NY Medicaid reimburses for over-the-counter nicotine patches included on the Medicaid Pharmacy List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx.
Emergency Contraception Drug Policy
Both prescription and OTC Emergency Contraception are a covered benefit for all Medicaid members without age
restriction. This includes individuals enrolled in the Family Planning Benefit Program.

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, naloxone reimbursement is handled by billing the drug via NDC using reimbursement methodology, and if both drug and administration are billed, they must be submitted separately; this provider manual is more up to date overall and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2024-04-01 is consistent on the drug/administration billing split, but the provider manual is the controlling current guidance for this issue. [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0059 — Oct 01, 2025]; [mu_no4_apr24_pr.pdf::0005 — Apr 01, 2024]
answers.policy_definition: Policy documents state that NYS Medicaid covers OTC naloxone when it is either prescribed by the practitioner of a NYS Medicaid member or dispensed to a NYS Medicaid member in compliance with the State-issued naloxone standing order. The April 2024 policy also says that for the drug, the claim must be submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field and the pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via NDC using reimbursement methodology; for administration, the claim must use procedure code 96372 and the pharmacy NPI in the Prescriber ID field. [mu_no4_apr24_pr.pdf::0005 — Apr 01, 2024]; [mu_no2_feb24_pr.pdf::0008 — Feb 01, 2024]
answers.provider_manual_definition: The newest provider manual guidance says that if billing for both the drug and administration, the claims must be submitted separately. For the drug, the claim must use the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field, and the pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via NDC using reimbursement methodology; for administration, the claim must use procedure code 96372 and the pharmacy’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field. [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0059 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources say the drug is reimbursed when billed via NDC using reimbursement methodology. [mu_no4_apr24_pr.pdf::0005 — Apr 01, 2024]; [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0059 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources require separate handling of the drug and administration components, with administration billed under procedure code 96372. [mu_no4_apr24_pr.pdf::0005 — Apr 01, 2024]; [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0059 — Oct 01, 2025]
differences: The policy documents also define coverage eligibility for OTC naloxone, stating it is covered when prescribed by a NYS Medicaid member’s practitioner or dispensed under the State-issued naloxone standing order; the provider manual excerpt provided does not include that coverage statement. [mu_no2_feb24_pr.pdf::0008 — Feb 01, 2024]; [mu_no4_apr24_pr.pdf::0005 — Apr 01, 2024]

The provider manual is newer and adds the explicit instruction that when billing both drug and administration, the claims must be submitted separately; the April 2024 policy excerpt states the billing fields and reimbursement method but does not use the same separate-claims wording. [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0059 — Oct 01, 2025]; [mu_no4_apr24_pr.pdf::0005 — Apr 01, 2024]
answers.caveats: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for the provider manual beyond the drug/administration billing and reimbursement instructions quoted above; the excerpts do not show any broader naloxone coverage criteria or any exception beyond the standing-order/prescriber language in policy. [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0059 — Oct 01, 2025]; [mu_no2_feb24_pr.pdf::0008 — Feb 01, 2024]

policy.text:
The update aligns with the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) policy change under the
Transitional Drug Add-on Payment Adjustment (TDAPA), as detailed in the CMS End Stage Renal Disease
Prospective Payment System final rule dated November 12, 2024 where CMS finalized its policy to include
oral-only phosphate binders in the ESRD Prospective Payment System bundled payment and its mechanism
for collecting utilization and price information for these drugs (providers should refer to 89 Federal Register
98822, located at: https://www.federalregister.gov/documents/2024/11/12/2024-25486/medicare-program-
end-stage-renal-disease-prospective-payment-system-payment-for-renal-dialysis). NYS Medicaid will follow
CMS’s approach by incorporating these costs into the APG reimbursement through a standard APG claim
submission.
Please note: For acute kidney injury (AKI) patients, binders remain covered under Medicare Part D. For NYS
Medicaid members with AKI, binders remain covered under the NYS Medicaid pharmacy benefit (NYRx), not
through the APG dialysis clinic payment. This policy ensures alignment between Medicare and NYS Medicaid
coverage for AKI patients.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2025 Volume 41 Number 8

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy
providers who receive a “no TPL reimbursement” must ensure that the claim was adjudicated within the TPL’s claim
process requirements. This includes submitting the claim under the appropriate coverage benefit, medical or
pharmacy, such as with the Healthcare Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code
(NDC). Additionally, pharmacies not contracted with the member’s TPL Plan may need to attempt an override with the
plan, or enroll with the plan, or advise the member and prescriber of the need to change dispensing to a network
pharmacy.
It is the responsibility of the prescriber to prescribe, according to the plan’s formulary, and to pursue any claim issues
such as, but not limited to:
• Necessary prior authorizations or appeals, or
• Prescription alternatives (for a non-formulary or non-preferred drug) or
• Necessary changes (quantity or day supply, etc.) or
• Selecting an in-network pharmacy that is agreeable to the member, or
• Billing the appropriate benefit (medical vs. pharmacy or HCPCS vs NDC).

provider_manual.text:
• Refills of compounds must be specifically requested by the patient or patient´s designee before the item is
prepared and submitted for payment.
• Ingredients in a compound claim will only be reimbursed if available on the NYS Medicaid List of Reimbursable
Drugs.
• Ingredients will only be reimbursed if they are compendia-supported for the intended route of administration
and indication.
• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or
for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYS Medicaid Fee-for-Service Programs.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to safety
reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or compendia-
supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient additives to
a commercially available product.

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2024-02-01 says NYS Medicaid covers over-the-counter naloxone when prescribed or dispensed under the State-issued standing order, while the provider manual excerpt does not provide a naloxone-specific reimbursement rule.
answers.policy_definition: NYS Medicaid “covers naloxone over the counter (OTC) when prescribed by the practitioner of a NYS Medicaid member or dispensed to a NYS Medicaid member in compliance with the State-issued naloxone standing order.” [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4-5 — Feb 01, 2024] Earlier policy also described naloxone as “the life-saving medication that can reverse opioid overdose” in the context of opioid overdose prevention, but did not state reimbursement terms in the provided excerpt. [New York State Medicaid Update December 2022 Volume 38 Number 14:1 — Dec 01, 2022]
answers.provider_manual_definition: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for provider manual. The newest provider manual excerpt only states general billing/reimbursement rules such as: “The pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025] It also says claims for drugs, OTC products, or supplies not furnished must be reversed within 14 days. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:10 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources address Medicaid pharmacy-related handling of medication/OTC items rather than a non-Medicaid program: the policy says Medicaid “covers naloxone over the counter (OTC)” [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4-5 — Feb 01, 2024], and the provider manual discusses reimbursement for “the medication” when billed via NDC and general claims rules for “drugs, over-the-counter products, or medical/surgical supplies.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15,10 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources are consistent that reimbursement/coverage depends on proper claim handling and billing context: the policy ties coverage to being “prescribed” or “dispensed ... in compliance with the State-issued naloxone standing order” [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4-5 — Feb 01, 2024], while the provider manual requires billing “via National Drug Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025]
differences: The policy excerpt gives naloxone-specific coverage criteria, stating Medicaid covers OTC naloxone when “prescribed” or “dispensed ... in compliance with the State-issued naloxone standing order.” [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4-5 — Feb 01, 2024] The provider manual excerpt does not mention naloxone specifically, so it does not define naloxone reimbursement in the provided text. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15,10 — Oct 01, 2025]

The provider manual is newer overall and provides general reimbursement mechanics, including that “The pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025] The policy excerpt is older and focuses on coverage of OTC naloxone rather than the billing method. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4-5 — Feb 01, 2024]
answers.caveats: The provided provider manual excerpts do not contain a naloxone-specific reimbursement rule, so the most direct grounded guidance on naloxone reimbursement comes from the policy update dated 2024-02-01. The provider manual is newer overall (2025-10-01) and should receive more weight for general pharmacy billing methodology, but not for a naloxone-specific rule absent in the excerpt.

policy.text:
February 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 4

Reminder: Pharmacy Coverage Includes Over the Counter Naloxone
New York State (NYS) Medicaid covers naloxone over the counter (OTC) when prescribed by the practitioner
of a NYS Medicaid member or dispensed to a NYS Medicaid member in compliance with the State-issued
naloxone standing order. Information regarding the naloxone standing order can be found in the NYS
Department of Health (DOH) Non-Patient Specific Prescription for Naloxone with Pharmacy Dispensing
Protocol document, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/diseases/aids/general/opioid_overdose_prevention
/docs/naloxone_standing_order_pharmacies.pdf.
To comply with NYS laws, regulations, and NYS Medicaid policy, a pharmacy must submit a claim and
dispense naloxone OTC pursuant to a fiscal order or via a standing order.

policy.text:
September 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Coverage Expanded to Include Over the Counter
Naloxone
New York State (NYS) Medicaid now covers naloxone over-the-counter (OTC) when either prescribed by the
practitioner of a NYS Medicaid member or dispensed to a NYS Medicaid member in compliance with the
State-issued naloxone standing order. Information regarding the naloxone standing order can be found on
the NYS Department of Health (DOH) “Standing Order for Naloxone in Pharmacies” web page, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/diseases/aids/general/opioid_overdose_prevention/pharmacy_standing_order.ht
m#:~:text=All%20pharmacies%20in%20New%20York,and%20opioid%2Dbased%20pain%20killers. A
pharmacy, in compliance with NYS law and regulations, and NYS Medicaid policy, may submit a claim and
dispense naloxone OTC pursuant to a fiscal order or via a standing order.
