policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 2

Policy and Billing
For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the National Provider Identifier (NPI) of the ordering prescriber
in the Prescriber ID field. The pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug
Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology. For the administration, the claim must be submitted using
procedure code “96372” and the pharmacy NPI in the Prescriber ID field. A pharmacy will be reimbursed
the administration fee of $13.36.
Questions and Additional Information:
• Pharmacists should refer to the NYSED “Pharmacists” web page, located at: https://www.op.
nysed.gov/pharmacist, for additional information.
• NYRx claim questions should be directed to the eMedNY Call Center at (800) 343-9000.
• NYRx Pharmacy coverage and policy questions should be directed to the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy
Policy Unit by telephone at (518) 486-3209 or by email at NYRx@health.ny.gov.

provider_manual.text:
Note: Reimbursable drugs are listed alphabetically in sections by Rx Type.
Drug Coverage Limitations
Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs
(unless provided by a facility which includes the cost of drugs in their all-inclusive rate). The following are examples of
drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by Medicaid in accordance with Policy and/or state or federal Legislation:
• Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs for the treatment of sexual dysfunction pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), and Social Services Law §365-a(4)(f).
• Drugs without a federal rebate agreement pursuant to SSA §1927(a).
• Drugs indicated for cosmetic use or hair growth pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs that are not approved as safe and effective by the FDA pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(4)(C).

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, naloxone reimbursement is handled by billing the drug via NDC using the ordering prescriber’s NPI, and the pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication; this provider manual guidance is more up to date overall and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2024-04-01 is consistent on reimbursement mechanics for the drug and adds that administration is billed separately with procedure code 96372 and the pharmacy NPI.
answers.policy_definition: Policy documents state that NYS Medicaid covers naloxone OTC when it is “prescribed by the practitioner of a NYS Medicaid member or dispensed to a NYS Medicaid member in compliance with the State-issued naloxone standing order” [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4-5 — Feb 01, 2024]; the April 2024 policy adds that “For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the National Provider Identifier (NPI) of the ordering prescriber in the Prescriber ID field. The pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology. For the administration, the claim must be submitted using procedure code ‘96372’ and the pharmacy NPI in the Prescriber ID field” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:2-3 — Apr 01, 2024].
answers.provider_manual_definition: The provider manual states that “For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field. The pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025]; it also says that if billing both drug and administration, “these claims must be submitted separately” and “For the administration, the claim must be submitted using procedure code 96372, and the pharmacy’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025].
similarities: Both sources say the drug is reimbursed when billed via NDC using reimbursement methodology: “The pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:2-3 — Apr 01, 2024] and “The pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025].

Both sources require the ordering prescriber’s NPI for the drug claim: “the claim must be submitted using the National Provider Identifier (NPI) of the ordering prescriber in the Prescriber ID field” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:2-3 — Apr 01, 2024] and “the claim must be submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025].
differences: The provider manual is newer and more current overall: it is dated “Oct 01, 2025” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025], while the newest policy chunk is dated “Apr 01, 2024” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:2-3 — Apr 01, 2024].

The provider manual explicitly says that if billing both drug and administration, “these claims must be submitted separately” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy chunk states the separate billing mechanics for administration but does not use the same explicit “submitted separately” wording [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:2-3 — Apr 01, 2024].
answers.caveats: The February 2024 policy chunk confirms OTC naloxone coverage when prescribed or dispensed under the standing order, but it does not add reimbursement mechanics beyond coverage scope [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4-5 — Feb 01, 2024].

NYS Medicaid Policy.

pharmacy formulary.

billed directly to NYRx by a pharmacy. Nothing in this policy is meant to suggest that practitioner-administered drugs
must be dispensed as a Pharmacy benefit. The policy regarding practitioner-administered drug billing is addressed in
the Physician’s Manual found here: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Physician/index.aspx.

site of administration. This is considered “white bagging” and is acceptable under the following guidelines:
• Drugs should only be dispensed by the pharmacy directly to the patient when they are to be self-
administered. The policy surrounding self-administered drug delivery is found in section titled Delivery
above.

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
February 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 4

Reminder: Pharmacy Coverage Includes Over the Counter Naloxone
New York State (NYS) Medicaid covers naloxone over the counter (OTC) when prescribed by the practitioner
of a NYS Medicaid member or dispensed to a NYS Medicaid member in compliance with the State-issued
naloxone standing order. Information regarding the naloxone standing order can be found in the NYS
Department of Health (DOH) Non-Patient Specific Prescription for Naloxone with Pharmacy Dispensing
Protocol document, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/diseases/aids/general/opioid_overdose_prevention
/docs/naloxone_standing_order_pharmacies.pdf.
To comply with NYS laws, regulations, and NYS Medicaid policy, a pharmacy must submit a claim and
dispense naloxone OTC pursuant to a fiscal order or via a standing order.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
MMC Plans will
continue to be responsible for providing reimbursement for other medical benefits, such as DSMT.
MMC Reimbursement
The “FFS Billing Guidance for Pharmacies” section referenced above is specific to NYS Medicaid FFS. For
NYS Medicaid members enrolled in an MMC Plan, providers should check with the MMC Plan of the enrollee
for implementation details, reimbursement fees, and billing instructions. Additionally, MMC Plans will
reimburse providers no less than the NYS Medicaid FFS rate for DSMT services and must cover the cost of
DSMT services when rendered by qualified providers who do not participate in the network of the MMC Plan.
March 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 8

Questions and Additional Information:
• NYRx FFS claim questions should be directed to the eMedNY Call Center at (800) 343-9000.
• NYRx coverage and policy questions should be directed to the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy Policy Unit
by telephone at (518) 486-3209 or by email at NYRx@health.ny.gov.
• MMC general coverage questions should be directed to the OHIP Division of Health Plan Contracting
and Oversight (DHPCO) by email at covques@health.ny.gov or by telephone at (518) 473-1134.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update March 2023 Volume 39 Number 6
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy
providers who receive a “no TPL reimbursement” must ensure that the claim was adjudicated within the TPL’s claim
process requirements. This includes submitting the claim under the appropriate coverage benefit, medical or
pharmacy, such as with the Healthcare Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code
(NDC). Additionally, pharmacies not contracted with the member’s TPL Plan may need to attempt an override with the
plan, or enroll with the plan, or advise the member and prescriber of the need to change dispensing to a network
pharmacy.
It is the responsibility of the prescriber to prescribe, according to the plan’s formulary, and to pursue any claim issues
such as, but not limited to:
• Necessary prior authorizations or appeals, or
• Prescription alternatives (for a non-formulary or non-preferred drug) or
• Necessary changes (quantity or day supply, etc.) or
• Selecting an in-network pharmacy that is agreeable to the member, or
• Billing the appropriate benefit (medical vs. pharmacy or HCPCS vs NDC).
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• The NYRx provides reimbursement for prescription drugs included on the NY Medicaid Pharmacy List of
Reimbursable Drugs, which can be found at: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx.
• Prescriptions must be dispensed and billed by a Medicaid enrolled pharmacy, using the member’s individual
Medicaid Client Identification Number (CIN).
Smoking Cessation Policy
• Smoking cessation therapy consists of certain prescription and non-prescription agents.
• Some smoking cessation therapies may be used together. Professional judgment should be exercised when
dispensing multiple smoking cessation products.
• For all smoking cessation products, the member must have a prescription or fiscal order.
• NY Medicaid reimburses for over-the-counter nicotine patches included on the Medicaid Pharmacy List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx.
Emergency Contraception Drug Policy
Both prescription and OTC Emergency Contraception are a covered benefit for all Medicaid members without age
restriction. This includes individuals enrolled in the Family Planning Benefit Program.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Additional information regarding the Medicare Part D Prescription Drug Program and residential health care
facilities may be accessed at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/.
See Section 4.0 for Long Term Care Pharmacy Short Cycle Billing information.
Items Provided by Child (Foster) Care Agencies
Please see this link for items provided by Childcare Agencies:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/carveout.htm.
OMH Residential Treatment Facility Prescription Drug Carve-Out
Reimbursement of prescription drugs for children and youth between the ages of five and twenty-one who are
residents of the Office of Mental Health (OMH) Residential Treatment Facilities (RTF) is a NYRx benefit and billed
directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy.
Physician administered drugs, OTC drugs, medical supplies, immunization services (vaccines and their administration),
nutritional supplies, sick room supplies, adult diapers, and durable medical equipment (DME) are not carved out of the
RTF rate and remain the responsibility of the facility.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Multiple Source Drugs
Reimbursement is only available for those multiple source drugs contained on the List of Medicaid Reimbursable
Drugs.
For certain brand name prescriptions not in the Brand Less Than Generic Program, to be eligible for reimbursement,
prescribers must certify that the brand name drug is required by:
• writing directly on the face of the prescription “Brand Necessary” or “Brand Medically Necessary” in their own
handwriting in addition to the “DAW”, or
• in the case of electronic prescriptions, the prescriber must insert an electronic direction to clarify “Brand
Medically Necessary” or “Brand Necessary”.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2024-02-01 says NYS Medicaid covers OTC naloxone when prescribed or dispensed under the State-issued standing order, while the provider manual excerpt available here does not specifically define naloxone reimbursement.
answers.policy_definition: NYS Medicaid “covers naloxone over the counter (OTC) when prescribed by the practitioner of a NYS Medicaid member or dispensed to a NYS Medicaid member in compliance with the State-issued naloxone standing order.” [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4-5 — Feb 01, 2024] Earlier policy context also notes naloxone as “the life-saving medication that can reverse opioid overdose,” but does not address reimbursement. [New York State Medicaid Update December 2022 Volume 38 Number 14:1 — Dec 01, 2022]
answers.provider_manual_definition: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for provider manual. The newest provider manual excerpt provided says only that “The NYRx provides reimbursement for prescription drugs included on the NY Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs,” and that “Prescriptions must be dispensed and billed by a Medicaid enrolled pharmacy,” but it does not specifically mention naloxone reimbursement. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:31 — Oct 01, 2025]
similarities: Both sources indicate Medicaid reimbursement/coverage is tied to pharmacy dispensing and Medicaid enrollment or authorization conditions, rather than an unrestricted payment rule: policy says naloxone is covered when “prescribed” or “dispensed ... in compliance with the State-issued naloxone standing order,” and the manual says prescriptions “must be dispensed and billed by a Medicaid enrolled pharmacy.” [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4-5 — Feb 01, 2024; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:31 — Oct 01, 2025]

Both sources frame naloxone as a pharmacy benefit context item rather than a medical-service definition: policy says “Pharmacy Coverage Includes Over the Counter Naloxone,” and the manual states “The NYRx provides reimbursement for prescription drugs included on the NY Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs.” [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4-5 — Feb 01, 2024; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:31 — Oct 01, 2025]
differences: The policy source specifically addresses naloxone and states the reimbursement/coverage rule for OTC naloxone: “covers naloxone over the counter (OTC) when prescribed ... or dispensed ... in compliance with the State-issued naloxone standing order.” [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4-5 — Feb 01, 2024] The provider manual excerpt does not specifically mention naloxone and instead gives only a general reimbursement rule for drugs on the reimbursable list. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:31 — Oct 01, 2025]

The provider manual is newer and more general: “The NYRx provides reimbursement for prescription drugs included on the NY Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs,” but the provided excerpt does not confirm whether naloxone is included on that list or describe OTC standing-order reimbursement. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:31 — Oct 01, 2025] The policy source is older but more specific to naloxone and OTC standing-order dispensing. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4-5 — Feb 01, 2024]
answers.caveats: The provider manual excerpts provided do not contain a naloxone-specific reimbursement rule, so the manual cannot be used here to confirm or narrow naloxone coverage beyond the general NYRx reimbursement language. The newest provider manual source is more recent overall and should guide current interpretation where it speaks directly, but for naloxone-specific OTC reimbursement the grounded policy excerpt is the only direct evidence provided. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:31 — Oct 01, 2025; New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4-5 — Feb 01, 2024]
